PREFACE. 

The  following  pages  are  the  result  of  many  years  of  experiment,  investigation 
and  study.  We  have  aimed  to  prepare  a  work  for  the  use  of  housekeepers  on  a 
more  thorough  and  comprehensive  plan  than  has  been  heretofore  attempted. 
As  the  book  is  intended  for  the  use  of  the  average  housekeeper,  there  is  nothing  in 
it  which  cannot  be  easily  understood  by  any  person  of  ordinary  intelligence,  for  we 
have  taken  much  pains  to  present  the  results  of  modern  scientific  investigations 
in  a  clear  and  simple  way,  avoiding,  as  far  as  possible,  the  use  of  technical 
terms.  Most  of  the  household  books  in  current  use  give  the  processes  for  doing 
things  merely,  with  no  attempt  to  explain  the  reasons  for  the  processes  or  the 
principles  which  underlie  them.  We  also  give  as  clearly  as  possible 
the  most  detailed  directions  in  all  our  recipes,  but  we  do  not  stop  there,  as  we 
think  any  one  can  work  more  intelligently  by  understanding  not  only  how  to  do 
a  certain  thing,  but  also  why  it  is  done  one  way  rather  than  another,  and  the 
principles  which  underlie  the  process.  For  this  reason  throughout  the  work  we  sys- 
tematically explain  principles  as  well  as  processes.  We  have  long  felt  that  a  sad 
defect  in  most  cook  books  is  their  utter  failure  to  explain  those  simple, 
fundamental  principles  which  every  cook  should,  if  possible,  understand.  If  these 
principles  are  once  thoroughly  understood  the  mystery  and  uncertainty  of  kitchen 
operations  will  vanish,  and  cooking  will  simply  be  adopting  certain  clearly  under- 
stood methods  to  produce  certain  definite  results,  and  success  Will  always  follow. 

Within  the  last  few  years  much  excellent  work  has  been  done  by  the  scientists 
who  have  been  investigating  the  chemistry  of  cooking  and  the  chemical  com- 
position and  nutritive  value  of  the  different  foods.  We  have  endeavored  to 
clearly  present  to  our  readers  the  most  important  results  of  their  labors,  for  we 
believe  the  time  has  come  for  housekeepers  to  understand  both  the  composition 
of  foods  and  their  nutritive  value,  as  well  as  the  chemical  changes  produced  in 
them  by  the  various  operations  of  cooking. 

For  years  we  have  been  gathering  material  for  this  book,  resulting  in  the 
accumulation  of  a  great  mass  of  recipes.  These  have  been  culled  and  tested,  and 
in  making  selections  our  rule  has  been  to  choose  those  which  were  most  sim- 
ple and  economical,  because  the  book  is  primarily  designed  for  the  use  of  the 
masses,  whose  means  are  always  limited,  and  we  aim  to  meet  their  every  day  wants, 
although  we  present  also  an  ample  number  of  more  elaborate  recipes  suitable  for 
special  occasions.  Our  endeavor  has  been  to  make  the  collection  as  complete  and 
comprehensive  as  possible,  and  to  give  new,  choice  and  well-tested  recipes  in 
every  department  of  household  cookery. 

In  preparing  this  work  we  have  constantly  had  four  main  objects  in  view. 
(1)  To  secure  the  fullest,  latest,  and  most  reliable  information  possible  on  the 
subjects  treated.  (2)  To  explain  processes  and  methods  for  saving  time 
and  labor,  for  the  average   housewife  is  sadly  overworked  and  her  time  and 


4  PREFACE. 

strength  are  of  the  utmost  value.  (3)  To  select  the  best  and  most  economical 
recipes;  and  (4)  to  point  out  ways  to  prevent  waste- 
In  the  general  department  of  household  topics  we  present  a  more  complete 
and  systematic  treatment  of  the  various  subjects  connected  with  household 
management  than  can  be  found  elsewhere,  and  the  information  therein 
contained  will  certainly  be  of  great  practical  value  to  housekeepers.  The 
effort  of  the  editor  has  been  to  produce  a  thoroughly  reliable  and  comprehensive 
guide  to  housekeeping  in  all  its  branches,  which  no  housewife  can  afford  to  do 
without.  The  book  has  not  been  written  by  any  one  individual,  but  many  pens 
have  been  employed  more  or  less  in  its  preparation. 

The  book  will  certainly  shed  much  needed  light  on  the  problems  which  confront 
and  often  harass  housekeepers,  explain  the  fundamental  principles  which  under- 
lie their  work,  and  present  a  mass  of  recipes  which  will  materially  aid  them  in 
their  labors,  and  we  are  convinced  that  there  is  no  subject  more  intimately 
connected  with  the  welfare  of  the  people  than  that  of  housekeeping  in  its  various 
branches.  The  Editor. 


SABt-E  SHOWING  THE  COMPARATIVE  AMOUNT  OF  NUTRIENTS  iN  DIFFER- 

ENX  FOODS. 


The  following  table  will  show  the  comparative  amount  of  nutrients,  after  evaporating 
the  water,  remaining  in  the  edible  portions  of  the  more  important  articles  of  food,  i.  e„  after 
removing  the  refuse,  like  bones,  shell,  skin,  etc.  The  nutritive  value  of  the  different  foods, 
and  the  elements  of  which  they  are  composed,  are  explained  more  fully  elsewhere,  but 
this  table  will  bring  clearly  before  the  eye,  at  a  glance,  the  striking  difference  in  the  nutri- 
tive value  of  different  foods.  In  vegetables,  one  of  their  great  benefits  to  the  system  is  de- 
rived from  the  potash  and  other  salts  which  they  contain,  as  we  elsewhere  explain,  and 
these  make  little  showing  in  a  table  like  this,  but  the  table  will  serve  to  give  some  idea  of 
the  value  as  food  of  different  articles.  There  is  a  lamentable  ignorance  at  the  present 
time  regarding  the  true  value  to  the  system  of  different  articles  of  food,  causing  much  loss, 
which  is  especially  burdensome  to  poor  people.

TABLES  Of  WEIGHTS  AND  MEASURES. 

Notice.— The  standard  of  measure  which  we  have  adopted  in  this  hook  is  the  ordinary 
coffee  cup,  which  holds  Vz  pint.  Where  not  otherwise  specified,  we  always  mean  that  by  a 
cup.  Of  course  the  size  of  the  cups  and  spoons  in  actual  use  vary  somewhat,  and  extraor- 
dinary dampness  or  dryness  of  the  materials  will  affect  their  weight,  but  these  tables  are 
as  accurate  as  we  can  make  them.  Ln  making  up  any  recipe,  using  the  same  measure 
throughout  will  keep  the  proportions  correct. 

Before  measuring  sugar,  meal,  flour,  soda,  salt  and  spices,  they  should  be  sifted.  Ma- 
terials like  baking  powder  or  mustard,  which  have  been  packed,  should  be  stirred  and 
crushed  if  you  do  not  sift  them. 

To  get  Vz  a  spoonful,  fill  the  spoon,  divide  it  through  the  center  lengthwise,  and  use  one 
half.  To  get  an  even  spoonful  of  any  dry  material,  fill  the  spoon  and  level  it  off  with  a 
knife.  A  heaping  spoonful  is  all  it  will  hold.  To  get  a  spoonful,  fill  the  spoon  and  shake  it 
a  little  until  it  is  slightly  rounded  on  top. 

As  a  cup  is  smaller  at  the  bottom  than  at  the  top,  54  a  cup  does  not  mean  to  fill  it  54  the 
way  up,  but  a  little  more  than  that.  The  tin  measuring  cups  holding  54  pint,  and  divided 
into  fourths  and  thirds,  are  the  best  to  use;  they  can  be  purchased  at  tin  or  hardware 
stores. 

Throughout  the  work,  to  save  space  we  drop  off  the  "  ful "  in  such  words  as  "cupful," 
"tablespoonful,"  etc.,  and  write  simply  "cup,"  "tablespoon,"  etc.  We  always  mean  full 
measure  unless  we  add  the  word  "heaping,"  or  "scant." 


COMMON  ARTICLES  OF  FOOD. 

Barley:— 1  cup  weighs  4  ounces;  1  table- 
spoon, heaped,  Vz  ounce. 

Bread  crumbs,  grated:— 1  cup  weighs  2 
ounces. 

Butter: — 1  even  cup,  hard  or  melted, 
weighs  7  ounces;  a  rounded  tablespoon 
weighs  1  ounce;  the  "size  of  an  egg" 
weighs  2  ounces. 

Coffee,  ground:— 4  cups  weigh  1  pound;  1 
cup  weighs  4  ounces;  2  rounded  table- 
spoons weigh  1  ounce. 

Cornmeal: — 1  even  cup  weighs  454  ounces; 
a  heaping  tablespoon  Vz  ounce. 

Corn-starch: — 1  cup  weighs  8  ounces;  a 
heaping  tablespoon  54  ounce. 

Currants:— 1  cup,  cleaned  and  dried, 
weighs  6  ounces. 

Eggs: — 10  eggs,  average  size  (or  9  if  very 
large)  weigh  1  pound;  1  white  of  egg 
weighs  about  1  ounce,  and  1  yolk  about 
1  ounce. 

Flour  (wheat,  well  sifted  always):— 1 
quart,  heaped,  weighs  1  pound;  1  cup 
level,  weighs  4  ounces,  or  6  ou  nces  when 
heaped;  1  tablespoon,  heaped,  holds  1 
ounce;  1  teaspoon,  heaped,  Vz  ounce. 

Lard:— Same  as  butter. 

Meat: — 1  solid  cup  of  chopped  meat  weighs 
8  ounces;  a  pint=l  pound. 

Nutmegs:— 5  medium  sized  nutmegs  weigh 
1  ounce. 

Raisins: — 1  cup  weighs  Vz  pound,  or  8 
ounces. 

Rice: — 1  cup  weighs  8  ounces;  1  table- 
spoon, heaped,  Vz  ounce. 

Salt:— A  "pinch"  =1  saltspoon. 

Sago:— 1  cup  weighs  8  ounces;  1  table- 
spoon, heaped,  Vz  ounce. 

Suet:— Same  as  butter. 

Sugar,  granulated,  or  dry  brown:— 1  cup, 
heaped,  weighs  8  ounces;  1  tablespoon, 
heaped,  Vz  ounce. 

Tea:— 1  cup,  heaped,  weighs  2  ounces;  1 
tablespoon,  heaped,  weighs  %  ounce;  1 
teaspoon,  heaped,  V%  ounce.

MEASURING  CUPS,  ETC. 

Cup:—  1  cup  holds  Mj  pint  or  8  fluid  ounces, 
or  16 tablespoons  or  32  teaspoons;  1  cup 
holds  7  ounces  of  lard  or  butter,  4  oz.  of 
flour  (when  heaped  6  oz.  flour  or  4  of 
coffee) ;  2  ounces  of  tea  and  8  ounces  of 
raisins. 

Basting-spoon:— 4  basting-spoons  make  1 
cupful ;  1  basting-spoon  equals  4  table- 
spoons or  8  teaspoons. 

Tablespoon:— 1  tablespoon  holds  Vz  ounce 
of  water;  8  tablespoons  liquid  make  1 
gill;  16  tablespoons  liquid  make  1  cup- 
ful; 1  tablespoon,  heaped,  holds  Vt 
ounce  of  barley,  corn-meal,  corn- 
starch, ground  spice,  flour,  sago,  sugar; 
and  Vz  ounce  of  ground  coffee,  and  ^ 
ounce  of  tea. 

Teaspoon:— 1  teaspoon  holds  Vz  table- 
spoon; 16  teaspoons  liquid  make  1  gill, 
and  32  teaspoons  liquid  make  1  cup.  1 
teaspoon  holds  H  ounce  of  ground  cof- 
fee, and  \%  ounce  of  tea,  and  Vz  of  the 
other  articles  given  above  for  a  table- 
spoon. 

A  tumbler,  common  size,  holds  V4  pint,  or 
8  fluid  ounces. 

A  wine  glass,  common  size,  holds  Vz  gill, 
or  2  fluid  ounces. 


AVOIRDUPOIS  WEIGHT. 

16  drams  (dr.)  make  1  ounce  (oz). 

16  ounces  make  1  pound  (ft). 

25  pounds  make  1  quarter  (qr.). 

4  quarters  make  1  hundred  weight  (cwt.). 

20  hundred  weight  make  1  ton  (T.). 


LIQUID  OR  WINE  MEASURE. 

4  gills  make  1  pint  (pt.). 
2  pints  make  1  quart  (qt.). 
4  quarts  make  1  gallon  (gal.). 
3154  gallons  make  1  barrel  (bbl.). 
63  gallons  make  1  hogshead  (hhd.). 
2  hogsheads  make  1  pipe  or  butt  (pi.). 


DRY  MEASURE. 

2  pints  make  1  quart. 
8  quarts  make  1  peck. 
4  pecks  make  1  bushel. 
8  bushels  make  1  quarter. 


W 


CLOTH  MEASURE. 

2U  inches  make  1  nail. 

4  nails  make  M  yard. 

4  quarters  make  1  yard. 

3  quarters  make  1  Ell  Flemish. 

5 quarters  make  1  Ell  English. 

Cloth  Measure  is  little  used  at  present.  In 
measuring  goods  sold  by  the  yard,  it  is  di- 
vided into  halves,  quarters,  eighths  and  six- 
teenths. 


LINEAR  .MEASURE. 

12  inches=l  foot. 
3feet=l  yard. 
6V2yards=l  rod  or  pole. 
40rods=l  furlong. 
8furlongs=l  mile. 
3miles=l  league. 

3  barley-corns=l  inch. 

4  inches=l  hand. 
9  inches=l span. 

21.888  inches=l  sacred  cubit. 

18  inches=l  English  cubit. 

6  feet=l  fathom. 

120  fathoms=l  cable's  length. 

6086.7  feet=l  knot. 

3  feet=l  pace. 

1.152M  common  miles=l  geographical  mile. 

3  geographical  miles=l  league. 

60  geographical  miles=l  degree. 

360  degrees^ the  circumference  of  the  globe. 

THE  METRIC  SYSTEM. 

Measures  of  Weight.

1  gramme=15.432  grains. 
1  decagramme=0.022046  pounds. 
1  hectogramme=0.22046  pounds. 
1  kilogramme=2.2046  pounds. 
1  myriagramme=22.046  pounds.  , 

Measures  of  Capacity. 

1  millilitre=0.0610  cubic  in.=338  fluid  oz. 

1  litre= 1.0567  quarts. 

1  dekalitre=2.6417  gallons. 

1  kektolitre=26.4175  gallons=2.84  bushels. 

1  kilolitre=264.175  gallons=28.37  bushels. 

Measures  of  Length.

1  centimetre=0.3937  inch.

MEDICINAL  TERMS  AND    APOTHECA- 
RIES WEIGHTS  AND  MEASURES. 

Measures. 
60  minims  (M)  or  drops=l  fluidrachm  (/  3.) 
8  fluidrachms=l  fluid  ounce  (/  ?.) 
16  fluid  ounces=l  pint  (O.) 
8  pints=l  gallon  (Cong.) 

Weights. 
20  grains  (gr.  xx)=l  scruple  (3  or  sc.) 
3  scruples  (3  ii.i)=l  drachm  (3  or  dr.) 
8  drachms  (3  viij)=l  ounce  (1  or  oz.) 
12  ounces  (1  xij)=l  pound  (tt>.) 

Physicians  in  writing  prescriptions  use 
the  Roman  numerals  instead  of  figures,  but 
the  small  letters  only,  which  they  precede 
by  the  symbols.  They  write  j  for  i  when 
it  terminates  a  number.  Thus?vij  means 
7  ounces,  3xiv  means  14  scruples,  etc.  Q 
is  an  abreviation  for  recipe,  or  take;  P  for 
particula,  or  little  part;  q.  s.  quantity  suf- 
ficient; P.  asq.  for  equal  parts;  q.  p.  as 


much  as  you  please;  gr.  for  grain;  ss.  for 
semi;&,  aa.  for  equal  quantities,  ij.  for  2; 
gttisadrop;  Cong,  is  an  abreviation  of 
congius,  the  latin  for  gallon;  O.  for  octarius, 
the  latin  for  one-eighth.  The  minim  is 
equal  to  a  drop  of  water;  a  pint  of  water 
weighs  a  pound. 


MISCELLANEOUS    TABLES   COMPRIS- 
ING VARIOUS  THINGS  WORTH 
KNOWING. 

Counting.— 12  units  make  a  dozen;  12 
dozen  make  a  gross;  12  gross  make  a  great 
gross.    20  units  make  a  score. 

Paper.— 24  sheets  make  1  quire;  20 quires 
make  1  ream;  2  reams  make  1  bundle. 

Books.— A  sheet  of  paper  folded  in  two 
leaves  makes  a  folio  or  4  pages;  in  four 
leaves  a  quarto,  4 to,  or  8  pages;  in  8  leaves 
an  octavo,  8  vo,  or  16  pages;  in  12  leaves,  a 
duodecimo,  12  mo,  or  24  pages;  in  16  leaves, 
a  16  mo,  or  32  pages;  in  18  leaves  an  18  mo, 
or  36  pages.  These  terms  are  applied 
without  regard  to  the  size  of  the  sheet 
folded,  which  may  vary  widely.

Beans,  a  bushel=60  lbs. 

Beef,  a  barrel=200  fl>s. 

Buckwheat,  a  bushel=40to52  fi> 

Butter,  a  firkin=56  lbs. 

Butter,  a  tub =84  lbs. 

Bran,  a  bushel=20  lbs. 

Clover  seed,  a  bushel=60  lbs. 

Corn,  a  bushel,  shelled=56  B>s  (in  most 
states). 

Corn,  a  bushel,  on  the  cob=70  or  68  B>s.*

INTRODUCTION. 


"We  grow  like  what  we  eat.    Bad  food  depresses.    Good  food  exhalts  us  like  an  inspira- 
tion." 

Primitive  man  did  not  cook.  Although  the  origin  of  man  is  veiled  in  obscur- 
ity, and  the  habits  and  customs  of  the  earliest  races  are  somewhat  conjectural,  all 
the  indications  are  that  for  an  indefinite  period  primitive  man  must  have  lived 
largely  on  raw  nuts  and  fruits.  Then,  in  his  slow,  upward  advance,  his  earliest 
weapons  were  clubs  or  rude  stone  implements,  with  which  he  killed  animals, 
whose  flesh  he  tore  apart  and  devoured  raw.  When  by  some  happy  accident 
primitive  man  discovered  fire,  it  marked  the  dawn  of  a  new  era  for  him.  It  was 
undoubtedly  one  of  the  most  important  and  beneficial  discoveries  ever  made  by 
the  race.  Many  savage  races  have  been  discovered  even  in  recent  times  who 
were  unacquainted  with  fire,  and  who,  on  seeing  it,  regarded  it  with  a  super- 
stitious feeling  of  awe,  and  attributed  to  it  some  form  of  life.  Fire  having  been 
discovered,  men  must  have  tried  to  dry  and  roast  their  food.  Probably  burying 
seeds  and  meat  in  hot  ashes  was  the  earliest  form  of  cooking.  This  would  im- 
prove its  flavor  and  make  it  more  easily  eaten,  but  it  would  befoul  the  food  more 
or  less.  Then  man  hit  on  the  plan  of  suspending  his  meat  on  spits  above  the  fire, 
and  so  grilling- arose.  It  is  not  probable  that  even  in  the  days  of  Homer  the  art 
of  cookery  was  much  advanced  beyond  this  stage,  for  nowhere  in  his  poems  does 
he  speak  of  boiled  meat,  although  in  the  "Iliad"  we  read  that  the 
great  Achilles  and  his  friend  Patroclus  regaled  the  three  Grecian  leaders  with 
bread,  wine  and  broiled  meat.  The  Jews,  however,  early  learned  the  art  of  boil- 
ing, and  they  had  kettles,  in  one  of  which  the  mess  of  pottage  for  which  Esau 
sold  his  birthright  to  Jacob,  must  have  been  boiled.  As  civilization  advanced, 
the  art  of  cookery  slowly  developed,  until,  in  the  days  of  Roman  luxury  (about  the 
time  of  the  birth  of  Christ)  it  attained  great  prominence,  and  Athenseus  has  pre- 
served the  names  of  the  authors  of  many  cook  books,  Archestratus  being  probably 
the  first,  as  he  was  certainly  the  most  famous.  Men  of  note  from  the  island  of 
Sicily  became  the  most  celebrated  cooks,  and  commanded  great  prices  for  their 
services. 

The  cereals  (named  from  Ceres,  the  goddess  of  agriculture)  were  early  known 
to  the  race.  The  Assyrians,  Egyptians  and  Greeks  used  to  make  bread  in  which 
oil,  with  aniseseed  and  other  spices,  was  an  element,  but  it  was  unleavened.  Tra- 
dition says  that  somewhere  about  the  30th  Olympiad  (about  724  B.  C.)  the  slave 
of  an  archon  at  Athens  left  some  wheaten  dough  in  an  earthen  pot  and  forgot  it. 
Some  days  afterwards  he  found  it,  but  it  was  turning  sour  and  he  was  about  to 
throw  it  away,  when  his  master  appeared,  and  so  he  mixed  it  with  some  fresh 
dough  on  which  he  was  working.  The  bread  produced  by  this  dough  in  which 
alcholic  fermentation  had  begun  was  found  delicious  by  the  archon  and  his 
friends,  and- when  the  slave  was  summoned,  he  told  his  secret.  Thus  leavened 
bread  was  discovered  by  accident.  The  news  spread  rapidly,  a  great  demand  for 
the  new  bread  sprang  up,  and  certain  persons  set  up  as  bread  makers  (the  first 
bakers)  and  "Athenian  bread"  soon  became  famous  throughout  Greece.  Down 
as  late  as  the  Roman  Emperors,  however,  no  bolted  flour  was  known,  and  to  this 
day,  in  many  parts  of  Germany  the  entire  meal  is  used. 

(9) 


10  INTRODUCTION. 

The  common  people  among  the  Greeks  ate  a  kind  of  barley-gruel,  which  was 
a  national  dish  and  was  made  as  follows: — -"Dry  near  the  tire  in  the  oven  20  lbs. 
of  barley  flour;  then  parch  it;  add  3  lbs.  linseed  meal,  %  lb.  coriander  seed,  2  oz. 
salt,  and  the  quantity  of  water  necessary."  To  this  a  little  millet  was  sometimes 
added  to  give  the  paste  greater-  cohesion  and  delicacy.  Oatmeal  was  used  by 
the  Greeks  and  Romans,  and  so  was  rice,  which  they  considered  beneficial  to  the 
chest.  Wheat  was  used,  and  a  favorite  Carthagenian  pudding  (which  was  also  a 
favorite  with  the  Romans)  was  made  with  wheat  flour  as  follows: — "Sift  the  flour 
and,  with  some  water,  put  it  into  a  wooden  vessel,  and  for  10  days  renew  the 
water  twice  each  day;  then  press  out  the  water  and  put  the  paste  in  another 
vessel;  reduce  it  to  the  consistency  of  thick  lees,  pass  it  twice  through  a  piece  of 
linen;  then  dry  it  in  the  sun  and  boil  it  in  milk;  season  according  to  taste." 

The  ancient  Egyptians  cultivated  very  much  the  same  agricultural  products 
as  at  the  present  day,  and  the  same  is  true  of  the  Greeks,  and  we  find  the  vine, 
the  fig,  the  olive,  the  apple  and  other  fruits,  mentioned  by  the  ancient  writers. 
In  the  vegetables  used  for  food  there  has  been  much  more  change  in  modern 
times  than  with  the  cereals.  Every  one  knows  that  the  potato  was  introduced 
after  the  discovery  of  America,  but  many  other  vegetables  now  in  common  use 
were  unknown  to  our  forefathers  even  a  few  generations  back,  and  the  fruits  then 
in  common  use  were  very  different  from  those  now  in  popular  favor.  Prof.  Tho- 
rold  Rogers,  writing  of  the  condition  of  things  in  England  four  or  five  centuries 
ago,  says:  "Scurvy  in  its  most  violent  forms,  and  leprosy,  modified  perhaps  by  the 
climate,  were  common  disorders,  for,  as  has  often  been  said,  the  people  lived  on 
salt  meat  half  the  year,  and  not  only  were  they  without  the  potato  but  they  do 
not  appear  to  have  had  other  roots  now  in  common  use,  as  carrots  and  parsnips. 

Onions  and  cabbage  appear  to  have  been  the  only  esculent  vegetables Spices, 

the  cheapest  of  which  was  pepper,  were  quite  out  of  their  reach.  Sugar  was  a 
very  costly  luxury,  and  our  forefathers  do  not  appear,  judging  from  the  scarcity 
of  the  notices,  to  have  been  very  skillful  in  their  management  of  bees."  How 
would  our  readers  like  to  get  along  without  sugar  and  spices,  and  with  no  escu- 
lent vegetables  but  the  onion  and  cabbage ! 

If  we  turn  from  the  past  to  the  cookery  of  the  present  day,  we  find  that  each 
nation  has  its  own  peculiarities.  Among  some  of  the  European  nations  the  art  of 
cookery  has  been  carried  to  a  high  degree  of  perfection,  and  we  might  learn  many 
valuable  lessons  by  a  study  of  their  methods.  One  thing  in  which  they  excel  is 
in  their  use  of  soups.  Soups  are  not  a  luxury,  nor  are  they  the  last  resort  of  poverty, 
and  all  classes  in  Europe  regard  them  as  a  necessary  part  of  their  dinner.  Amer- 
icans might  use  them  more  generally  to  advantage.  We  elsewhere  discuss  the 
peculiarities  of  the  cookery  of  the  leading  nations  of  Europe. 

When  we  consider  the  onerous  duties  of  the  housekeeper,  who  in  the  majority 
of  cases  is  also  the  cook,  we  cannot  over-estimate  the  importance  of  her  position, 
holding  as  she  does  in  her  keeping,  in  a  great  degree,  the  welfare,  health  and 
happiness  of  her  household.  She  has  the  general  care  of  the  home;  she  must 
provide  apparel  for  her  family,  suited  to  the  varying  seasons,  and  must  prepare 
or  supervise  the  preparation  of  3  meals  per  day  for  365  days  in  the  year.  This 
last  division  involves  on  the  part  of  the  housekeeper  an  appalling  amount  of 
mental  as  well  as  physical  labor,  and  it  is  hoped  the  recipes  for  cooking  which 
we  give,  will  greatly  assist  her  in  this  department. 

Count  Rumford,  "clear  of  thought,  and  straight  of  vision,"  remarked  that 
"the  number  of  inhabitants  who  may  be  supported  in  any  country  upon  its 
internal  products,  depends  as  much  upon  the  state  of  the  art  of  cookery,  as  upon 
that  of  agriculture."  If  cookery  be  of  so  much  importance,  it  ought  certainly  to 
be  studied  with  the  greatest  care.  If  any  work  requires  "eternal  vigilance,"  it  is 
cookery.  There  are  so  many  ways  where  a  little  variation  in  quality  of  ingre- 
dients; a  little  time  lost  just  when  some  article  of  food  needs  attention  or  the. 
fuel  fails  to  burn  as  expected,  that  one  must,  for  the  time  being,  give  it  undivided 
thought.  Although  some  processes  require  more  attention  than  others,  none  can 
be  properly  performed  if  left  long  to  itself.  A  good  cook  is  pretty  frequent  in 
her  visits  to  the  fire  to  see  how  the  operation  is  going  on,  and  to  seize  the  proper 
moment  in  giving  her  assistance. 

The  model  cook  should  be  neat  in  her  apparel;  dress  her  hair  in  a  tidy  man- 
ner and  always  in  her  own  room;  the  toilet  should  never  be  made  in  the  kitchen, 


INTRODUCTION.  11 

neither  wholly,  nor  in  part.  Her  hands  and  nails  should  be  scrupulously  clean; 
if  there  is  no  time  to  attend  particularly  to  the  nails,  they  should  be  cut  so  short 
that  no  special  care  is  needed  for  them,  aside,  from  the  use.  of  a  nail-brush.  Long, 
black  finger-nails  on  the  hands  of  a  cook,  are  not  appetizing  to  those  who  partake 
of  her  food. 

A  good  cook  is  never  wasteful,  for  the  reason  that  she  knows  how  to  turn 
everything  to  account.  A  few  scraps  of  cold  meat  may  be  minced,  nicely  flavored, 
and  with  an  egg  and  a  few  bread  crumbs,  dainty  rissoles,  or  croquettes,  can  be 
made.  Cold  vegetables,  scraps  of  meat  and  cold  fish,  can  be  made  into  salads, 
or  hashes,  that  are  liked  by  most  people. 

While  cooking,  a  clean  towel,  and  a  dish-pan  of  hot  water  constantly  at  hand 
in  the  sink  is  indispensable.  It  is  convenient  for  washing  any  utensil  that  may 
be  needed  while  at  work,  and  in  it  the  hands  may  be  cleansed  from  time  to  time 
if  they  become  smeared;  there  are  also  many  spare  minutes  while  baking,  when 
dishes  no  longer  needed  may  be  washed  and  put  away,  thus  making  lighter  the 
task  of  cleaning  up  when  all  is  done. 

Punctuality  in  serving  meals  cannot  be  too  much  insisted  on.  As  soon  as 
possible  after  one  meal,  preparation  and  forethought  for  the  next  will  greatly 
facilitate  this  desirable  condition.  After  the  house  is  put  in  order  in  the  morning, 
vegetables  should  be  made  ready,  the  soup  put  over  the  fire  if  it  is  a  part  of  the 
meal,  and  the  desert  arranged.  If  a  late  dinner  is  served,  the  luncheon  should  be 
punctually  laid  at  noon,  and  the  preparations  for  dinner  can  be  leisurely  made  in 
the  afternoon.  Cooking  for  a  noonday  meal  should  commence  early  in  the  day, 
for  hurry  and  good  cooking  can  never  go  together. 

Intoxicating  Liquors. — In  all  recipes  contained  in  the  department  of  cookery, 
which  advised  the  use  of  intoxicating  liquors,  we  have  substituted  fruit  juices— 
often  using  orange  or  lemon  juice.  Nothing  can  excel  the  unfermented  juices 
of  fruits  as  a  flavoring,  or  for  giving  a  desirable  piquancy  to  various  dishes. 
They  make  an  almost  perfect  substitute  for  liquors,  and  for  obvious  reasons  are 
much  safer  for  family  use.  Fruit  juice  can  be  easily  substituted  for  liquor  in 
any  recipe  by  those  who  desire  to  do  so.  In  fruit  cake,  %  CUP  or  10  tablespoons 
of  molasses  is  equal  to  1  wine  glass  of  brandy  or  other  liquor.  Those  so  disposed 
can  substitute  molasses  for  liquor  in  this  way  in  any  such  recipe  found  in  books, 
magazines  or  newspapers;  a  cake  thus  made  will  be  quite  as  good  as  though  the 
liquor  was  used.  As  the  alcohol  evaporates  in  baking,  however,  liquors  are  not 
so  objectionable  in  cakes  as  in  sauces. 

SUGGESTIONS  ABOUT  FOOD  AND  FEEDING. 

The  rapid  increase  of  human  knowledge  within  the  last  few  decades,  which 
we  all  view  with  more  or  less  wonder,  is  affecting  every  department  of  human 
activity.  The  art  of  housekeeping  is  feeling  its  influence,  and  it  is  becoming 
clear  that  the  time  has  come  for  a  science  of  housekeeping.  Many  thoughtful 
minds  are  studying  the  subject,  but  no  one  can  work  at  it  long  without  seeing 
that  household  science  lays  all  other  sciences  under  tribute.  Surely  the  running 
of  a  household  is  a  no  less  important  task  than  running  a  steam  engine  or 
steamboat,  and  it  requires  as  much  technical  knowledge  and  skill.  In  an 
address  made  a  few  years  ago  by  the  President  of  the  American  Institute  of 
Mining  Engineers  before  that  body,  he  said:  "If  technical  instruction  and 
skill  in  our  workmen  be  essential  to  the  economical  development  of  industry, 
and  the  welfare  of  those  engaged  in  it,  the  technical  education  of  their  wives 

is  not  one  whit    less  important Scientific  housekeeping  is  neither 

beneath  the  attention  of  the  refined,  nor  beyond  the  reach  of  the  uncultured. 
It  is  the  duty  of  the  rich;  it  is  the  salvation  of  the  poor."  Evidently  this  is 
perfectly  true. 

_  Now  no  one  can  thoroughly  master  the  art  of  cookery  without  knowing  some- 
thing of  the  chemical  composition  of  the  various  foods,  and  the  uses  they  serve  in 
the  system.  This  knowledge  is  especially  important  to  the  poor,  for  they  must 
necessarily  practice  the  most  rigid  economy,  and  they  should  know  what  foods 
are  best  adapted  to  supply  the  real  needs  of  their  families,  and  how  they  can  ob- 
tain the  most  and  best  nourishment  for  the  least  money;  and  yet  they  are  the 
ones  who  know  the  least  about  the  subject,  as  a  rule.    Carefully  kept  statistics 


12  INTRODUCTION. 

show  that  in  families  consisting  of  a  man  and  his  wife  and  3  or  4  children,  where 
the  income  is  small  or  moderate,  nearly  60$  of  the  whole  amount  is  spent  for  food. 
Of  course  in  families  enjoying  a  larger  income  the  proportion  is  less,  but  this 
shows  the  importance  of  the  question,  especially  when  it  is  remembered  that 
those  having  moderate  incomes  constitute  the  great  majority  of  the  people.  Let 
us  glance,  therefore,  (briefly  necessarily  for  want  of  space)  at  some  of  the  prin- 
ciples which  underlie  the  subject  of  food  and  feeding,  leaving  other  branches  of 
housekeeping  for  later  treatment.  And  first  let  us  take  the  assimilation  and  di- 
gestion of  food. 

THE  PROCESSES  OF  DIGESTION. 

An  old  and  very  good  illustration  likens  the  human  body  to  a  steam  engine, 
and  the  food  and  water  it  uses,  to  the  fuel  and  water  fed  to  the  engine.  This  anal- 
ogy, carried  out,  will  show  why  any  person  who  works  hard,  either  with  the  mind 
or  body,  requires  more  food  than  one  who  does  but  little,  a  fact  which  is  well 
known.  On  the  other  hand,  when  idle  persons  eat  large  quantities  of  nitrogen- 
ous food,  they  become  victims  of  inflammatory  disease,  or  their  health  otherwise 
suffers,  according,  probably,  to  whether  they  assimilate  it  or  not.  And  what  are 
the  processes  by  which  these  wonderful  bodies  of  ours  assimilate  the  food  which 
is  given  to  them?  Let  us  see.  From  the  time  the  food  enters  the  mouth,  it  may 
be  considered  as  passing  along  one  continuous  tube  or  canal  (which  is  some  35 
feet  long  and  of  varying  size),  and  as  it  passes  along,  part  is  taken  up  and  assim- 
ilated, and  part  is  finally  rejected  as  useless  to  the  body.  This  journey  through 
the  body  may  be  divided  into  5  stages,  in  which  the  food  is  submitted  to  the  al- 
ternate action  of  certain  alkaline  and  acid  fluids  which  cut  it  up  and  fit  it  for 
assimilation  by  the  system. 

First.— When  food  is  taken  into  the  mouth,  it  is  masticated,  that  is,  it  is 
crushed  and  torn  by  the  teeth  so  that  it  will  readily  fall  to  pieces  and  can  then 
be  easily  acted  on  by  the  digestive  juices.  While  in  the  mouth  the.  food  is 
thoroughly  mixed  (or  should  be)  with  a  fluid  secreted  by  the  salivary  glands,  and 
known  as  saliva.  This  saliva  is  a  glairy,  adhesive  and  alkaline  fluid,  which  pro- 
duces the  first  chemical  change  in  the  food;  the  saliva  possesses  the  power  of 
acting  very  rapidly  on  starch,  and  it  turns  part  of  it  into  sugar,  which  is  the  first 
step  towards  its  digestion. 

Second. — The  food  now  passes  into  the  stomach,  where  it  is  subjected  to  the 
action  of  the  gastric  juice.  This  is  secreted  in  considerable  quantities  by  the 
inner  lining  of  the  stomach,  and  it  is  a  clear,  transparent  and  acid  fluid,  contain- 
ing a  peculiar  and  important  principle,  called  pepsin.  This  acid  gastric  juice 
neutralizes  the  alkaline  saliva  and  checks  its  further  action*  (although  as  it 
penetrates  the  mass  of  food  somewhat  slowly,  the  action  of  the  saliva  is  not 
checked  immediately  on  entering  the  stomach)  and  it  is  strong  enough  to  dis- 
solve the  fibrin  and  albumen  of  the  food.  Acted  upon  by  this  fluid,  and  by  cer- 
tain wave-like  muscular  movements  of  the  stomach,  the  food  in  time  becomes 
thoroughly  dissolved,  with  the  exception  of  the  fatty  and  oily  matter,  which  is  not 
thus  far  affected.  The  stomach  acts  solely  on  albumenoids,  and  not  upon  starch, 
sugar  or  fat.  Small  quantities  of  mild  acids,  like  vinegar  and  lemon  juice,  add  to 
the  solvent  power  of  the  gastric  juice  and  are  therefore  beneficial  with  meats  and 
vegetables  not  easily  digested.  It  will  be  evident,  that  to  be  easily  acted  on 
by  the  juices  of  the  stomach,  the  food  should  readily  separate  into  minute  part- 
icles, and  it  should  not  be  enveloped  in  fat,  as  is  the  case  with  pastry.  Unlike 
the  lobster  tribe,  the  human  stomach  does  not  contain  teeth  and  so  cannot  grind 
and  tear  the  food  in  pieces,  hence,  if  it  is  not  thoroughly  masticated  by  the  teeth, 
before  reaching  the  stomach,  digestion  will  be  slow  and  difficult,  and  a  fit  of  in- 
digestion will  be  very  apt  to  follow.  Prom  the  stomach  the  food  passes  into  the 
small  intestine,  or  duodenum,  and  it  is  then  called  chyme. 

Third. — While  the  food  is  in  the  duodenum,  or  small  intestine,  it  is  subjected 
to  the  action  of  the  bile.    This  is  a  greenish  colored,  viscid  fluid,  of  a  bitter  taste 


*One  of  the  fundamental  principles  of  chemistry  which  every  one  should  iknow,  is  that 
an  alkali  will  neutralize  an  acid,  or  an  acid  an  alkali.  The  two  being  antagonistic,  (if  we 
can  use  that  term)  one  will  neutralize  the  other  at  any  time. 


INTRODUCTION.  13 

and  an  unpleasant  smell.  It  is  slightly  alkaline,  but  consists  principally  of  two  pe- 
culiar resinous  acids  (cholic  and  choleic)  in  combination  with  soda.  The  precise  ac- 
tion of  the  bile  on  the  chyme  or  food  is  not  very  clear,  but  it  neutralizes  the  acids  of 
the  gastric  juice  and  makes  it  alkaline,  so  that  the  pancreatic  juices  can  act  on  it, 
and  it  probably  assists  in  converting  it  into  chyle,  as  it  is  then  called,  and  in 
rendering  it  capable  of  absorption  bj<  the  lacteals.  This  chyle  is  a  milky  fluid, 
which  supplies  the  matter  from  which  the  blood  is  formed. 

Fourth. — While  in  the  duodenum,  and  being  acted  on  by  the  bile,  the  chyme 
is  also  subjected  to  the  action  of  the  pancreatic  juice,  which  is  secreted  by  the 
pancreas.  This  pancreas  is  sometimes  sold  as  sweetbreads,  and  many  are 
familiar  with  it  under  that  name.  This  pancreatic  juice  is  alkaline,  much  like 
saliva,  from  5  to  7  oz.  of  it  being  secreted  daily,  but  it  has  the  power  of  acting  on 
the  fats  and  oils  and  converting  them  into  an  emulsion,  and  thereby  fits  them 
for  absorption  into  the  system.  Oil  and  water  will  not  mix,  but  when  cut  into 
fine  particles  by  an  alkali,  like  soda  or  potash,  oil  will  mingle  with  water.  Now 
this  is  what  the  alkaline  pancreatic  juice  does  for  the  fats  and  oils — it  cuts  them 
into  fine  particles  so  that  they  mingle  readily  with  the  watery  fluid,  and  are 
afterwards  readily  absorbed  by  the  villi,  but  it  produces  little  other  change  in 
them.  Also  the  process  of  converting  starch  into  sugar  goes  on  here  once  more. 
It  is  in  the  duodenum  that  the  fats  and  oils  are  really  digested.  The  reader  will 
notice  that  the  alkaline  juices,  like  the  saliva,  etc.,  act  mainly  on  the  fats  and 
carbohydrates  of  the  food,  while  the  acid  juices  of  the  stomach  act  mainly  on  the 
albumenoids.  (Those  who  wonder  what  "albumenoids"  and  "carbohydrates"  are, 
will  find  an  explanation  later  on.) 

Fifth. — The  food  now  passes  along  the  intestinal  canal,  pushed  along  by  many 
muscles,  and  it  is  acted  on  by  the  intestinal  fluids,  which  are  the  last  in  the  pro- 
cess, and  which  appear  to  be  somewhat  akin  to  saliva. 

Next,  this  fluid  {chyle)  as  it  passes  along,  is  absorbed  into  the  lacteal  system  by 
small  projecting  filaments  {villi)  and  it  is  then  conveyed  to  the  mesenteric 
glands,  as  they  are  called,  and  these  glands  change  a  part  of  it  to  corpuscles,  and 
it  is  carried  along  in  small  tubes  to  an  elongated  pouch  with  a  formidable  looking 
name  (the  receptaculum  chyli)  which  lies  in  front  of  the  spine.  From  this  pouch 
the  fluid  is  conveyed  up  a  tube,  called  the  thoracic  duct,  to  the  left  side  of  the 
neck,  where  a  little  valve  opens  and  it  flows  into  and  mixes  with  the  impure 
blood  returning  through  the  veins;  these  carry  it  to  the  heart,  which  pumps  it 
into  the  lungs,  where  it  is  subjected  to  the  action  of  the  air,  the  oxygen  in  which 
consumes  its  impurities.  It  is  now  fresh,  pure  blood,  and  the  arteries  distribute 
it  through  the  body  to  perform  its  work  in  the  system.  Every  3  to  5  minutes  the 
blood  courses  over  the  system,  according  to  the  state  of  the  health  or  the  amount 
of  exercise  taken.  As  the  blood  thus  comes  to  it,  every  part  of  the  body  has  the 
singular  power  of  taking  to  itself  the  elements  it  needs  for  growth  or  repair. 
The  liver  takes  the  elements  needed  for  bile;  the  glands  of  the  mouth  those 
needed  for  saliva;  the  glands  of  the  eye  those  needed  for  tears;  the  cartilages 
take  their  needed  sulphur,  soda,  etc.;  the  bones  take  their  phosphate  of  lime, 
calcium,  magnesia,  etc.;  the  muscles  take  their  albumen,  fibrin,  phosphates, 
etc.;  the  nerves  and  brain,  most  wonderful  of  all,  get  their  phosphorus,  water, 
fats  and  albumen,  forming  that  wonderful  brain  substance  by  which  we  feel, 
smell,  taste,  hear,  see  and  think,  and  become  conscious  of  life,  and  the  stupendous 
universe  about  us,  with  its  tremendous  and  absorbing  problems. 

From  the  foregoing  it  will  be  clear  that  the  blood  is  what  the  food  makes  it, 
and  as  the  supply  of  food  is  increased  or  decreased,  or  its  quality  altered,  so  the 
blood  is  affected,  and  the  health  maintained  or  impaired;  hence  the  importance  of 
adopting  such  rules  of  diet  and  regimen  as  will  ensure  a  due  supply  of  healthy 
blood  to  the  system.  So  much  for  the  process  by  which  the  body  assimilates  its 
food.  If  this  process  is  once  clearly  understood,  it  will  make  plain  many  facts  in 
regard  to  foods  and  their  uses.  Now  let  us  inquire  a  little  into  the  composi- 
tion of  the  body,  which  grinds  up,  dissolves  and  assimilates  its  food  in  this 
remarkable  way. 

COMPOSITION  OF  THE  BODY. 

The  principal  chemical  substances  found  in  the  body  are  oxygen,  hydrogen, 
carbon,   nitrogen,    sulphur,   phosphorus  and   chlorine,    and  the    metals,  iron, 


U  INTRODUCTION. 

sodium,  potassium,  lime  and  magnesia.  The  body  derives  its  nourishment  from 
compounds  of  the  elements,  not  directly  from  the  elements  themselves;  thus, 
although  nitrogen  composes  f  of  the  air,  it  only  nourishes  the  body  when  it 
comes  as  a  compound  resulting  from  some  form  of  life.  The  4  most  important 
elements  are  oxygen,  hydrogen,  carbon  and  nitrogen.  A  human  body  weighing 
154  lbs.  is  found  to  contain,  oxygen  111  lbs.;  hydrogen  14  lbs.;  carbon  21  lbs.; 
nitrogen  3  lbs.  10  oz.  In  a  body  of  the  same  weight  we  find  that  the  compounds 
in  which  these  elements  exist,  and  their  weights  in  the  body  are: 

lbs.  oz. 

Water,  containing  oxygen  and  hydrogen           116  0 

Gelatine,  containing  the  4  elements                 '  16  0 

Albumen,            "          "  "        "                                4  3 

Fibrin,                 "           "  "        "                                 4  4 

Fat,  containing  carbon  and  hydrogen                12  0 

Of  course  these  figures  are  only  approximate.  The  various  other  elements  also 
always  appear  in  different  combinations,  as  calcium  in  the  bones  and  teeth; 
sulphur  in  the  tissues  and  secretions;  phosphorus  in  the  bones  and  brain,  and  so 
on.  These  necessary  compounds  of  the  human  body  represent  albumenoids  or 
flesh-formers,  carbohydrates  or  heat-givers,  and  salts  or  mineral  matters.  These 
elements  are  received  into  the  human  system  in  the  form  of  food,  and  become 
distributed  to  the  different  parts  of  the  body  by  the  process  of  digestion 
and  assimilation.  If  any  of  these  elements  are  wanting,  the  system  suffers  and 
they  must  be  restored  to  the  individual.  For  example,  rickets  in  children  is 
caused  by  the  bones  containing  too  little  lime,  and,  hence,  the  best  remedy  in  its 
early  stage  is  plenty  of  lime  water,  or  some  other  substance  containing  lime. 
Now  we  find  that  the  ultimate  elements  of  the  food  we  take  are  precisely  simi- 
lar to  the  ultimate  elements  of  the  human  body,  therefore  let  us  next  examine 
the  composition  of  the  different  foods  so  that  we  may  know  how  to  supply  the  va- 
rious elements  needed  by  the  system. 

THE  COMPOSITION  OF  FOODS. 

Air,  food  and  water  are  the  essential  chemical  compounds,  without  which  no 
living  creature  can  exist.  Nature  has  lavishly  enveloped  us  with  the  first  of 
these  elements,  and  water,  the  next  great  want,  is  given  without  stint  to  those 
who  choose  to  dwell  where  it  is  abundant;  but  to  obtain  food  has  given  man  the 
most  concern,  and  civilized  man  has  encompassed  the  globe  to  minister  to  his 
needs  in  this  line.  According  to  government  rations  for  soldiers,  about  2% 
pounds  of  dry  food  daily  is  required  for  each  man,  3^  part  being  meat. 

Liebig  divides  food  into  2  classes: — First,  the  nitrogenous  elements  of  nutri- 
tion, i.  e.,  the  flesh-formers.  In  this  class  are  placed  albumen,  vegetable  and  meat 
fibres,  etc.  In  the  second  class  are  the  non-nitrogenous  elements,  or  heat  pro- 
ducing foods,  as  fat,  starch  and  sugar.  A  mixed  diet  has  been  found  to  be  the 
most  conducive  to  health,  and  the  best  drink  is  water.  This  fluid  forms  over  % 
of  the  weight  of  the  body.  The  function  of  food  may  therefore  be  described  as 
twofold:  First,  to  produce  and  maintain  the  structures  necessary  to  all  life;  and 
second,  to  generate  heat. 

A  distinguished  physiologist  has  said,  "the  whole  animal  body  is  modified 
protoplasm;"  and  this  protoplasm  (the  original  cells  of  nutrition)  combined  with 
air  and  water,  is  found  in  the  food  we  take  into  the  system.  The  food  necessary 
for  the  perfect  development  of  the  various  tissues  of  the  body  — bone,  cartilage, 
muscle,  brain,  with  its  system  of  nerves,  and  blood  — the  all-pervading  and  all- 
sustaining  mother-tissue  — must  consist  of  a  judicious  combination  of  the  3  great 
nutritive  elements,  viz.,  the  Albumenoids,  or  Proteids  as  they  are  frequently 
called,  the  Carbohydrates  and  the  Fats,  and  a  certain  amount  of  Salts  are  also 
needed.  But  some  reader  will  say,  what  is  meant  by  "albumenoids"  and  "carbo- 
hydrates" and  what  are  they?  Let  us  examine  the  various  food  elements  a  little. 
If  you  will  recall  our  previous  illustration,  where  the  human  body  was  likened 
to  a  steam  engine,  it  will  be  evident  that  2  things  will  be  needed:  First,  certain 
parts  which  do  not  burn,  like  the  boiler  and  metal  work  of  the  engine;  and 
second,  the  introduction  of  certain  substances  which  do  burn,  like  the  fuel  fed 
to  the  engine.    Now  nitrogen  possesses  the  peculiar  power,  when  combined  with 


INTRODUCTION.  15 

the  other  elements,  of  interfering  with  their  power  to  unite  with  oxygen,  that  is, 
it  prevents  their  burning,  for  what  we  term  burning  is  simply  a  rapid  combina- 
tion of  oxygen  with  other  elements  (oxydation  it  is  called);  thus,  in  burning  coal, 
for  instance,  the  oxygen  of  the  air  rapidly  unites  with  the  carbon  of  the  coal, 
and  heat  is  given  off  — that  is  what  takes  place  as  it  burns.  Now,  when  hydrogen 
and  carbon  are  united  with  nitrogen,  as  is  the  case  with  the  tissues  of  the  body, 
(nitrogen  forms  16  %  of  animal  tissues)  the  nitrogen  prevents  the  tissues  them- 
selves from  burning  readily,  although  oxydizing  (burning)  processes  take  place 
in  them.  Let  this  principal  be  clearly  understood,  for  it  forms  the  basis  of  all 
that  is  known  about  a  suitable  dietary  for  the  system;  for  a  perfect  diet  should 
consist  of  enough  nitrogenous  material  to  produce  and  maintain  the  tissues,  as 
we  have  said  before,  and  hydrocarbons  enough  to  generate  heat  for  working  the 
body.  Although  the  primary  use  of  nitrogenous  food  is  to  produce  and  maintain 
the  tissues,  secondarily  it,  also  facilitates  the  absorption  of  the  carbohydrates; 
and  although  the  carbohydrates  produce  force,  they  also,  in  some  measure,  con- 
tribute to  the  formation  of  tissue.  The  first  of  the  great  food  elements  of  which 
we  spoke  were  the  albumenoids,  so  let  us  see  what  they  are. 

Albumenoids  (they  are  often  also  called  Proteids)  is  a  name  applied  to  all  the 
foods  which  serve  the  same  purpose  as  albumen.  Albumen  (from  albus,  meaning 
white)  is  a  complex  substance,  chemically  considered.  It  contains  hydrogen, 
carbon,  some  oxygen,  a  little  sulphur,  and  its  essential  characteristic  of  nitrogen. 
The  albumenoids,  therefore,  all  contain  nitrogen,  while  neither  fats,  starches  nor 
sugars  do,  and  for  this  reason  they  are  often  called  nitrogenous,  and  sometimes 
they  are  spoken  of  as  the  flesh-formers.  As  we  explained  in  describing  the  pro- 
cess of  digestion,  the  albumenoids  are  principally  digested  in  the  stomach,  and 
after  passing  into  the  blood,  they  are  conveyed  to  the  tissues  which  they  nourish, 
(albumen  being  changed  to  fibrin,  and  fibrin  to  flesh)  and  we  have  just  explained 
the  importance  of  the  fact  that  they  do  not  readily  oxydize.  The  surplus  and 
waste  albumenoids  are  oxydized  (burned)  in  the  liver,  and  there  they  give  the 
acids  of  the  bile,  and  the  solids  of  the  urine,  and  their  imperfect  oxydation  pro- 
duces biliousness.  And  now  let  us  see  about  the  foods  from  which  we  derive  the 
albumenoids.  The  white  of  an  egg  is  almost  pure  albumen,  and  is  the  most 
familiar  instance  of  it;  but  nothing  can  live  and  grow  without  nitrogen,  and  so 
our  supplies  of  albumenoids  come  as  lean  meat,  fish,  gluten  in  flour,  milk  curd  or 
cheese,  gelatine,  fibrin  in  oats,  in  beans,  peas  and  lentils,  and  in  eggs,  while  in  the 
yolk  of  the  egg  it  is  called  vitellin.  Albumen  is  found  in  many  foods  besides 
egg;  the  blood  of  many  animals  contains  it,  most  vegetable  juices  and  many 
seeds  and  nuts.  Fibrin  (an  albumenoid)  is  both  animal  and  vegetable,  and  is 
found  in  the  muscles  of  animals,  and  in  wheat  and  other  grains.  Casein  is  an 
albumenoid  that  is  found  in  the  curd  of  milk,  and  in  the  pulses,  i.  e.,  beans,  peas 
and  len  tils.  Vegetable  casein  is  sometimes  called  legumen,  but  was  given  that  name 
before  it  was  known  to  be  practically  the  same  as  animal  casein  of  milk.  Gela- 
tine and  some  substances  nearly  like  it,  are  known  as  gelatinoids,  and  they  can 
replace  albumen  in  part,  though  not  altogether.  Ossein  is  the  part  of  bones  to 
which  they  owe  their  strength  and  elasticity.  When  boiled  under  enough  press- 
ure to  prevent  the  escape  of  steam,  it  changes  to  gelatine,  but  in  cold  water  it  is 
insoluble. 

It  is  easy  to  see  that  the  albumenoids  are  more  costly  than  the  starches,  and 
even  than  the  fats,  and  that  therefore  these  are  the  foods  that  the  poorest 
people  suffer  from  the  want  of.  On  the  other  hand  these  are  the  foods  that  taste 
well,  and  of  these  many  well-to-do  people  eat  too  much.  They  are  sometimes 
called  flesh-formers  because  their  chief  use  is  to  provide  material  to  build  up 
the  body  (in  the  growth  from  childhood  to  maturity)  and  in  adult  life  to  replace 
material  lost  in  work.  It  does  not,  however,  follow  that  the  more  flesh-formers 
they  eat,  the  larger  the  children  will  grow,  or  that  the  more  a  person  swallows 
after  middle  life  is  passed,  the  more  he  will  economize  his  failing  strength.  To 
overload  a  child  with  food  is  a  sure  way  to  make  him  small  and  feeble,  and  after 
middle  life,  if  people  get  heavier,  it  is  because  they  accumulate  stores  of  fat, 
which  is  certainly  no  advantage.  Although  the  system  can  convert  one 
albumenoid  into  another,  it  cannot  form  an  albumenoid  out  of  fat  or  starch; 
hence,  no  other  food  element  can,  for  any  length  of  time,  be  substituted  for  the 
albumenoids  without  injury  to  the  system. 


16  INTRODUCTION. 

Putrefaction  was  supposed,  until  a  few  years  ago,  to  toe  caused  by  the  action  of 
oxygen,  but  it  has  now  been  demonstrated  that  it  is  caused  by  minute  germs,  called 
bacteria.  When  the  bacteria  attack  dead  matter,  which  contains  nitrogen,  the  result  is 
putrefaction.  Fermentation  is  the  result  when  they  attack  matter  which  does  not 
contain  nitrogen.  Meat,  fruit  and  food  are  preserved  from  the  action  of  these  bacteria 
by  dryin"-  by  canning,  and  by  using  antiseptics.  Drying  protects  against  them  because 
they  cannot  live  without  water,  and  their  spores  will  not  germinate  without  moisture. 
Canning  offers  protection  by  heating  the  article  canned  until  the  bacteria  are  all  killed, 
and  then  sealing  up  the  can  so  that  no  more  can  enter;  and  antiseptics  act  by  poisoning  or 
destroying  these  low  forms  of  life. 

Carbohydrates.— In  the  human  body,  as  in  the  steam  engine,  the  fuel  consists 
of  carbon  and  hydrogen,  which  readily  unite  with  oxygen— that  is,  they  burn,  and 
so  supply  heat  and  power  to  run  the  machine  or  organism.  This  fire  burns  con- 
stantly in  the  body,  but  "  instead  of  taking  place  in  one  spot  and  so  rapidly  as  to 
be  accompanied  by  light,  as  in  the  fire  in  the  grate,  it  takes  place  in  each  drop  of 
blood,  and  so  slowly  and  continuously  as  not  to  be  noticed."  From  the  2  ele- 
ments of  carbon  and  hydrogen,  starch,  sugar  and  fat  are  built  up.  In  using  the 
fuel — food,  the  system  turns  the  starch  to  sugar,  and  the  sugar  to  fat.  It  is  in  the 
form  of  fat  that  the  surplus  carbohydrates  are  stored  up,  and  it  is  then  drawn  on 
in  times  of  need.  The  average  amount  of  fat  so  stored  up  suffices  for  about  10 
days'  fuel,  but  the  hybernating  bear  lives  through  the  winter  on  his  stored  up 
fat.  Fats  we  speak  of  later,  but  by  the  carbohydrates  are  generally  meant 
starch,  and  the  different  sugars  (sugar  of  milk  and  the  vegetable  sugars),  although 
cellulose,  pectose,  gum,  etc.,  are  closely  allied  to  the  carbohydrates,  and  would 
be  classed  with  them.  The  starches  and  sugars  come  mainly  from  the  vegetable 
world.  The  average  working  man  requires  17  to  18  ounces  of  carbohydrates  daily 
in  his  food,  a  much  larger  quantity  of  these  carbohydrates  than  of  fats  being 
needed  to  furnish  a  given  amount  of  force  to  the  body. 

Sugar  is  so  easily  dissolved  and  diffused  that  it  requires  no  preliminary  di- 
gestive process  to  prepare  it  for  assimilation;  it  passes  without  change  into  the 
circulation.  If,  however,  it  is  supplied  in  excess  of  the  needs  of  the  sys- 
tem, on  reaching  the  stomach  it  undergoes  lactic  acid  fermentation,  and  thus 
occasions  the  acidity  from  which  some  dyspeptics  suffer.  When  not  in  excess, 
the  sugar  is  carried  on  to  the  liver,  where  it  undergoes  certain  changes  which 
lead  us  to  think  that  it  contributes  to  the  production  of  fat,  rather  than  force. 
There  are  3  kinds  of  sugar — sucrose,  or  cane  sugar;  glucose,  or  grape  sugar; 
lactose,  or  milk  sugar,  and  they  are  all  composed  of  carbon,  hydrogen  and  oxy- 
gen. Where  taken  as  food,  both  lactose  and  sucrose  are  converted  by  the  gastric 
juice,  etc.,  into  glucose,  in  which  form  they  are  absorbed  into  the  circulation. 
Glucose  undergoes  no  change.  Sugar  fuses  at  320°,  and  at  420°  F.  it  turns  into 
a  brown  substance,  called  caramel.  Sugar  will  decompose  by  fermentation  and 
break  up  into  simpler  compounds. 

Saccharine.— This  sweetening  agent  which  is  being  substituted  in  place  of  sugar  in  some 
instances,  was  discovered  about  1879  by  Oonstantine  Fahlberg,  then  a  student  in  Johns 
Hopkins  University  at  Baltimore.  It  is  derived  from  coal-tar,  and  possesses  500  times  the 
sweetening  power  of  sugar.  It  is  not  a  carbohydrate,  like  sugar,  and  it  possesses  no  nutri- 
tive value,  as  it  passes  through  the  system  unchanged.  It  is  an  acid,  will  not  ferment, 
and  has  some  antiseptic  properties,  retarding  decomposition  and  fermentation.  It  Is 
slightly  soluble  in  cold  water,  more  so  in  warm,  and  quite  soluble  in  boiling  water.  Its  use 
is  not  at  all  injurious.  As  it  has  no  nutritive  value,  it  must  be  regarded  more  as  a  spice 
than  as  a  food.  Owing  to  its  intense  sweetness  it  must  be  much  diluted  for  use.  Because 
of  its  antiseptic  properties  it  will  be  useful  in  preserving  fruits  and  fruit  juices.  It  is  also 
very  useful  in  diseases  like  diabetes,  glycosure,  obesity,  etc.,  where  sugar  is  injurious,  and 
also  in  babies'  food.  It  is  one  of  the  new  additions  made  by  modern  science  to  the 
resources  of  the  housewife,  as  well  as  the  physician  and  manufacturer,  and  its  use  will 
doubtless  rapidly  increase  in  the  near  future.  It  should  never  be  put  into  cold  water  and 
then  boiled  to  dissolve  it,  as  that  causes  its  partial  decomposition;  add  it  to  hot  fluids  only 
after  they  are  removed  from  the  fire.  It  should  never  come  into'  direct'  contact  with 
metallic  vessels. 

Starch. — Before  starch  can  be  taken  into  the  blood,  the  digestive  juices  must 
turn  it  into  sugar,  and  we  explained  how  that  is  done  in  describing  the  process 
of  digestion.  Raw  starch  would  pass  out  of  the  system  unassimilated.  When 
boiled,  the  granules  are  burst  and  are  then  readily  converted  into  sugar.  If  not 
acted  on  by  the  saliva,  it  passes  on  to  the  duodenum,  or  small  intestine,  where  its 
digestion  really  takes  place.  If  the  stomach  is  over-loaded  with  starch  at  any 
time,  the  digestion  will  be  influenced  by  certain  fermentations  which  result.  It 
seems  to  be  the  opinion  of  the  best  authorities  at  present  that  the  fermentation 
of  starchy  foods,  for  which  their  stomachs  are  not  prepared,  is  the  cause  of  the 


INTRODUCTION  17 

severe  and  troublesome  diarrhoeas  of  small  children.  To  show  the  value  of  cook- 
ing and  the  differences  in  starches,  it  may  be  said,  that  experiment  has  shown 
that  uncooked  starch  from  Indian  corn  may  be  completely  turned  into  sugar  by 
the  action  of  the  saliva  in  3  minutes,  oat  starch  in  6  minutes,  wheat  starch  in  40 
minutes,  and  potato  starch  in  3  hours,  but  after  thorough  cooking,  all  starches 
require  nearly  the  same  time,  so  that  cooking  greatly  aids  its  digestion.  Under 
the  microscope,  starch  is  seen  to  consist  of  little  rounded  granules,  varying  in 
form  according  to  the  plant  from  which  they  are  derived.  The  granules  are 
covered  with  a  skin  which  is  insoluble  in  cold  water,  but  which  bursts  in  boiling 
water,  letting  out  the  starch  which  is  inside. 

Bread,  potatoes,  rice,  barley  and  all  the  floury  foods  contain  more  starch  than 
anything  else,  and  corn-flour,  sago,  tapioca  and  arrowroot  are  nearly  pure 
starches.  There  is  much  starch  also  in  beans,  peas  and  lentils,  though  they  are 
often  spoken  of  as  flesh -forming  foods,  because  of  the  large  amount  of  casein 
which  they  contain.  There  is  no  starch  in  milk,  but  sugar  which  takes  its  place. 
It  will  be  seen  that  the  starchy  foods  are  the  cheapest  and  most  abundant  of  all, 
so  that  whenever  people  have  enough  of  any  food,  they  are  pretty  sure  to  have 
enough  of  these.  The  poor  people  get  these  most  readily,  but  the  richer  classes 
and  brain-workers  take  more  albumenoids  and  fats,  and  less  of  the  carbohydrates, 
at  least  in  their  starchy  form.  Starch  is  composed  of  oxygen,  hydrogen  and 
carbon,  and  when  heated  to  401°  F.  it  becomes  dextrin,  which  is  more  easily  di- 
gested than  starch,  although  its  chemical  composition  is  the  same.  This  dextrin 
is  used  in  preparing  many  of  the  "infant  foods,"  and  it  is  sometimes  called 
"British  gum,"  or  "starch  gum." 

The  Cellulose  or  cell  structure  of  plants  is  closely  allied  to  the  carbohydrates, 
it  being  composed  of  oxygen,  hydrogen  and  carbon.  It  must  first  be  changed  in- 
to sugar  before  it  can  be  absorbed  into  the  system.  Cellulose  appears  in  the  mem- 
brane which  surrounds  the  grains,  in  the  rinds  of  fruits,  in  the  skins  of  seeds,  in 
the  woody  fibre  of  grain  stalks,  etc.,  while  cotton,  linen  and  linen  paper  are 
mainly  composed  of  it.  When  submitted  to  the  action  of  strong  sulphuric  acid 
it  may  be  turned  to  sugar;  but  while  the  chemist  in  his  laboratory  can  change 
cellulose  into  sugar,  the  human  stomach  does  not  readily  act  on  it.  In  young 
and  tender  plants,  like  lettuce,  celery,  cabbage,  etc.,  the  stomach  digests  from 
50$  to  60$  of  the  cellulose,  but  in  older  plants  it  often  becomes  almost  indigest- 
ible. Even  the  ox  with  his  many  stomachs  only  gets  out  of  grass  and  hay  about 
60$  of  its  albumenoids,  and  with  man,  vegetable  foods,  as  generally  prepared, 
yield  from  25$  to  50$  less  of  their  nutrients  than  do  animal  foods.  A  working 
man  will  digest  97$  of  the  meat  he  eats,  while  only  60$  of  the  nutrients  of  a  dish  of 
beans  are  assimilated,  because  the  beans  are  encased  in  a  tough  skin  of  cellulose; 
and  this  shows  the  need  of  thorough  cooking,  and  the  separation  of  the  cellulose  as 
far  as  possible  from  the  other  ingredients.  Cellulose  should,  on  the  whole,  be  re- 
garded as  a  foreign  substance  to  be  gotten  out  of  the  way,  rather  than  as  a  food. 

Pectose  is  composed  of  carbon,  hydrogen  and  oxygen,  and  is  found  in  many 
fruits,  like  currants,  plums  and  peaches,  and  in  some  vegetables,  like  turnips, 
beets,  etc.  It  turns  to  a  vegetable  jelly  known  as  pectine  when  boiled  in  water, 
and  this  it  is  which  makes  the  preserves  firm  which  are  made  from  plums,  pears, 
currants,  peaches,  etc. 

Ghivi,  which  in  its  composition  is  nearly  allied  to  cane  sugar,  is  found  dis- 
solved in  the  juices  of  many  fruits  and  stems,  like  apple,  plum,  etc.,  and  is 
usually  accompanied  with  a  little  lime  and  potash.  Gum  arable  and  Senegal  are 
2  examples.  Mucilage  appears  in  the  onion,  quince-seeds,  linseed,  etc.,  but  does 
not  dissolve  to  a  thin  liquid  like  gum  arabic,  although  it  probably  undergoes  so- 
lution and  absorption  in  the  body.     It  is  classed  with  the  carbohydrates. 

Fats. — The  fats  usually  employed  as  food  are  the  vegetable  oils,  like  olive  oil 
— and  latterly  in  this  country  cotton-seed  oil  — and  the  animal  fats,  .as  butter, 
suet,  lard,  etc.,  etc.  Fat  is  composed  of  carbon,  hydrogen  and  oxygen.  It  is  an 
essential  article  of  food,  and  many  people  suffer  in  health  for  the  want  of  it, 
especially  among  the  poor  who  cannot  afford  the  dearer  fats,  or  do  not  understand 
the  cheaper,  and  among  the  sickly,  who  cannot — or  fancy  they  cannot — digest 
fat  in  any  form.  -If  it  is  only  digested,  one  fat  is  as  good  for  food  as  another. 
Cod  liver  oil  and  cream  are  the  easiest  to  digest.  Fats  that  are  greatly  heated 
decompose,  and  then  are  always  difficult  of  digestion,  which  is  the  reason  why 

2 


18  INTRODUCTION. 

fried  food  often  disagrees.  Many  persons  who  cannot  eat  a  lump  of  fat  with 
meat,  hot  or  cold,  can  eat  buttered  toast,  or  suet  pudding,  or  take  fat  in  some  of 
the  many  forms  in  which  the  cook  can  serve  it.  Fat  and  starch  can  replace  one 
another  to  some  extent,  but  there  must  be  some  fat,  and  it  is  better  in  this  cli- 
mate to  have  some  starchy  or  floury  food.  In  the  coldest  countries  plants  do  not 
grow,  and  so  starch  is  there  unknown,  fat  taking  its  place. 

The  nutritive  value  of  fats  and  oils  is  the  same,  and  they  may  be  considered 
as  formed  by  the  union  of  a  fatty  acid  and  glycerine;  thus,  if  3  parts  of  some 
fatty  acid,  like  palmitic,  be  heated  with  1  part  of  glycerine  in  a  closed  tube,  palm 
oil  and  water  are  produced.  Wax,  though  probably  of  similar  value  as  a  nutri- 
ent, differs  from  oils  and  fats  somewhat,  notably  in  not  yielding  glycerine.  The 
proper  digestion  of  fat  is  a  matter  of  great  importance,  for  it  is  essential  to 
healthy  tissue  formation,  as  well  as  being  a  fuel-food,  and  it  is  essential  to  tissue 
repair  as  well  as  tissue  growth.  Many  maladies  may  be  averted  if  the  system 
can  be  made  to  digest  fat  properly.  Fat  and  sugar  do  not  furnish  bile  acids,  and 
hence  the  bilious  are  comparatively  well  with  them,  but  they  may  lead  indi- 
rectly to  biliousness.  If  more  food  is  eaten  than  the  body  needs,  the  hydrocar- 
bons, which  are  readily  oxydized,  will  be  burnt  (oxydized)  first,  and  the  surplus, 
or  nitrogenous  elements,  will  be  imperfectly  oxydized,  which  is  the  reason  that  a 
rich  meal  causes  biliousness  in  persons  subject  to  such  attacks. 

Fat  swallowed  in  lumps  is  almost  indigestible,  and  hence  it  should  be  taken 
into  the  system  in  a  finely  divided  state.  Children  who  will  not  eat  fat  meat  will 
often  take  the  same  amount  of  fat,  as  butter,  spread  on  bread.  More  force  is 
produced  by  the  burning  or  oxydation  of  fat  in  the  body,  than  by  any  other  food 
material,  as  it  possesses  2%  times  the  power  of  starch  for  maintaining  heat  and 
activity.    The  average  working  man  requires  2  oz.  of  fat  daily. 

Carbonates  and  Salts. — These  include  common  salt  (chloride  of  sodium), 
potash,  phosphates  of  lime  and  iron.  Common  salt  is  a  necessary  food,  and  the 
only  mineral  habitually  added  to  food  as  such.  Potash  is  found  in  all  fresh 
fruits  and  vegetables;  probably  no  defect  is  more  common  than  the  want  of 
these,  especially  in  our  large  towns.  Lime  is  necessary  for  the  building  up  of  the 
bones  and  teeth.  It  is  found  in  whole  grains,  and  the  young  get  it  in  their 
milk;  when  we  see  weak  and  distorted  limbs  and  broken  teeth,  we  know  that  it 
has  been  missing.  Iron  occurs  in  nearly  all  articles  used  for  food,  though  in 
minute  quantities.  Distinct  traces  are  found  in  the  ashes  of  all  plants  used  for 
food.  These  salts  are  essential  to  health  and  without  them  many  diseases  would 
fasten  on  the  body,  like  the  scurvy  which  was  such  a  terror  to  sailors,  and  which 
vegetables  cure  like  magic.  The  bones  of  an  adult  man  contain  at  least  70  %  of 
mineral  matter,  mostly  phosphate  of  lime,  while  the  muscles,  blood  and  tissue 
contain  salts  of  potash  and  soda,  and  some  iron. 

Salt. — Common  salt  is  the  most  widely  distributed  substance  in  the  body;  it 
exists  in  every  fluid  and  in  every  solid;  and  not  only  is  it  everywhere  present, 
but  in  almost  every  part  it  constitutes  the  largest  portion  of  the  ash  when  any 
tissue  is  burnt.  In  particular  it  is  a  constant  constituent  of  the  blood,  and  it 
maintains  in  it  a  proportion  which  is  almost  wholly  independent  of  the  amount 
which  is  consumed  with  the  food.  The  blood  will  take  up  so  much  and  no  more, 
however  much  we  may  take  with  our  food;  and  on  the  other  hand,  if  none  be 
given,  the  blood  parts  with  its  natural  quantity  slowly  and  unwillingly.  From 
salt  also  comes  the  hydrochloric  acid  for  the  bile,  and  the  soda  for  the  bile  salt  in 
the  liver.  It  is  of  immense  importance  in  the  processes  ministering  to  the 
nutrition  of  the  body,  for  not  only  is  it  the  chief  salt  in  the  gastric  juice,  and 
essential  for  the  formation  of  bile,  but  it  is  an  important  agent  in  promoting  the 
process  of  diffusion,  and  therefore  of  absorption.  Direct  experiment  has  shown 
that  it  promotes  the  decomposition  of  albumen  in  the  body.  Nothing  can 
demonstrate  its  value  better  than  the  fact  that  if  albumen  without  salt  is  intro- 
duced into  the  intestine  of  an  animal,  no  portion  of  it  is  absorbed,  while  it  all 
quickly  disappears  if  salt  be  added.  The  conclusion,  therefore,  is  obvious,  that 
salt,  being  wholesome,  and  indeed  necessary,  should  be  taken  in  moderate  quan- 
tities, and  that  abstention  from  it  is  likely  to  be  injurious. 

Acids  are  found  in  fruits  and  most  vegetable  products.  ■  They  appear  partly 
in  the  free  state  and  partly  in  the  form  of  salts.  Citric,  Malic,  Tartaric  and 
Oxalic  are  the  4  principal  acids  found  in  vegetable  products.    Acetic  acid  is  mainly 


INTRODUCTION.  19 

produced  by  the  fermentation  of  alcohol  or  sugar,  but  also  occurs  in  some  fruits, 
especially  when  they  are  over-ripe  or  decaying.  In  the  process  of  digestion  the 
acids  all  act  much  alike,  exerting  a  solvent  action  on  some  foods,  but  their 
nutritive  power  is  small.  Too  little  is  consumed  to  give  out  much  heat  or  force, 
and  besides  they  are  already  highly  oxydized,  and  easily  turn  into  the  final  prod- 
ucts of  oxydation — carbonic  acid  and  water.  Citric  acid  is  the  most  wholesome, 
tartaric  and  malic  less  so,  and  oxalic  the  least  so  of  any  acid. 

Water  undergoes  no  chemical  change  in  the  system  and  produces  no  force; 
yet  it  is  indispensable  as  a  component  part  of  food,  for  it  facilitates  the  chemical 
changes  which  take  place. 

Potential  Energy  of  Food. — As  we  elsewhere  explain,  one  office  of  food  is  to 
furnish  the  body  with  heat  and  muscular  power.  Although  the  carbohydrates 
and  fats  serve  principally  to  furnish  these,  the  albumenoids  can  also  be  used  by 
the  body  for  this  purpose;  for  while  the  carbohydrates  and  fats  cannot  take 
the  place  of  the  albumenoids,  the  latter  can,  to  a  certain  extent,  supply 
the  place  of  the  former.  Scientists  by  experimenting  with  an  apparatus  called  a 
calorimeter  determine  the  amount  of  heat  (or  muscular  energy)  which  any  given 
food  can  furnish  to  the  body,  and  this  they  call  its  "potential  energy."  To  ex- 
press the  amount  of  these  heat  (or  force)  producing  elements,  they  adopt  a  stand- 
ard which  they  call  a  "Calorie."  A  Calorie  is  an  amount  of  heat  which  will 
raise  lib. of  water  4°  F.,  or  stated  in  terms  of  mechanical  energy,  it  will  lift  1.53 
tons  1  foot.  Now  to  tell  the  average  woman  that  lib. of  potatoes,  for  example, 
contains  375  Calories,  means  to  her  almost  nothing  at  all,  but  to  tell  her  that  lib. 
of  potatoes  contains  375  Calories,  while  lib.  of  wheat  flour  contains  1645  Calories, 
ought  to  mean  a  great  deal,  for  it  means  that  lib.  of  flour  contains  nearly  5  times 
as  much  force  producing  power  (i.  e.,  potential  energy)  as  there  is  in  lib.  of 
potatoes.  So  also  lib.  of  neck  of  beef,  worth  perhaps  8  cents,  contains  1100 
Calories  (20  cents'  worth  would  contain  2750  Calories),  while  lib.  of  sirloin,  worth, 
say,  20  cents,  only  contains  1210  Calories,  so  that  clearly  the  neck  is  very  much 
the  most  economical  piece  to  buy,  as  it  will  supply  the  body  with  more  than 
twice  as  much  power  for  the  same  money.  By  studying  the  tables  given  else- 
where, in  this  comparative  way,  not  only  regarding  the  potential  energy,  but  the 
relative  amounts  of  albumenoids  and  other  elements  as  well,  much  information 
ought  to  be  obtained  of  practical  value  to  housekeepers. 


THE  AMOUNT  AND  CHARACTER  OF  FOOD  NEEDED. 

The  amount  and  character  of  the  food  required  varies  with  different  individ- 
uals, depending  on  age,  sex,  climate,  season  of  the  year,  physical  and  mental  ex- 
ertion, etc.  Under  a  meat  diet  there  is  a  more  copious  secretion  of  gastric  juice, 
while  under  a  vegetable  diet  the  saliva  is  more  abundant,  showing  that  there  is 
a  provision  in  the  system  for  variation  in  the  food.  A  certain  amount  of  meat 
undoubtedly  increases  the  bodily  vigor,  although  it  is  not  absolutely  necessary 
for  health.  Of  the  entire  weight  of  the  body,  in  the  plant-eating  animals  the 
digestive  organs  form  15  to  20$;  in  the  flesh  eating  animals  they  form  5  to  Q%;  in 
man  they  form  7  to  8$.  It  will  be  seen  that  man  approaches  much  nearer  to  the 
flesh-eating  than  to  the  plant-eating  animals,  and  yet,  by  the  aid  of  proper  cook- 
ing (which  is  a  great  help)  man  can  digest  many  of  the  vegetables  much  more 
quickly  and  completely  than  can  be  done  by  a  plant-eating  animal  like  the  cow. 
But  to  get  the  amount  of  albumenoids  essential  to  health  on,  say,  an  exclusive 
potato  diet,  a  man  would  heed  at  least  10  lbs.  a  day,  and  Dr.  Edward  Smith 
found  some  poor  Irish  laborers  who  actually  eat  that  much.  Such  a  diet,  how- 
ever, would  distend  the  digestive  organs  unnaturally,  and  make  one  dull  and 
stupid.    A  mixed  diet  is  undoubtedly  best. 

In  early  life  all  vital  processes  are  most  rapid,  and  provision  must  also  be 
made  for  the  growth  of  all  parts  of  the  body,  while  in  old  age  the  vital  processes 
become  least  active;  they  are  more  active  in  the  spring,  in  cold  climates,  and  in 
highlands,  and  less  so  at  the  end  of  summer,  in  hot  climdttes,  and  in  valleys,  and 
exercise  always  stimulates  these  processes.  Man  requires  more  nitrogenous  food 
than  woman. 

Looking  at  the  different  nations  of  the  world,  we  find  they  have  almost  in- 


20 


INTRODUCTION. 


stinctively  chosen  the  foods  most  suited  to  their  needs.  The  Esquimaux,  eating 
his  raw  blubber  and  other  fatty  and  oily  foods,  obtains  his  carbonaceous  food  in 
its  most  concentrated  form,  furnishing  the  body  with  the  fuel  needed  to  give 
warmth  to  withstand  the  intense  cold.  In  Sweden,  Norway  and  other  northern 
countries,  they  tend  towards  the  Esquimaux's  oily  diet.  In  India  and  China,  on 
the  other  hand,  where  rice  is  universally  eaten,  there  is  very  little  need  for  the 
concentrated  heat  producers,  the  starch  and  sugar  of  rice,  with  a  little  olive 
oil  or  melted  butter,  answering  their  needs  well.  In  Italy  the  general  use  of 
macaroni  furnishes  them  with  the  nutritive  elements  of  wheat  in  its  most  con- 
densed form.  And  so,  in  the  light  of  the  principles  we  have  explained,  we  might 
examine  the  characteristic  diets  of  other  nations,  but  it  must  not  be  inferred  that 
the  staple  articles  of  food  in  the  different  countries  are  in  all  instances  perfectly 
adapted  to  their  needs,  as  some  at  least  of  the  national  dietaries  might  with  a 
little  knowledge  be  much  improved. 

But  while  the  leading  races  have  in  a  general  way  hit  on  the  foods  which  are 
in  the  main  suited  to  their  conditions,  our  complicated  modern  life  demands 
many  varieties  of  diet.  The  literary  man  requires  one  thing,  the  mechanic 
another;  the  growing  child  needs  food  suited  to  its  wants,  and  the  aged  grand- 
parent a  different  diet,  and  we  should  know  how  to  meet  these  varying  wants. 
Our  advancing  knowledge  of  the  human  organism  and  the  requirements  of  the 
system  demand  that  our  dietaries  shall  be  more  thoughtfully  prepared,  and 
adapted  to  our  needs.  If  there  is  an  excess  of  one  element,  and  a  lack  of  another, 
that  excess  or  lack  means  disorder  in  the  system,  and  in  time  disease,  for  which 
our  ignorance  is  wholly  responsible.  Moreover,  an  intelligent  selection  of  foods 
will  often  avert  the  effects  of  inherited  or  other  disorders,  and  a  child  burdened 
with  scrofulous  or  other  diseases,  may,  by  a  scientific  selection  of  the  proper 
foods,  be  often  saved  from  a  life  of  invalidism  or  an  early  death. 

It  is  impossible,  however,  to  lay  down  exact  rules  for  the  selection  of  food  in 
health  and  disease.  Only  general  principles  can  be  given,  known  scientific  facts 
can  be  explained,  well-tried  common  experience  recorded,  and  from  these  mate- 
rials the  diet  most  suited  to  special  cases  must  be  selected  with  intelligence  and 
judgment.  While  in  robust  health  the  appetite,  guided  by  the  experience  of  the 
individual,  is  a  generally  safe  guide,  in  debility,  dyspepsia,  gout  and  all  diseased 
conditions,  the  proper  selection  of  the  food  is  of  very  great  importance.  All  phy- 
sicians know  that  if  proper  dietetic  rules  were  universally  observed,  robust 
health  would  be  more  common,  recovery  from  disease  more  rapid,  and  the  death 
rate  much  diminished.  They  also  know  that  the  digestibility  of  food,  and  its 
proper  assimilation,  depend  as  much  upon  the  mode  of  its  preparation  as  upon 
the  condition  of  the  person  who  eats  it,  hence  the  importance  of  understanding 
the  principles  of  cookery.  One  physician  told  the  writer  that  the  trouble  with 
nearly  %  of  all  the  patients  who  came  to  him  for  advice  could  be  traced  to  indi- 
gestion, caused  by  improper  or  improperly  cooked  food. 

While  the  limits  of  this  work  will  not  allow  us  to  treat  as  fully  as  we  would 
like  the  large  and  important  subjects  of  food  and  dietetics,  we  can  indicate  the 
direction  in  which  the  best  thought  of  the  day  is  tending,  and  we  hope  some  of 
our  readers  may  be  stimulated  to  further  investigation  of  these  subjects,  for  it 
will  not  be  a  waste  of  either  time  or  labor.  It  will  well  repay  earnest  study  in 
leisure  hours,  and  it  can  safely  be  said  that  there  is  no  subject  which  deserves 
more  careful  study  by  women  than  that  of  the  selection  and  preparation  of  food. 

Now  the  intelligent  woman  who  attempts  to  provide  food  for  her  family  must

aim  to  supply  all  the  different  food  elements  in  proper  proportion,  and  to  do  it

COOKING  AND  ITS  OBJECT. 

The  principal  object  of  cookery  and  the  artificial  preparation  of  food  must  be 
to  assist  in  the  wonderful  series  of  changes  known  as  digestion  and  as- 
similation. The  art  of  cookery  flourished  for  ages  before  the  modern  sciences 
were  known,  and  until  recently  there  was  no  such  thing  as  a  science  of  cookery; 
but  the  mere  fact  that  cookery  long  existed  without  the  aid  of  science  is  no
