reason  why  it  should  continue  to  do  so.  In  the  latter  part  of  the  18th  century 
Count  Rumf ord  made  his  memorable  experiments  on  feeding  the  poor  in  Munich, 
and  although,  owing  to  the  ignorance  of  chemistry  and  physiology  which  then 
prevailed,  many  of  his  deductions  were  wrong,  the  interest  in  the  study  of 
the  food  problem  was  awakened.  The  first  experimental  agricultural 
college  in  the  world  was  established  at  Mockern,  Saxony,  in  1851,  but  since  then 
many  others  have  been  established,  and  these  and  the  great  universities  have  in 
their  laboratories  made  analyses  of  most  of  the  foods  used  by  men  and  animals, 
and  they  are  now  doing  some  most  excellent  work.  Since  1863,  when  Dr.  Ed- 
ward Smith  made  his  report  on  the  amount  of  food  needed  to  support  a  family 
(a  report  called  out  by  the  necessities  of  the  cotton  spinners  in  Cheshire  and 
Lancashire,  England,  growing  out  of  the  American  Civil  War),  other  scientists 
have  investigated  and  traced  all  the  food  taken  into  the  bodies  of  certain  individ- 
uals while  they  were  at  rest  and  while  they  were  at  work.  Estimates  have  thus 
been  made  of  the  amount  of  food  needed  to  sustain  both  men  and  women  in  va- 
rious conditions  in  life,  and  much  light  has  been  shed  on  the  subject  of  furnishing 
the  proper  amount  of  nourishment  to  sustain  the  human  system  in  health  and 
vigor.  And  how  about  the  science  of  cookery?  This  science  can  make  no  ad- 
vance without  the  help  of  chemistry  and  physiology,  but  these  are  comparatively 
new  sciences,  and  every  year  they  make  substantial  advances.  Only  a  few 
scientific  principles  have  yet  been  applied  in  cookery,  but  there  has  been  a  great 
advance  in  the  last  20  years  and  the  way  is  now  open  for  still  greater  progress  in 
the  future.  Scientists  have  been  so  absorbed  by  their  work  in  other  directions 
that  they  have  given  comparatively  little  attention  to  the  subject  of  cookery, 
although  a  beginning  has  been  made,  and  much  work  done.  The  day  cannot  be 
far  distant,  however,  when  all  the  resources  of  our  rapidly  advancing  sciences 
will  be  brought  to  bear  on  this  subject,  and  we  think  it  probable  that  through 
the  application  of  chemistry  to  the  processes  of  cookery,  changes  will  be  wrought 
in  the  not  distant  future  which  will  fully  equal,  in  their  practical  benefits  to  the 
human  race,  any  of  the  advances  which  have  so  dazzled  and  astonished  those 
who  have  been  privileged  to  live  in  the  last  few  decades.  And  these  are  not  the 
wild  dreams  of  visionaries,  but  the  cautiously  formed  opinion  of  those  who  know 
much  of  the  work  which  has  been  done  in  our  laboratories,  and  of  the  principles 
which  underlie  the  various  processes.  Mr.  Mattieu  Williams,  speaking  of  this 
subject,  asks  the  question,  "whether  a  very  great  refinement  of  scientific  cookery 
may  or  may  not  enable  us  to  convert  nutshells,  wood  shavings  and  sawdust  into 
wholesome  and  digestible  food?"  and  he  answers:  "I  have  no  doubt  whatever  that  it 


INTRODUCTION.  23 

can."  Other  scientists  see  nothing  unreasonable  in  such  an  opinion.  In  the  chem- 
ists' laboratory  already  an  old  handkerchief  and  a  piece  of  an  old  shirt  have  been 
turned  into  sugar.  Of  course  this  cannot  be  done  in  the  average  kitchen,  but  it 
shows  the  possibilities  before  us. 

When  we  consider  that  a  healthy  man  of  usual  size  takes  into  his  stomach 
every  year,  on  an  average,  about  300  lbs.  of  meat,  500  lbs.  of  bread,  300  lbs.  of  pota- 
toes and  vegetables,  90  lbs.  of  butter  and  fats,  and  150  gallons  of  tea,  coffee  and 
water,  or  other  fluids,  we  can  easily  see  the  great  importance  which  is  to  be  at- 
tached to  the  proper  cooking  and  preparation  of  this  material.  And  none  are 
more  interested  in  this  subject  than  the  poor.  Those  who  have  been  much 
among  the  working  classes  know  that  they  suffer  from  various  disorders  arising 
from  badly-cooked  and  therefore  ill-digested  food,  for  the  cure  of  which,  suitable 
food  skilfully  prepared  will  do  more  than  medicine.  Furthermore,  their  necessi- 
ties compel  them  to  make  much  out  of  little,  and  to  perform  every  cooking  op- 
eration with  the  fewest  appliances,  and  with  no  time  to  spare ;  but  perfection  in 
the  art  of  cookery  is  only  attained  by  experience,  a  careful  study  of  the  qualities 
of  foods,  and  some  knowledge  of  the  underlying  principles  involved. 

We  certainly  think  that  it  is  very  important  for  those  who  from  necessity  or 
choice  are  called  upon  to  prepare  the  food  on  which  the  life  and  health  of  the 
human  body  must  depend,  to  endeavor  to  understand  at  least  the  fundamental 
principles  which  underlie  their  work — not  only  for  their  own  sake,  but  for  those 
for  whom  they  provide,  or  may  be  called  upon  to  advise.  In  this,  as  in  other 
things,  "  knowledge  is  power."  While  many  among  the  poorer  classes  of  people 
suffer  because  of  the  limited  variety  and  inferior  quality  of  their  food,  large 
classes  of  more  prosperous  people  suffer  quite  as  much  from  the  imperfect  man- 
ner in  which  their  food  is  prepared  for  the  table. . 

Now,  if  we  ask  what  are  the  principal  reasons  for  preparing  and  cooking  food, 
the  following  would  be  given :  (1).  Cooking  makes  the  food  more  attractive  to 
the  senses.  Not  only  the  appetite,  but  the  eye  and  smell  are  gratified  by  the 
processes  of  baking,  roasting  and  stewing,  and  thus  the  food  becomes  more 
longed  for,  and  consequently  more  gratifying  than  it  could  possibly  be  in  a  raw 
state.  (2).  The  process  of  cooking  is  attended  or  preceded  by  processes  of  clean- 
ing, by  which  much  in  food  that  is  repulsive  and  useless  is  cast  aside.  (3).  The 
process  of  cooking  facilitates  and  hastens  the  process  of  digestion.  Boiling, 
stewing  and  roasting  make  flesh,  fish  or  vegetables  softer  or  more  juicy,  so  that 
they  are  more  easily  acted  on  by  the  digestive  fluids  and  more  easily  assimilated 
by  the  system.  (4).  Cookery  destroys  the  parasites  with  which  some  foods  are 
infested.  (5).  Because  of  the  economy  of  warm  food.  All  cold  food  introduced 
into  the  stomach  must  be  warmed  there  before  it  can  be  digested  and  assimilated, 
and  if  it  is  warmed  before  being  eaten,  it  is  a  distinct  saving  to  the  system. 

The  most  important  results  of  cookery  at  present  are  to  be  ascribed  to  the 
action  of  heat  upon  the  various  constituents  of  our  food.  Many  of  the  foods 
now  eaten  would  become  useless  to  us  if  they  had  to  be  eaten  raw.  Cooking  may 
not  alter  the  chemical  constitution  of  a  food,  and  yet  it  may  entirely  change  its 
practical  value  to  mankind.  Many  of  the  changes  wrought  by  heat  are  easily  ex- 
plained. Whether  albumen  is  barely  coagulated  or  is  hard  and  horny,  whether 
fiber  is  shrivelled  or  swelled,  whether  gelatine  is  dry  and  brittle  or  dissolved,  it 
does  not  take  a  scientific  head  to  discover,  but  science  tells  us  why  these  things 
are,  and  so  enables  us  to  bring  our  food  readily  into  whatever  state  we  will. 
Given  good  food,  and  one  cook  so  manipulates  it  that  the  eaters  are  well  nour- 
ished and  pleased;  another  cook  leaves  them  hungry  and  discontented.  Unhappily, 
as  things  go,  many  a  badly  cooked  dish  has  cost  an  industrious  husband  a  month's 
earnings  in  doctor's  bills,  and  many  a  human  life  has  been  sacrificed  to  the  in- 
genious preparations  of  our  kitchens.  If  every  intelligent  cook  would  try  and 
find  out  the  reasons  for  the  various  operations  of  the  kitchen,  instead  of  working 
by  rote  in  a  blind  sort  of  way,  the  gain  to  the  community  would  be  almost  ines- 
timable. 

TIMES  AND  MANNER  OF  EATING. 

Among  the  principal  laws  of  diet  which  have  been  established  by  experience, 
and  which  are  more  or  less  explained  and  confirmed  by  chemical  and  economical 
science,  are  these :    (1).    The  appetite,  with  all  its  liabilities  to  error,  is,  within 


24  INTRODUCTION. 

certain  limits,  and  under  the  guide  of  individual  experience,  a  valuable  guide  in 
determining  the  quality  and  quantity  of  our  food.  (2).  The  food  of  all  classes 
should  be  generous,  both  in  quantity  and  quality;  and  there  should  be  an  agree- 
able variety,  not  only  from  week  to  week  and  day  to  day,  but  at  each  principal 
meal.  (3).  The  quantity,  the  quality  and  the  variety  of  food  must  be  modified 
by  race,  climate,  season,  age,  sex,  occupation,  habit,  and  the  progress  of  civiliza- 
tion. (4).  The  principal  meals  should  be  taken  with  tolerable  regularity,  and  in 
calmness  and  leisure,  and  amid  agreeable  social  surroundings. 

The  custom  of  having  regular  hours  for  meals  is  peculiar  to  civilization,  and 
is  only  possible  where  food  is  tolerably  abundant  and  accessible;  it  is  most  neces- 
sary among  those  who  are  most  sensitive,  delicate  and  nervous.  The  times  for 
eating  for  the  great  majority  of  people  should  be  morning,  noon  and  night. 
Those  who  work  hard  cannot  labor  to  advantage  if  the  meals  are  more  than  6 
hours  apart,  as  they  begin  to  lose  strength. 

However  the  diet  may  be  regulated,  a  few  simple  rules  should  always  be  borne 
in  mind:  (1).  That  while  a  low  or  spare  diet  is  seldom  either  necessary  or  harm- 
less, the  stomach  should  never  be  filled  to  an  extent  sufficient  to  cause  any  sen- 
sation of  uneasiness.  (2).  That  eating  should  always  be  sufficiently  slow  to 
admit  of  thorough  mastication  of  the  food.  (3).  That  solid  food  should  not  be 
taken  with  much  fluid,  it  being  better  to  drink  freely  between  meals.  (4).  That 
neither  mind  nor  body  should  be  actively  exercised  very  soon  after  a  full  meal. 
If  these  simple  rules  could  be  adopted,  and  at  the  same  time  a  sufficient  amount 
of  exercise  could  be  taken,  pure  air  breathed,  and  proper  habits  of  life  adopted, 
the  health  and  happiness  of  most  people  would  be  much  improved. 

THE  HOUSEWIFE'S   AIM. 

In  conclusion  we  may  say,  that  the  work  which  devolves  on  the  housewife  in 
providing  the  food  for  a  family,  may  be  divided  into  4  heads:  (1).  In  obtaining 
the  necessary  food  elements,  she  should  endeavor  to  obtain  them  in  their  cheapest 
form — this  would  come  under  the  head  of  marketing.  (2).  Having  purchased 
the  food,  she  should  so  cook  it  as  to  best  utilize  its  nutritive  elements — this  re- 
quires intelligent  cooking  so  that  the  digestibility  of  the  food  may  be  increased 
instead  of  lessened,  as  is  too  often  the  case.  (3).  To  so  season  and  flavor  the 
food  as  to  make  it  palatable  and  inviting.  To  make  food  so  attractive  that  it  will 
be  eaten  with  zest  is  an  item  too  often  overlooked  by  the  indifferent  cook.*  (4). 
To  utilize  every  scrap  so  that  nothing  is  wasted. — To  render  such  assistance  as 
we  can  to  our  readers,  in  these  various  branches  of  their  work,  will  be  our  aim 
in  the  following  pages. 

*The  old  saying  that  an  attractive  article  of  food  causes  the '"mouth  to  water"  has  a 
basis  in  fact,  because  the  sight  of  it  will  cause  the  salivary  glands  to  freely  discharge  their 
juices,  which  is  the  "watering  of  the  mouth"  referred  to,  while  a  repulsive  article  of  food 
does  not  do  so;  an  attractive  dish  of  food,  therefore,  clearly  stands  the  best  chance  of  being 
well  digested. 


MARKETING. 

The  thrifty  housewife  will  find  it  advantageous  to  do  the  marketing  for  the 
household  herself,  instead  of  simply  ordering  through  others.  By  so  doing  she 
will  secure  2  advantages — she  can  (1)  select  the  best  provisions,  and  (2)  get  them 
at  the  lowest  prices.  Many  housekeepers  dread  going  to  market,  and  think  it  is 
not  a  proper  place  for  ladies;  this  is  a  mistake,  for  as  many  gentlemen  will  be 
found  among  marketmen  as  in  any  other  business,  and  as  practical  knowledge  of 
this  kind  possessed  by  the  housewife  directly  affects  the  comfort  of  the  family, 
and  the  cost  of  supporting  it,  information  of  this  character  should  be  obtained 
as  far  as  possible  by  every  housekeeper.  As  a  variety  of  food  is  essential  to  both 
the  health  and  comfort  of  every  individual,  the  endeavor  should  be  made,  when 
purchasing,  to  provide  a  succession  of  different  kinds  of  food,  both  animal  and 
vegetable.  It  is  often  imagined  that  variety  involves  expense,  whereas  the  very 
opposite  is  true,  as  the  wider  the  range  of  a  housekeeper's  ideas  in  marketing,  the 
greater  the  opportunity  of  purchasing  to  advantage.  As  there  is  sometimes  a 
run  on  the  market  for  special  articles,  which  advances  their  prices,  while  other 
articles  may  be  proportionately  cheap,  it  is  often  best  not  to  decide  beforehand 
just  what  will  be  bought.  A  good  deal  of  care  is  required  in  marketing,  espec- 
ially for  a  large  family  with  a  limited  income,  and  we  hope  the  hints  we  offer  on 
these  subjects  will  prove  serviceable  to  our  readers.  One  thing,  which  many 
people  need  to  be  particularly  reminded  of,  is  that  to  pay  cash  down  is  the  only 
way  to  insure  the  best  value,  either  in  the  goods  purchased  or  in  comfort  gained. 
Poor  people  especially  suffer  from  ignorance  about  the  nutritive  value  of  dif- 
ferent foods.  They  seem  to  think  that  there  is  some  peculiar  virtue  in  the 
choicest  cuts  of  meat,  the  best  flour,  the  most  delicate  vegetables  and  the  highest 
priced  butter,  but  it  is  an  erroneous  idea.  Experienced  marketmen  say  that  the 
rich  will  buy  the  coarser  cuts  of  meat,  but  the  poor  refuse  them,  and  that  the 
money  of  the  poor  is  the  most  wastef  ully  expended.  The  amount  of  money 
wasted  in  injudicious  marketing  by  people  of  small  means  is  surprising.  They 
quite  generally  depend  on  buying  high  priced  foods,  rather  than  on  intelligent 
cooking  and  serving  to  make  their  tables  attractive.  It  is  certain  that  the  poor 
display  the  worst  economy  in  purchasing  as  well  as  in  the  cooking  of  their  food. 
Now  people  should  know  (poor  people  especially)  that  the  lower  priced  and 
tougher  parts  of  meat  contain  fully  as  much  nutriment  as  the  high  priced  cuts. 
In  buying  large  classes  of  food  (not  all)  it  is  emphatically  not  true  that  the  high- 
est priced  is  the  cheapest.  The  rich  will  always  buy  the  choicest  articles,  but  it 
is  delicate  flavor,  not  nutriment,  which  they  pay  for.  In  buying  codfish,  beans 
or  flour,  10  cents  will  get  as  much  nutriment  for  our  bodies  as  50  or  75  cents  will 
obtain  in  tenderloin,  salmon  or  lobster.  A  little  study  and  skill  in  preparing 
them  is  all  that  is  needed  to  utilize  the  lower  priced  foods  and  make  them  at- 
tractive. Tough  meat  can  be  made  tender  in  various  ways  which  we  explain 
elsewhere.  Buy  meat  ahead  in  winter,  and  keep  it  until  it  grows  tender.  Half 
a  calf's  head  will  make  a  splendid  soup.  Buy  the  fat  middle  rib  frequently,  and 
then  stew  it  long  and  well.  Make  soups  of  trimmings  and  tough  pieces,  and  the 
next  day  chop  up  the  tasteless  meat  and  make  it  palatable  with  flavors  and 
sauces.  Make  stews  and  ragouts  of  fairly  good  pieces,  and  broil  or  bake  the  parts 
which  are  tender  and  juicy.  When  fish  is  cheap,  buy  it.  Catfish,  for  example, 
are  excellent  when  fried. 

Considering  their  nutritive  value,  eggs,  even  when  cheapest,  are  not  as  cheap 
as  the  lowest  priced  cuts  of  meat  from  fat  animals,  but  they  are  valuable  in 
furnishing  variety,  and  they  can  be  used  in  making  various  egg  dishes.  Eggs  at 
15  cts.  a  doz.  are  about  equivalent  to  meat  at  12^  cts.  a  lb.;  at  30  cts.  a  doz.  they 
correspond  to  meat  at  25  cts.  a  lb.  It  will  be  seen  that  more  nourishment  can  be 
obtained  with  a  given  amount  of  money  by  buying  the  cheaper  parts  of  meat, 
like  brisket,  neck  and  flank.  Cheese  ought  to  be  more  highly  valued  than  it  is, 
and  can  be  bought  profitably  once  a  week.  (See  what  we  say  about  it  elsewhere.) 

(25) 


26  MARKETING. 

It  is  surprising  how  much  can  be  saved  to  a  family  by  a  little  intelligence  in 
buying,  and  some  knowledge  of  scientific  cookery.  A  diet  which  is  both  nour- 
ishing and  palatable  can  be  supplied  at  a  comparatively  small  cost  in  that  way. 

Great  care  should  also  be  taken  that  food  is  not  only  well  bought,  but  that 
nothing  is  thrown  away,  or  suffered  to  be  wasted  in  the  kitchen.  The  shank 
bones  of  mutton,  so  little  esteemed  in  general,  give  richness  to  soups  or  gravies, 
if  well  soaked  and  crushed  before  they  are  added  to  the  boiling,  being  particularly 
nourishing  for  sick  persons.  Roast  beef  bones,  or  shank  bones  of  ham,  make  ex- 
cellent stock  for  pea  soup.  The  dripping  from  a  half  spoiled  joint  is  useless  for 
food,  and  the  bone  will  certainly  spoil  soup.  Save  all  scraps  of  meat  and  bones 
left  from  roasts  and  broils,  and  utilize  them  in  the  soup  pot.  Also  save  the 
.  trimmings  from  corned  beef,  tongue,  ham,  etc.,  as  they  will  make  many  relishes. 
Have  the  trimmings  sent  home  with  the  meat  when  buying  it,  as  they  may  be 
used  in  soups  and  sauces. 

BUYING  MEATS. 

In  addition  to  the  special  directions  given  below,  it  may  be  useful  to  observe 
that  moderately  fat  meat  is  the  best  in  every  respect.  If  it  is  very  lean,  the  ani- 
mal has  been  underfed,  and  the  meat  will  probably  be  tough  and  tasteless;  if 
there  is  an  excess  of  fat,  it  renders  the  joint  costly  when  purchased  at  current 
rates.  Very  lean  meat  is  dear  at  any  price.  A  golden  rule  to  act  on  is  that  the 
inferior  joints  of  the  best  meat  are  better  than  the  best  joints  of  inferior  meat. 
Lean  meat  is  nitrogenous,  or  flesh-forming,  and  is  preferable  for  use  in  hot 
weather,  or  for  people  living  in  a  warm  climate;  but  in  cojd  countries  where 
heating  food  is  required,  fat  meats  are  more  necessary. 

Although  the  flesh  of  young  is  tenderer  than  that  of  adult  animals,  it  is  more 
difficult  to  digest.  Dr.  Smith  has  shown  that  this  is  mainly  because  the  flesh  of 
young  animals  cannot  be  perfectly  masticated;  hence  veal  and  lamb  are  more 
difficult  to  digest  than  beef  or  mutton,  and  are  worse  for  dyspeptics.  The  tis- 
sues are  more  gelatinous,  and  less  nourishing  and  stimulating  in  young  than  in 
adult  animals;  but  if  very  old,  the  flesh  may  be  so  tough  as  to  nearly  defy  both 
mastication  and  digestion. 

Good  raw  meat  is  neither  very  pale  nor  very  dark;  it  is  a  rich  red,  and  has  a 
marbled  look,  given  by  the  small  veins  of  fat  running  through  it.  To  the  touch, 
it  is  firm  and  elastic,  and  should  scarcely  wet  the  finger.  When  taken  from  a 
dish  upon  which  it  has  been  standing,  little  or  no  moisture  should  remain  behind. 
Bad  or  inferior  meat  is  either  lacking  in  color,  or  is  of  a  dark  purplish  tint.  It  is 
wet  or  sodden  to  the  touch,  and,  if  left  standing,  will  leave  a  pool  of  blood  and  water 
behind.  Without  having  an  actually  tainted  smell,  it  has  often  a  very  sickly  one; 
the  inside  fat  surrounding  the  kidneys  and  liver  is  often  suffused  with  blood. 
Meat  which  is  over  fresh,  or  too  recently  killed,  will  manifest  itself  by  the  smell 
quite  as  readily  as  tainted  meat — it  will  suggest  excessive  bloodiness. 

Do  not  buy  butcher's  meat,  or  poultry,  that  shows  any  signs  of  cruel  treatment  before 
being  slain;  meat  with  bruises,  discolored  spots  and  extra vasated  clots  of  blood  should  be 
avoided,  as  the  flesh  of  suffering  animals  undergoes  a  change  which  is  unwholesome. 

Putrid  Meat. — The  albuminous  substances  m  meat  when  they  putrefy  generate  certain 
organic  poisons,  which,  taken  into  the  system,  produce  symptoms  much  like  severe  cholera 
morbus,  and  often  result  fatally.  These  cases  are  most  often  caused  by  canned  meats  and 
sausage,  but  may  be  caused  by  any  putrid  meat. 

Beef. — This  should  have  an  open  grain,  smooth  and  juicy;  it  should  yield 
easily  to  pressure  and  have  a  rich  carnation  color.  The  fat  should  be  of  a 
whitish  or  cream  color — the  whiter  and  firmer  the  better.  Tht  bone  should  be 
small,  and  the  flesh  full.  If  the  meat  is  purplish,  and  the  fat  yellow,  it  is  poor 
beef.  When  fed  on  oil  cakes,  the  fat  will  be  of  a  deeper  color,  and  the  flesh  flabby. 
Stall-fed  beef  has  a  lighter  color  than  grass-fed,  and  beef  long  kept  turns  a 
darker  color  than  fresh  killed.  Ox  beef  is  best,  and  that  of  a  heifer  next.  Beef 
of  a  dark-brownish  or  purplish-red  should  be  avoided;  this  color  is  sometimes  the 
result  of  some  feverish  disease,  or  the  animal  has  been  slaughtered  before  resting, 
after  being  heated  and  exhausted  by  driving — in  either  case,  the  meat  is  un- 
wholesome. A  strict  observance  of  the  above  simple  rules  will  insure  even  an  in- 
experienced buyer  against  imposition. 

The  flavor  of  beef  is  richer,  and  it  takes  less  of  it  to  satisfy  hunger  than  of 
the  flesh  of  any  other  domestic  animal,  and  it  is  on  the  whole  the  best  for  a 
standby. 


MARKETING.  2? 

In  order  to  obtain  a  good  juicy  piece  of  roasting  beef,  the  meat  must  be  of  the  best 
quality,  and  come  from  a  well  fatted  animal;  it  must  also  have  been  kept  a  sufficient  time 
after  being  slaughtered  to  enable  it  to  ripen  a  little  before  cooking,  but  not  to  the  point  of 
the  slightest  taint.  Meat,  if  cooked  immediately  after  slaughtering,  is-  apt  to  be  hard  and 
tough. 

The  best  roasting  pieces  are  the  sirloin  and  standing- rib  pieces.  The  latter  should  have 
all  the  bone  cut  out,  and  be  rolled  up  and  skewered  into  a  round  shape;  or  the  long  string 
niece  may  be  cut  off  and  salted  for  boiling,  and  hashed  for  another  day.  It  should  be  re- 
membered that  a  good  sized  joint  is  more  economical  in  the  long  run,  even  in  small  families, 
than  a  very  small  one. 

Good  boiling  pieces  of  fresh  beef,  are  the  neck  piece,  the  shoulder  clods,  the  "aitchbone, 
and  any  part  of  the  animal  left  after  the  roasts  and  steaks  are  reserved.  The  boiling 
pieces  may  be  kept  in  a  pickle  of  brine,  with  sugar  in  the  solution,  and  a  little  saltpetre. 

The  best  parts  of  beef  for  steaks  are  the  sirloin,  porter-house,  and  the  first  cut  of  the 
round;  the  second  round  is  also  fairly  good.  Other  round  steaks  can  be  chopped  finely  in  a 
chopping  bowl  or  minced  in  a  machine  now  made  for  that  purpose,  for  Hamburg  or  Salis- 
bury steaks.  Hamburg  steaks  are  highly  seasoned,  but  Salisbury  steaks  are  more  suited  to 
invalids.  Pieces  of  the  shoulder-clod  can  also  be  minced  and  used  in  this  way,  as  well  as 
other  nice  pieces  of  beef  from  parts  not  suited  for  roasting  or  broiling  without  chopping. 
Chopped  steaks  are  most  desirable  for  old  people  and  invalids,  also  for  delicate  children. 
People  with  good  teeth  should  use  them  in  order  to  keep  them  good. 

The  fillet  of  beef  is  the  tenderloin— the  choicest  part  of  the  animal.  This,  by  foreign 
cooks  is  "larded,"  the  method  of  doing  which  we  explain  elsewhere.  In  this  country  the 
combined  flavor  of  fresh  beef  and  salt  pork  is  not  so  much  in  favor. 

Veal. — This  must  be  fresh  killed  to  be  good.  The  lean  should  be  white, 
smooth  and  juicy,  and  the  joints  stiff;  the  fat  should  be  white,  firm  and  abundant. 
Stale  veal  is  moist  and  clammy,  the  joints  are  flabby,  and  there  is  a  faint  musty 
smell.  In  buying  the  head,  see  that  the  eyes  are  plump  and  bright — not  dull  and 
sunk  in  the  head.  In  buying  legs,  get  those  which  are  not  skinned,  as  the  skin 
is  good  for  jelly  or  soup. 

Veal  is  neither  so  nutritious  nor  so  easily  digested  as  beef.  It  is  more  easily 
digested  when  roasted  or  broiled  than  when  boiled.  Some  people  cannot  digest 
veal  at  all,  and  it  causes  griping  and  diarrhoea  if  they  eat  it. 

Veal  is  best  when  the  animal  is  from  2  to  3  months  old.  The  flesh  of  the  bull-calf  is  the 
most  suitable  for  joints,  being  of  a  firmer  grain.  That  of  the  cow-calf  is  best  for  made 
dishes.  The  fillet  of  the  cow-calf  is  generally  preferred  because  it  has  the  udder.  The 
finest  calves  have  the  smallest  kidneys,  and  when  the  veal  is  good,  these  are  well  covered 
with  fat.  Although  veal  can  be  obtained  all  the  year  round,  it  is  best  from  May  to  Septem- 
ber. The  animal  should  not  be  bled  before  killing,  for,  although  the  meat  is  then  whiter, 
the  animal  is  reduced  in  vitality  and  weakened  by  the  bleeding,  and  is  in  the  condition  of  a 
diseased  animal.  When  the  flesh  is  of  a  yellowish  color,  it  is  either  from  an  animal  that  was 
ill,  or  the  meat  has  been  kept  too  long  since  slaughtering. 

Veal,  like  all  young  meat,  has  a  tendency  to  turn  very  quickly.  It  is  both  unpalatable 
and  most  unwholesome  when  it  is  at  all  tainted,  and  it  cannot  be  recovered,  as  brown 
meats  sometimes  are,  by  the  use  of  charcoal.  Therefore,  it  ought  not  to  be  kept  more  than 
2  days  in  summer  and  4  in  winter.  If  eaten  quite  fresh,  it  is  apt  to  be  a  little  tough.  To  as- 
sist it  in  keeping,  the  pipe  should  be  removed  from  the  loin  as  soon  as  it  comes  from  the 
butcher.  The  skirt  also  should  be  taken  at  once  from  the  breast,  the  inside  scraped  and 
wiped,  and  dredged  with  flour.  If  there  is  any  danger  of  the  veal  becoming  tainted,  wash 
it  and  put  it  into  boiling  water  for  10  minutes;  then  plunge  it  into  cold  water  till  cool,  wipe 
dry,  and  put  it  into  the  coolest  place  that  can  be  found. 

The  roasting  portions  are  the  fillet,  shoulder  and  loin;  also  the  best  end  of  the  neck. 
The  head  is  a  splendid  dish,  properly  managed;  the  feet  make  excellent  jelly;  both  the 
breast  and  knuckle  will  form  capital  little  affairs  on  one's  dinner  table,  as  the  former  will 
roast,  and  the  latter,  boiled  and  served  with  parsley,  is  accounted  a  delicacy  by  many. 
Veal  is  invaluable  in  the  stock  pot,  being  so  rich  and  delicate. 

Mutton. — This  should  be  of  a  rich  red  color,  close  in  the  grain  and  juicy;  the 
flesh  should  pinch  tender  and  rise  again  when  dented  with  the  finger.  The  fat 
should  be  white — whiter  than  beef  fat.  Look  at  the  vein  in  the  neck  of  the  fore- 
quarter — it  should  be  a  fine  blue.  Mutton  is  at  perfection  at  between  3  and  6 
years.  The  bones  should  be  small  and  legs  short.  The  flesh  of  wethers  is  better 
than  that  of  ewes,  as  the  latter  are  not  killed  as  long  as  they  are  profitable  for 
raising  lambs,  and  so  their  flesh  is  apt  to  be  tough  from  age. 

Mutton  is  not  so  nutritious  as  beef,  but  it  is  more  easily  digested,  and  for  this 
reason  it  is  better  for  dyspeptics.  Mutton  broth  is  very  valuable  for  persons  suf- 
fering from  dysentery  or  diarrhoea. 

All  parts  of  the  sheep  known  as  joints  are  roasted;  the  neck  and  leg  are,  however,  fre- 
quently boiled.  The  "haunch  is  a  favorite  joint  and  so  is  the  saddle,  From  the  loin,  chops 
are  cut;  cutlets  are  also  cut  from  the  thick  end,  and  sometimes  from  a  part  of  the  neck  or 
fleshy  part  of  the  leg.  In  proportion  to  the  flesh,  there  is  less  fat  and  bone  in  the  leg  than 
in  any  other  part  of  the  animal,  so  that  it  is  the  best  to  buy. 

Lamb. — In  this  the  flesh  should  be  of  a  f  aintish  white  in  color,  smooth  and 
firm  to  the  touch.  The  fat  should  be  white  and  light  in  appearance.  If  lamb 
has  a  greenish  or  yellowish  cast,  it  is  stale  and  unfit  to  eat.    If  the  veins  in  the 


28  MARKETING. 

neck-part  show  up  blue  and  prominent  in  the  lean,  it  is  an  excellent  indication 
that  the  meat  is  good.  Lamb  is  not  nearly  as  nutritious  as  mutton,  and  is  not 
suited  for  people  of  weak  digestive  power.  The  flavor  is  delicate,  which  is  its 
chief  attraction.    The  tissue  is  soft,  gelatinous  and  watery. 

Lamb  should  be  fresh,  and  not  bought  long  before  being  used.  It  is  usually  roasted, 
although  the  leg  is  often  boiled  when  in  season.  The  best  and  cheapest  part  for  stews  is  the 
breast.  If  the  flesh  of  lamb  is  pale  and  flabby,  it  is  either  from  an  unhealthy  animal,  or  it 
was  deprived  of  food  and  water  for  some  time  before  being  killed,  and  in  either  case  the 
meat  is  unwholesome.  Where  economy  is  a  consideration,  lamb  should  not  be  bought  before 
it  is  5  months  old. 

Pork. — If  the  rind  of  pork  is  tough  and  thick,  then  it  is  old;  if  thin  and 
springy,  then  it  is  young.  In  the  best  pork  the  meat  is  fine  and  close  grained, 
without  a  superabundance  of  fat.  The  lean  will  almost  break  if  pinched  between 
the  thumb  and  finger.  The  skin  can  be  indented  readily  with  the  fingers.  The 
fat  should  be  white,  smooth  and  firm.  If  the  meat  is  clammy,  or  smells  offen- 
sively, it  is  tainted  and  should  on  no  account  be  bought.  Small  kernels  in  the  fat 
indicate  disease.    Buy  good  pork  or  none  at  all. 

In  a  sucking  pig  the  tongue  should  be  clean,  the  flesh  pinkish  in  hue,  and  the 
skin  clear  and  fresh. 

Bacon  and  ham  are  more  easily  digested  than  fat  pork,  and  so  form  an  ex- 
ception to  the  rule  that  fresh  meat  can  be  digested  more  easily  than  dried.  The 
large  amount  of  fat  in  good  bacon  makes  it  a  good  heat  producer  in  cold 
weather. 

The  roasting  parts  of  the  pig  are  the  leg,  loin  and  spare  rib  (a  favorite  piece).  Although 
the  leg  can  be,  and  is  often  boiled,  the  remaining  portions  are  usually  salted,  the  leg  form- 
ing a  ham,  and  the  sides  flitches  of  bacon. 

Ham  and  Bacon. — In  selecting  a  ham,  run  a  knife  into  it  close  to  the  bone;  if 
the  blade  when  withdrawn  has  an  agreeable  odor,  the  ham  is  good;  if  it  has  a 
greasy  appearance  and  an  offensive  smell,  the  ham  is  bad.  Bacon  maybe  tested 
in  the  same  manner;  the  lean  should  be  red  and  adhere  closely  to  the  bone;  the 
fat  should  be  firm  and  pinky  white.  When  bacon  looks  yellowish,  it  is  rusty  and 
not  fit  to  use.  The  rind  should  be  thin.  Large  bacon  is  almost  always  coarse, 
and  lean  bacon  is  seldom  good. 

Sweet-breads. — In  selecting  these  for  the  table,  choose  those  which  are  white, 
firm  and  plump,  and  which  come  from  the  throat  of  the  calf.  There  is  another 
kind  which  is  really  the  pancreas,  and  which  is  only  suitable  for  chopped  dishes, 
like  croquettes,  etc. 

Tongue. — This  should  be  thick  and  firm,  and  on  the  under  side  there  should 
be  considerable  fat.  The  tongue  most  used  is  that  of  the  beef.  That  of  the 
lamb  can  be  bought  both  pickled  and  fresh,  and  that  of  the  sheep,  pig  and  calf 
can  all  be  bought;  calf's  tongue  is  usually  considered  the  best. 

Venison. — In  this,  the  fat  should  be  clear,  bright  and  thick.  If  the  cleft  of  the 
haunch  is  smooth  and  close,  it  is  young;  if  close  and  rough,  it  is  old.  It  can  be 
tested  by  the  smell,  like  ham,  by  passing  a  knife  along  the  bone  of  the  haunch  or 
shoulder.    The  flesh  will  look  darkish  if  it  is  not  new  and  sweet. 

Sausage. — Even  when  the  outside  appears  all  right,  bad  sausage  when  broken 
is  apt  to  show  small  cavities,  and  be  sticky  and  soft  inside;  if  these  signs  appear, 
or  if  there  are  yellow  or  greenish  spots,  or  if  it  tastes  rancid  or  smells  putrid,  it 
should  not  be  eaten. 

BUYING  POULTRY  AND  GAME. 

In  buying  poultry,  3  things  are  necessary — it  should  be  young,  in  good 
condition  and  fresh.  In  selecting  poultry,  choose  that  which  is  full  grown  but 
not  old.  The  safest  rule  in  choosing  poultry  is  to  feel  of  the  breast  bone;  when 
young,  it  will  be  soft  and  pliable  like  gristle;  when  old,  it  will  be  tough.  Also 
when  young  and  fresh  killed,  the  skin  is  thin  and  tender,  the  joints  not  very  stiff, 
and  the  eyes  full  and  bright.  The  freshness  of  a  bird  can  be  discovered  more  by 
smell  than  anything  else;  a  good  buyer  would  prefer  a  bird  untrussed.  A  bird 
should  be  roasted  rather  than  boiled  if  there  is  any  doubt  about  its  freshness. 
No  food  should  be  given  poultry  for  about  24  hours  before  it  is  killed,  that  there 
may  be  no  food  in  the  crop  to  sour. 

In  fowls,  the  light  meats  are  not  so  highly  flavored  as  the  dark,  nor  are  they  bo 
rich  in  nitrogen,  but  they  are  more  easily  digested. 


MARKETING.  29 

Turkeys.—  See  that  the  legs  are  smooth  and  black,  the  spurs  short,  breast  full, 
and  neck  long.  The  eyes  should  be  bright  and  full,  and  the  feet  flexible.  If  the 
eyes  are  sunk  and  the  feet  dry,  the  bird  is  stale. 

Chickens. — See  that  the  feet  are  flexible,  the  skin  soft  and  moist,  and  the  eyes 
bright  and  full.     If  the  eyes  are  sunk,  and  the  feet  dry,  it  is  stale. 

Fowls. — In  a  young  fowl  the  spurs  are  short  and  the  legs  smooth.  If  the  legs 
and  combs  are  limber,  it  is  young;  if  those  are  rough  and  stiff,  it  is  old.  When 
fresh,  the  vent  is  close  and  dark.  In  an  old  fowl  the  skin  looks  purplish,  and  the 
neck  and  feet  are  long  and  thin. 

Geese. — A  young  goose  has  a  yellowish  bill  and  flexible  feet,  with  few  hairs  on 
either.  If  the  bill  and  feet  are  reddish,  with  many  hairs,  the  bird  is  old;  if  the 
feet  are  dry,  it  is  stale. 

Ducks. — These,  both  wild  and  tame,  when  young  have  flexible  feet;  the  belly 
should  be  thick  and  firm,  and  the  skin  clear. 

Crame. — In  selecting  this,  pluck  a  few  feathers  from  the  under  part  of  the  leg; 
if  the  skin  is  not  discolored,  the  bird  is  fresh.  M.  Soyer  says,  "  The  age  may  be 
known  by  placing  the  thumb  into  the  beak,  and  holding  the  bird  up  with  the  jaw 
part;  if  that  breaks, it  is  young;  if  not,  it  is  old,  and  requires  keeping  longer  before 
cooking."  All  old  fowls  may  likewise  be  told  by  their  hard,  rough  or  dry  feet. 
Wild  game  has  a  peculiar  gamey  smell,  but  it  can  readily  be  distinguished  from 
that  of  tainted  meat. 

There  is  no  bird  whose  flesh  is  poisonous  in  itself,  or  which  may  not  in  case  of  necessity 
be  eaten.  Persons  have  been  poisoned  by  eating  quail  in  the  spring,  but  it  was  caused  by 
the  buds  of  the  mountain  laurel  which  the  bird  had  eaten. 

Partridges. — These,  if  young,  will  have  black  bills  and  yellow  legs;  if  old,  white 
bills  and  blue  legs. 

Hares  and  Babbits. — These  when  fresh  killed  are  stiff  and  whitish;  when 
stale,  the  body  is  limber  and  the.  flesh  darker.  When  young,  the  ears  will  tear 
like  brown  paper.  In  both,  the  claws  should  be  smooth  and  sharp.  In  a  young 
hare,  the  cleft  in  the  lip  is  narrow,  and  the  claws  are  readily  cracked  if  turned 
sideways. 

Squirrels. — These  are  judged  much  like  rabbits,  but  the  flesh  is  always  more 
or  less  dark;  when  young,  it  will  be  smooth  and  firm;  if  old,  it  will  be  limber  and 
flimsy. 

Killing  Fowls,  etc. — Fowls,  rabbits,  and  some  other  animals,  being  sometimes 
purchased  alive,  and  the  beauty  of  their  appearance  when  dressed  depending  in 
some  measure  on  their  being  killed  in  a  proper  manner,  it  is  desirable  that  a 
cook  should  know  how  to  kill  them  expeditiously  and  neatly. 

Kill  a  turkey  by  passing  a  small  penknife  into  the  mouth  and  cutting  it  below 
the  tongue;  then  pass  the  knife  up  through  the  head  into  the  brain.  After  this, 
hang  it  up  by  the  legs  to  let  it  bleed  freely.  All  smaller  fowls  are  killed  by 
pulling  and  dislocating  the  neck  at  the  head.  In  killing  geese  and  ducks,  com- 
mence by  tying  a  string  around  the  beak,  then  pinion  the  wings  over  the  back  to 
prevent  fluttering;  then  make  an  incision  or  cut  across  the  upper  part  of  the 
neck,  close  to  the  head,  and  suspend  by  the  feet  to  bleed  freely.  Kill  a  rabbit  by 
holding  it  by  the  hind  legs,  letting  the  head  hang  down,  and  give  it  a  smart  blow 
with  the  edge  of  the  hand  behind  the  ears.  All  birds  should  be  kept  for  a  short 
time  after  being  killed  with  their  feathers  on,  and  not  drawn  till  they  are  to  be 
used.  Furred  animals  should  not  be  skinned  until  they  are  to  be  used,  but 
should  be  paunched  as  soon  as  they  are  cold. 

BUYING  FISH  AND  SHELL-FISH. 

In  selecting  fish,  choose  those  which  are  firm  and  thick,  with  bright,  promi- 
nent eyes,  the  fins , hard  and  stiff,  the  scales  brilliant,  and  the  gills  bright  red; 
such  fish  are  fresh.  When  stale,  the  eyes  sink  and  look  flat  and  dull,  the  fins  be- 
come soft  and  flabby,  the  gills  dark,  the  scales  dim,  and  the  fish  grows  soft.  If 
you  find  the  eyes  of  a  fish  gouged  out,  be  sure  the  fish  is  stale,  and  the  dealer 
dishonest.  Dress  fish  at  once,  sprinkle  on  salt,  and  use  them  the  same  day  if 
possible;  put  them  over  ice  in  warm  weather,  or  corn  them  for  the  next  day. 
The  offensive  odor  of  stale  fish  is  one  of  the  best  tests.  All  fish  should  be  cooked 
at  once  after  being  packed  in  ice,  for  they  will  become  soft  and  lose  their  flavor 
quickly  afterwards. 


30  MARKETING. 

Occasionally  fish  are  brought  to  market,  which  are  killed  by  the  refuse  thrown  into  the 
water  by  large  manufacturing  establishments;  such  fish  will  have  a  foul  odor,  and  the 
flesh  will  be  soft,  spongy  and  yellowish.  Never  eat  fish  which  are  caught  in  putrid 
water.  If,  when  boiling  fish,  the  water  blackens  silver,  it  is  caused  by  hydrogen  sulphide 
(the  same  gas  found  in  bad  eggs).  It  comes  from  putrefaction,  and'  the  fish  should  be 
thrown  away.    A  decomposing  fish  has  a  pale  look,  and  a  bluish  belly. 

The  amount  of  nourishment  contained  in  fish  varies  with  the  species,  but  as  a  rule  they 
are  less  satisfying  and  less  stimulating  than  butcher's  meat,  though  some  of  the  red-fleshed 
fish  nearly  approach  it.  Fish  oil  is  more  digestible  than  any  other  kind  of  fat,  and  therefore 
cod-liver  oil  is  given  to  invalids.  Dr.  Pavy  says:  "The  quality  of  fish  as  an  article  of  food 
is  influenced  by  the  act  of  spawning,  and  presents  considerable  variations  at  different 
periods.  It  is  just  previous  to  spawning  that  the  animal  is  in  its  highest  state  of  perfec- 
tion. .  .  During  the  process  of  spawning,  its  store  of  fatty  matter  is  drawn  upon  and  it  be- 
comes poor,  thin,  watery  and  flabby.  It  is  now  said  to  be  'out  of  season'  and  requires  time 
to  arrive  in  condition  again.  In  fish  like  the  cod,  where  the  fatty  matter  accumulates 
specially  in  the  liver,  this  organ  presents  a  most  striking  difference  in  volume  and  condi- 
tion before  and  after  spawning;  whilst  in  such  as  the  salmon,  herring,  etc.,  where  the  fat  is 
dispersed  among  the  flesh,  it  is  the  body  which  affords  the  chief  evidence  of  change.  As 
salmon  enters  the  rivers  from  the  sea  for  the  purpose  of  depositing  its  spawn,  it  is  plump, 
and  well  provided  with  fat.  On  its  return,  the  contrast  in  its  condition  is  very  great.  It  is 
now  so  exhausted  and  thin,  as  to  be  looked  upon  as  unfit  for  food."  Fish  out  of  season  is 
never  cheap,  even  if  bought  at  a  low  price. 

Cod,  when  edible,  will  have  very  red  gills,  the  body  thick  at  the  neck,  the  flesh 
white  and  firm,  and  eyes  still  bright.  Shad,  if  good,  are  white  and  thick,  eyes 
bright,  gills  red,  body  stiff  and  firm.  In  mackerel  look  for  the  same  points,  but 
with  more  care,  as  mackerel  is  so  tender  that  it  does  not  carry  or  keep  so  well 
unsalted,  as  other  fish.  Striped  bass  depend  on  being  put  on  the  fire  as  soon  as 
possible  after  being  killed;  if  the  eyes  are  sunken  and  the  gills  pale,  they  have 
been  too  long  out  of  the  water.  Trout  should  have  the  gills  red  and  hard  to 
open,  the  eyes  lustrous,  and  the  body  stiff.  To  blue  fish  and  many  other  vari- 
eties apply  the  same  rules  as  for  striped  bass.  In  choosing  eels,  the  fat  ones  are 
best.  They  should  be  alive  just  before  being  cooked,  as  they  lose  their  sweetness 
if  long  kept.  In  selecting  sturgeon,  choose  those  which  are  white,  with  tender 
skin,  blue  veins  and  even  grain. 

Oysters,  crabs  and  all  shellfish  can  be  judged  only  by  the  smell  as  to  their 
freshness.  Oysters  should  close  firmly  on  the  knife  when  being  opened;  when 
they  can  be  opened  easily,  they  are  good  for  nothing.  The  sooner  they  are  used 
after  being  opened,  the  better. 

Lobsters,  prawns  and  shrimps  should  be  very  stiff  after  they  are  boiled,  and 
the  tails  turn  far  inward.  When  they  are  soft  and  watery,  they  are  not  fit  to  eat. 
Lobsters  should  be  alive,  or  else  boiled  before  being  offered  for  sale;  they  are 
black  when  alive,  and  red  when  boiled.  In  a  lobster,  when  fresh,  the  tail,  if 
straightened,  will  spring  back  into  position.  The  male  lobster  is  best,  unless 
required  for  sauces,  etc.;  then  the  hen  is  best,  on  account  of  the  spawn  or  coral. 
Lobsters  and  crabs,  when  good,  ought  to  be  heavy  for  their  size. 

Turtles  or  terrapin  should  be  bought  alive. 

BUYING   VEGETABLES. 

These  should  be  procured  as  fresh  as  possible,  and  the  good  housekeeper  will 
endeavor  to  keep  her  table  supplied  all  through  the  year  with  a  good  variety. 
Many  varieties,  like  potatoes,  cabbages,  etc.,  can  be  best  bought  in  the  fall  and 
stored  in  the  cellar.  Others  may  be  canned  at  home,  or  bought  in  cans  when 
needed.  Those  to  buy,  are  potatoes,  turnips,  cabbages,  squashes,  onions,  beets, 
parsnips,  carrots,  and  artichokes.  We  elsewhere  explain  about  keeping  vegeta- 
bles and  also  canning  them.  Vegetables  should  be  ripe  when  purchased,  and 
they  are  best  when  in  full  season  and  in  greatest  plenty,  so  that  they  are  within 
the  reach  of  those  of  moderate  means.  Most  early,  artificially  raised  vegetables, 
like  peas,  potatoes,  etc.,  may  be  esteemed  as  rarities;  but  they  are  less  valuable 
as  foods. 

Asparagus. — In  buying  this,  see  that  the  heads  are  full  and  green;  when  the 
head  is  not  full,  and  the  color  is  light,  it  is  not  desirable. 

Beans. — Shell  beans  are  dry  and  smooth  when  fresh;  if  they  appear  sticky 
and  dull,  they  are  old.  String  beans  should  be  green,  break  crisply,  and  the 
bean  should  be  justbeginningto  form  in  the  pod;  if  they  look  yellow  or  wilted, 
they  are  not  fresh,  nor  desirable. 

Brussels  Sprouts. — Select  those  which  are  fresh  and  crisp,  and  which  are  com- 


MARKETING.  31 

pact  and  firm,  like  cabbage  heads;  loose  heads  are  undesirable.  Strip  off  the 
outer  leaves  and  soak  in  cold,  salted  water  an  hour  or  so  before  cooking. 

Cauliflower. — In  buying  this,  see  that  the  leaves  are  green  and  fresh,  and  the 
head  a  whitish  cream  color.  If  there  are  dark  spots  on  the  head,  and  the  leaves 
are  wilted,  it  is  bad. 

Celery. — See  that  the  stalks  break  off  crisply,  and  that  the  heads  are  solid  and 
white.  It  is  apt  to  be  tough  and  stringy  after  spring  opens,  and  bitter  before  the 
coming  of  frost.     It  is  best  during  the  winter. 

Corn. — Green  corn  should  be  milky  and  tender,  and  the  ears  should  be  well 
filled  out;  if  it  is  hard,  and  the  kernels  yellowish  or  straw  color,  it  is  too  old,  and 
even  long  boiling  will  not  make  it  good. 

Cucumbers. — If  these  are  intended  for  salad,  see  that  they  are  firm  and  green; 
if  for  pickling,  have  them  small;  (they  are  then  called  gherkins);  if  for  cooking, 
they  may  be  large  and  yellowed  a  little  without  harm. 

Dandelions. — The  cultivated  are  better  than  the  wild;  those  are  good  in  the 
spring,  but  are  apt  to  be  tough  by  the  last  of  May. 

Egg-Plant.  Buy  that  which  is  not  quite  ripe  and  which  is  firm.  The  best  is 
oval  in  shape,  large  and  purple. 

Lettuce. — In  buying  this,  see  that  the  leaves  are  green,  fresh  and  crisp;  keep  it 
until  used  in  a  refrigerator,  on  ice  if  possible,  or  in  a  cold  place  at  least. 

Mushrooms. — These  are  best  bought  from  reliable  dealers  only.  In  gather- 
ing them,  they  should  be  carefully  distinguished  from  poisonous  toadstools;  con- 
founding the  two  has  caused  many  deaths. 

Okra. — See  that  the  pods  are  green  and  crisp.  Although  its  use  was  formerly 
confined  mainly  to  the  South,  it  is  becoming  more  popular  at  the  North  every 
year.    It  can  be  purchased  nearly  all  the  year  round. 

Peas. — In  buying  these,  see  that  the  pods  feel  cool  and  dry  and  look  green.  If 
the  pods  have  turned  light  or  are  spotted,  they  are  old  or  not  freshly  picked,  and 
are  not  good. 

Potatoes  having  large  deep  eyes  cut  to  waste.  A  rough  skin  is  said  to  indi- 
cate a  mealy  potato,  and  a  smooth  skin  a  waxy  one,  but  that  is  not  a  sure  guide, 
and  the  best  way  is  to  boil  a  sample  and  watch  the  result.  Small  potatoes  are 
seldom  economical,  even  at  a  low  price,  the  waste  in  peeling  being  so  great. 
Medium  sized  potatoes  are  generally  the  best  to  buy,  choosing  in  preference 
those  with  small  eyes.  Small  potatoes,  after  being  well  washed,  may  be  boiled 
with  the  skins  on,  and  peeled  when  cold  for  hash. 

Spinach. — In  buying  this,  see  that  it  is  green  in  color  and  crisp. 

Tomatoes. — For  table  use,  get  those  which  are  red  and  ripe,  smooth,  medium- 
sized  ones  being  best;  for  pickling,  green  ones  may  be  bought. 

Sweet  Herbs.  The  good  housewife  will  find  it  convenient  to  keep  on  hand 
such  herbs  as  thyme,  sweet  marjoram  bay  leaves  and  sage.  A  few  cents  will  buy 
a  bunch  of  each,  and  they  should  be  kept  dry  and  tied  together,  and  in  a  box  or 
paper  bag.  Parsley  and  mint  are  used  green.  The  former  is  easily  raised  where 
it  cannot  be  readily  bought. 

BUYING  GROCERIES,  ETC. 

Butter. — In  buying  butter,  taste  it  and  smell  it.  If  streaky  or  spotted,  or 
with  a  reddish,  speckly  look,  it  is  not  good,  and  when  cut  with  a  knife,  should  it 
appear  milky,  it  is  a  sign  that  it  will  not  keep  good  long,  for  it  has  not  been 
washed  properly  and  it  will  waste.  If  it  smells  sour,  it  has  not  been  sufficiently 
washed  from  the  buttermilk;  if  it  is  veiny  or  open,  it  has  probably  had  an  in- 
ferior kind  worked  in.  To  choose  salt  butter,  plunge  a  knife  into  it;  if,  when 
drawn  out,  the  blade  smells  rancid  or  unpleasant,  the  butter  is  bad. 

Milk. — In  our  article  on  the  adulteration  of  food,  we  speak  of  the  tests  for 
milk.  Where  a  supply  of  really  pure  and  fresh  milk  is  not  available,  the  preserved 
milk  will  be  invaluable.  It  is  made  by  evaporating  nearly  the  whole  of  the  water 
in  the  milk,  from  which  it  is  prepared,  and  adding  sugar. 

Cheese. — Select  that,  which,  when  rubbed  between  the  fingers,  melts  and 
seems  smooth.  Poor  cheese  is  crumbly  and  dry.  Prof.  Vaughn  of  Ann  Arbor, 
Mich.,  says  that  cheese  which  will  instantly  and  intensely  redden  blue  litmus 
paper,  should  never  be  eaten.  The  test  is  easily  made;  if  the  cheese  is  dry,  a  bit 
of  it  should  be  moistened,  and  the  litmus  paper  then  applied. 


32  MARKETING. 

Eggs. — In  choosing  eggs,  apply  the  tongue  to  the  large  end;  if  it  feels  warm, 
the  egg  is  fresh.  Another  simple  test  for  eggs  is  to  thoroughly  dissolve  2  oz.  of 
salt  in  1  pint  of  water.  An  egg,  dropped  into  this,  if  freshly  laid,  goes  to  the 
bottom;  if  1  day  old,  it  rises  a  little;  if  3  days  old,  it  floats  in  the  liquid;  if  more 
than  5  days  old,  it  projects  above  the  surface,  projecting  more  and  more  as  by 
increased  age  the  water  inside  evaporates  through  the  pores  of  the  shell,  and  air 
enters  and  takes  its  place.  Before  breaking  an  egg,  hold  it  before  a  gas  or  candle 
flame  and  try  and  look  through  it;  if  clear,  it  is  fresh;  if  cloudy,  it  is  stale;  if  any 
dark  spots  appear,  it  is  bad.  No  egg  should  be  used  for  culinary  purposes  with 
the  slightest  taint  about  it. 

Coffee  and  Tea. — These  are  best  bought  in  considerable  quantities,  as  a  saving 
of  several  cents  a  pound  may  be  obtained,  if  a  half  chest  of  tea  or  a  bag  of  coffee 
is  bought  at  a  time.  (See  what  we  say  about  their  adulteration  in  our  article  on 
theAdulterationof  Foods.) 

Barley. — Barley  flower  does  not  keep  well;  it  is  apt  to  acquire  a  hot,  unpleas- 
ant taste.     Buy  little  at  a  time,  and  keep  in  a  cool,  dry  place. 

Barley  is  a  native  of  Western  Asia,  and  has  been  cultivated  from  remote  antiquity.  It 
is  hardy  and  can  be  cultivated  over  a  very  wide  range  of  climate.  It  has  too  little  gluten 
to  make  good  bread.  It  is  rich  in  phosphates,  and  though  less  nutritious  than  wheat,  it  is 
lighter  and  less  stimulating,  and  in  the  form  of  gruel  makes  a  soothing  drink  for  invalids. 
It  is  rich  in  starch  and  contains  some  mucilage.  Pearl  barley  is  barley  steamed,  and  the 
fibrous  coats  removed  and  the  grains  rounded  and  polished.  Patent  barley  is  pearl  barley 
ground  to  flour. 

Buckwheat  Meal. — A  small  quantity  of  this  at  a  time  is  enough  to  buy,  as  it 
does  not  keep  very  well. 

Buckwheat  is  a  native  of  Persia,  and  is  killed  by  frost.  It  is  rich  in  starch  and  albumen, 
and  its  ash  is  rich  in  potash  and  lime.  The  pancakes  are  not  suited  to  those  of  weak  diges- 
tion. Their  continuous  use  gives  rise  to  an  itching  of  the  skin  and  an  eruption,  the  only 
specific  for  which,  is  to  stop  eating  them  for  a  time.  The  albumenous  substances  found  in 
buckwheat  differ  materially  from  those  found  in  the  cereals,  and  its  food  value  is  less  than 
that  of  wheat,  oats  or  rye. 

Corn  or  Indian  Meal. — The  common  kind  is  best  bought  in  small  quantities 
as  it  does  not  keep  well;  the  granulated,  however,  will  keep  for  any  length  of 
time,  and  will  be  preferred  to  the  other  if  once  used.  When  decomposition  be- 
gins in  cornmeal,  it  has  a  rancid  odor,  and  a  little  of  it  placed  on  moistened  blue 
litmus  paper  (to  be  had  at  any  drug  store)  will  turn  it  red,  while  good  meal  does 
not  affect  the  color. 

The  fineness  of  grinding  cornmeal  affects  the  bread  made  from  it,  fine  meal  making  a 
compact,  and  the  coarse  a  more  open  and  spongy  bread.  Cornmeal  should  always  be  thor- 
oughly cooked  before  being  eaten;  it  is  owing  to  its  improper  preparation  that  its  reputation 
for  indigestibility  is  due.  It  should  be  perfectly  sweet  and  dry,  and  if  at  all  "heated"  or 
musty,  it  is  entirely  unfit  for  human  consumption.  Buy  it  in  small  quantities,  and  keep  it 
in  closed  tins  or  a  tightly  closing  jar,  and  in  a  dry  place.  For  further  particulars  regard- 
ing corn,  see  "Vegetables."  Samp  is  the  whole  grains  hulled.  Hominy  is  corn  hulled  and 
coarsely  broken. 

Cracked  Wheat. — This  will  keep  a  long  time.  The  crushed  cooks  the  quickest, 
but  the  cut  looks  best  when  served. 

Flour. — In  buying  this,  the  best  is  the  cheapest.  It  is  best  to  buy  at  least  a 
barrel  at  a  time,  as  it  is  not  economy  to  buy  in  small  quantities.  There  are  many 
brands  equally  good.  The  amount  of  water  which  flour  will  absorb  is  a  far  bet- 
ter test  of  its  quality  than  its  color,  because  flour  containing  an  abundance  of 
gluten  will  absorb  more  water  than  that  which  contains  a  larger  amount  of 
starch;  such  flour  will  make  more  bread,  and  is  therefore  better  to  buy.  Gluten  is 
a  tenacious,  colorless  or  greyish  substance,  and  was  known  in  ancient  times  as 
birdlime.  Starchy  flour  lacks  adhesive  properties;  it  crumbles  in  the  hand,  and 
may  be  blown  about  easily,  while  that  containing  a  good  amount  of  gluten,  when 
pressed  in  the  hand,  remains  compact  and  retains  the  imprint  of  the  lines  of  the 
skin,  while  it  has  a  creamy  white  tint.  Don't  buy  flour  of  a  bluish  white  tinge. 
A  good  test  for  flour  is  to  put  some  in  the  hollow  of  the  hand,  make  a  hole  in  the 
center,  and  add  enough  cold  water  to  form  a  soft  dough;  if  the  flour  is  of  good 
quality  and  fresh,  the  dough  when  pulled  out  will  not  break  easily,  but  will  be 
tenacious,  will  harden  in  the  air,  and  will  so  remain;  if,  however,  on  working  the 
dough,  it  sticks  to  the  fingers,  softens,  and  on  being  pulled  out,  is  brittle  and 
breaks  off  short,  the  quality  is  poor.  If  there  is  any  strong  or  unpleasant  odor,  it 
has  begun  to  spoil  and  is  worthless.    Flour  rich  in  gluten  takes  longer  to  rise 


MARKETING.  33 

than  that  containing  more  starch,  but  it  makes  better  bread.    That  containing 
more  starch  can  be  used  for  pastry. 

Wheat  flour  is  preferable  to  any  of  the  other  great  vegetable  products  on  which  men 
chiefly  live,  since  it  is  a  far  more  agreeable  food  than  maize  (Indian  corn)  and  far  more  nu- 
tritious than  rice.  It  is  probable  that  the  health  and  bodily  vigor  of  the  inhabitants  of 
temperate  climates  are  largely  attributable  to  the  extensive  use  of  this  food.  Wheat 
flour  contains  the  following  substances:  starch,  dextrin,  cellulose,  sugar,  albumen,  gliadin 
or  gluten,  mucin,  fibrin,  cerealin,  fat,  mineral  matters  and  water.  The  first  4  substances 
(the  carbohydrates)  form  nearly  %  of  the  entire  weight  of  the  flour.  The  gluten  and  other 
albumenoids  form  from  10  to  15$,  and  the  oils  about  1%.  The  outer  covering  of  wheat  is  a 
layer  of  almost  pure  silica,  which  is  perfectly  indigestible.  In  the  inside  layers  of  the  cov- 
ering are  found  cerealin  and  gluten,  and  the  starch  and  some  gluten  are  found  in  the  center. 
The  composition  and  value  of  the  bran  is  now  better  understood  than  formerly.  Coarse 
bran  containing  the  outer  layer  of  silica  is  valueless.  This  silica  is  indigestible  and 
remains  as  a  foreign  body  in  the  bowels,  causing  irritation  and  diarrhoea,  and  so  hurries 
the  food  along  the  alimentary  canal  before  the  nutritive  elements  can  be  absorbed,  while 
fine  flour  is  nearly  all  assimilated.  The  inner  layers  of  the  sheath  contain  gluten,  some 
starch,  and  the  cerealin  which  is  an  aid  to  digestion,  as  it  acts  like  disatase  in  the  conver- 
sion of  starchy  food  into  sugar.  The  finest  white  flour  is  derived  chiefly  from  the  central 
parts  of  the  kernel,  whilst  all  the  succeeding  portions  vary  in  color  according  to  the  de- 
grees of  admixture  of  the  different  layers  of  the  skin.  Good  seconds  flour  is  doubtless  the 
most  nutritious  and  cheapest  to  buy,  although  it  does  not  produce  the  whitest  bread;  but 
to  sacrifice  nourishment  to  mere  appearance  is  not  wise.  For  the  poor,  it  is  the  most  eco- 
nomical. 

Pastry  flour,  as  it  is  sometimes  called,  is  also  known  as  old  process  or  St.  Louis  flour.  It  is 
highly  sifted  and  contains  more  stai'ch  and  less  gluten  than  the  "seconds,"  but  it  makes 
excellent  pastry  and  cake.  Although  bread  made  from  it  contains  less  of  the  gluten 
(which  is  the  principal  albumenoid  element  of  flour)  than  is  obtained  from  the  other  flour, 
that  defect  can  be  remedied  by  obtaining  more  of  the  albumenoid  element  in  some  other 
food;  but  many  families  will  find  it  best  to  have  some  of  both  kinds  of  flour  on  hand,  one 
for  bread,  and  the  other  for  cake  and  pastry. 

Semolina  (also  called  Semou  le  or  Semola).— This  consists  of  particles  of  wheatwhich, in- 
stead of  being  ground  fine  like  flour,  is  reduced  to  grains,  varying  in  size  from  grains  of  sand 
to  small  millet.  As  the  gluten  is  retained  it  is  nutritious.  It  is  used  for  making  puddings, 
thickening  soups,  etc.  It  is  more  used  in  France  than  in  any  other  country,  and  in  Paris  a 
kind  of  bread  is  made  of  the  coarser  kinds  and  called  gruau. 

Graham. — A  good  deal  that  is  sold  is  made  from  poor  flour  mixed  with  bran; 
the  best  is  made  from  good  wheat  without  being  sifted.  Keep  it  in  a  dry,  cool 
place  and  it  will  keep  for  years. 

The  old  idea  that  graham  flour  is  good  for  those  suffering  from  constipation  is  no  longer 
entertained.  The  laxative  quality  is  caused  purely  by  the  mechanical  irritation  of  the  in- 
testines, caused  by  the  smal  1  particles  of  silica  (really  like  powdered  glass)  contained  in  the 
outer  covering  of  the  wheat.  The  irritation  thus  caused  is  sometimes  dangerous.  If  a 
cathartic  is  needed,  powdered  glass  is  not  the  best  thing  to  use.  The  bowel  thus  irritated, 
rushes  the  food  along  and  finally  ejects  it  before  all  its  nutriment  is  assimilated,  so  that 
much  is  wasted.  The  poorer  grades  of  graham  flour  are  not  fit  to  use.  A  moderate  amount 
of  the  best  grades  may  be  used,  but  is  not  suited  for  a  steady  diet. 

Oatmeal. — It  is  best  to  have  this  as  fresh  as  possible,  so  purchase  little  at  a 
time.    The  coarser  keeps  better  than  the  finely  ground. 

Oats  grow  better  in  cool,  moist  climates  than  in  those  which  are  hot  and  dry,  and  there- 
fore can  be  cultivated  in  situations  where  neither  barley  nor  wheat  can  be  raised.  The 
meal  is  less  white  than  wheat  flour,  and  is  chiefly  eaten  in  the  form  of  porridge.  Owing  to 
the  absence  of  gluten,  oatmeal  cannot,  like  wheat  flour,  be  made  into  light,  porous  bread. 
In  many  places,  notably  in  Scotland,  oatmeal  forms  an  important  food  for  the  people,  and 
it  admittedly  produces  a  healthy  and  vigorous  race  of  men.  The  peculiar  value  of  oatmeal 
is  owing  to  the  large  amount  of  fat  in  oats.  It  is  healthy  and  nutritious,  and  merits  an  ex- 
tensive use,  especially  among  working  people  and  children,  but  some  people  with  weak  di- 
gestive powers  find  it  irritating. 

Groats  or  grits  are  oats  deprived  of  their  outer  husks.  Embden  Groats  are 
the  same,  broken  into  smaller  pieces. 

Ryemeal. — As  this  does  not  keep  well,  it  is  best  to  purchase  it  in  small  quan- 
tities.   For  bread  and  muffins  the  meal  is  more  desirable  than  the  flour. 

The  native  country  of  rye  is  unknown.  Rye  flour  is  little  used  now  for  making  bread, 
except  when  mixed  with  wheat  flour.  It  is  inferior  to  wheat  flour  in  nutritive  properties, 
flavor  and  digestibility. 

Hominy. — -The  highest  grade  is  the  best,  and  it  will  keep  a  long  time. 

Macaroni. — This,  when  good,  has  a  yellowish  tinge,  and  when  cooked,  it  does 
not  break  or  burst. 

Lard. — See  that  this  is  white,  free  from  any  unpleasant  smell,  and  solid. 
Leaf  lard  is  best,  and  is  made  from  the  leaf  fat  taken  from  the  ribs  of  the  hog. 

Arrowroot. — This  is  found  in  perfectly  white  lumps  when  pure,  and  they  can 
be  crushed  readily  between  the  fingers.  If,  when  boiled  with  water  and  con- 
stantly stirred,  a  foam  appears  on  top,  it  has  been  adulterated  with  flour;  if  pure, 
there  will  be  no  foam. 


34  MARKETING. 

Almonds. — The  best  are  long  and  oval-shaped;  the  more  common  are  round 
and  flat. 

The  almond  is  the  seed  or  kernel  of  the  almond  tree,  which  resembles  the  peach,  and  is 
indigenous  to  Persia,  Syria  and  the  north  of  Africa.  The  2  most  important  kinds  are  the 
sweet  and  bitter.  The  bitter  are  used  to  flavor  confectionery,  etc.,  but  they  contain  much 
prussic  acid,  and  a  single  drop  of  the  essential  oil  will  kill  a  bird,  and  4  drops  a  dog;  these 
have  little  domestic  use.  The  sweet  are  the  only  ones  adapted  for  ordinary  use.  Although 
Considered  nourishing,  they  frequently  disagree  with  persons  of  weak  digestion,  the  husks 
being  especially  indigestible,  so  that  they  should  always  be  blanched.  Eaten  in  large 
Quantities,  they  are  purgative. 

Cocoanut. — As  there  is  danger  in  the  summer  that  the  heat  will  sour  the 
milk,  these  should  be  purchased  carefully. 

Pistachio  nuts  are  the  fruit  of  a  tree  growing  to  the  height  of  15  or  20  feet. 
The  tree  came  originally  from  Asia,  but  now  grows  plentifully  in  the  southern 
parts  of  Europe.  The  nuts  need  to  be  blanched  like  almonds.  They  are  costly, 
and  as  they  much  resemble  the  almond  in  flavor,  the  latter  is  often  substituted 
for  them  in  flavorings,  etc. 

Nutmegs. — Prick  these  with  a  pin;  if  oil  runs  out,  they  are  good. 

Nutmeg  is  the  kernel  of  the  fruit  of  an  evergreen  tree  now  cultivated  in  the  West  India 
Islands,  etc.  It  contains  about  6%  of  a  pungent  essential  oil.  It  is  aromatic,  stimulant,  and 
contains  narcotic  properties. 

Raisins. — The  best  way  is  to  buy  a  small  box  in  families  where  they  are  much 
used,  but  they  do  not  improve  with  age.  For  puddings  and  fine  cakes  the 
"seedless"  are  preferred,  but  the  "Valencia"  are  used  more  generally  because 
cheaper.    Loose  "Muscatels"  and  layer  raisins  are  the  best  for  table  use. 

Raisins  are  dried  grapes.  There  are  2  ways  of  preparing  them.  One  way  is  to  partially 
cut  through  the  stalk,  and  then  let  the  fruit  shrink  and  dry  on  the  vine,  by  the  heat  of  the 
sun.  The  other  method  is  to  dip  them  while  yet  on  the  vines  into  a  hot  solution  of  alkali, 
derived  from  wood  ashes,  to  which  olive  oil  and  salt  are  added.  The  oil  causes  them  to 
shrink  and  wrinkle,  and  the  alkali  removes  the  waxy  coat  which  impedes  their  drying. 
They  are  then  dried  in  the  sun  in  bunches,  on  lines  or  prepared  floors.  Muscatels  are  pre- 
pared by  the  first  method,  and  are  sometimes  called  "  raisins  of  the  sun."  Raisins  contain 
more  sugar  and  less  acid  than  gnapes,  and  are  therefore  more  nutritious,  but  eaten  to  ex- 
cess they  are  apt  to  derange  the  digestive  organs. 

Rice. — The  southern  is  better  than  the  Indian  rice,  and  cooks  more  quickly. 
It  should  be  examined  occasionally  to  see  if  insects  are  getting  into  it.  That 
which  swells  most  in  cooking  is  best. 

Salt. — This  if  good  should  be  dry,  white,  fine  and  perfectly  soluble  in  water. 
Lime  and  magnesium  are  found  in  the  coarser  kinds;  the  color  is  dark  often,  and 
it  absorbs  moisture  from  the  air.  Chloride  of  magnesium  makes  it  absorb  mois- 
ture, and  gives  it  the  bitter  taste  sometimes  noticed.  If  adulterated,  it  is  usually 
with  gypsum  (sulphate  of  lime),  which  gives  it  a  dull  look,  and  is  not  readily 
soluble  in  hot  water. 

Spices. — These  are  best  bought  in  small  quantities,  but  a  little  of  all  kinds 
should  be  kept  on  hand.     See  what  we  say  elsewhere  about  their  adulteration. 

Mixed  Spices.— Take  2  even  teaspoons  of  allspice,  2  heaping  teaspoons  of  cloves,  2  heap- 
ing teaspoons  of  mace,  and  6  heaping  teaspoons  of  ground  cinnamon,  and  thoroughly  mix; 
use  for  puddings  or  dark  cakes. 

It  is  difficult  to  draw  a  very  distinct  line  between  spices  and  condiments,  but  spices  are 
generally  added  to  articles  of  food  containing  sugar,  while  condiments  are  eaten  with  meat, 
and  generally  with  any  foods  which  contain  common  salt.  Cloves  and  allspice  are,  how- 
ever, eaten  with  meat,  and  cinnamon,  nutmeg  and  mace  with  sweet  foods.  Spices  can  be 
used  in  moderation,  but  if  used  in  excess,  they  irritate  the  mucous  membrane  and  are  harm- 
ful, often  causing  dyspepsia. 

Allspice  is  the  popular  name  given  to  Pimento  or  Jamaica  pepper.  It  is  the  berry  of 
a  tree  (the  Pimento,  Officinalis)  which  grows  in  the  West  Indies  and  South  America,  and  at- 
tains a  height  of  15  to  20  feet.  The  berries  are  picked  green  and  dried  in  the  sun,  when  they 
become  black.  Pimento  contains  about  4%  of  an  aromatic  pungent  oil  much  like  cloves.  It 
is  a  mild,  agreeable  and  innocent  spice. 

Angelica  is  an  aromatic  herb.  Its  roots  are  used  as  food  in  Norway.  The  stems, 
boiled  in  syrup,  yield  a  sweetmeat.  The  seeds  are  dried  and  used  for  flavoring  some 
cordials. 

Caraway.— These  seeds  are  the  fruit  of  a  biennial  plant  something  like  a  carrot.  The 
root  is  sometimes  used  like  the  carrot,  but  is  a  little  too  strong.  The  seeds  contain  an  es- 
sential oil,  and  are  used  to  flavor  cookies,  etc. 

Cassia  is  the  dried  bark  of  a  Chinese  species  of  cinnamon,  and  "Cassia  Buds"  are  the 
unripe  fruits  of  the  same  tree. 

Cinnamon.— This  is  the  inner  bark  of  a  tree,  a  species  of  the  laurel  family  which  grows 
to  the  height  of  20  or  30  feet,  and  is  found  in  Ceylon,  Java,  etc.  "  Clove  Oil"  is  dis- 
tilled from  the  leaf.  Cinnamon  contains  a  fragrant  essential  oil.  It  is  best  to  buy  it  in  the 
stick  or  roll;  pound  it  in  a  mortar,  and  sift  it  fine  for  use. 

Cloves  are  the  dried  flower  buds  of  the  clove  tree,  an  evergreen  tree  belonging  to  the 


MARKETING.  35 

myrtle  order.  Our  supplies  come  from  Zanzibar  and  the  West  Indies.  They  contain  a  con- 
siderable quantity  of  a  pungent  oil  which  is  aromatic  and  stimulapt. 

Coriander  Seeds  come  from  an  umbelliferous  annual  plant  found  in  Europe.  It  contains 
a  little  essential  oil. 

Ginger  is  the  root  of  a  plant  deriving  its  name  from  the  mountain  Ginger,  in  Hindoostan, 
of  which  it  is  a  native.  It  now  comes  chiefly  from  the  East  and  West  Indies.  It  is  dug  up  in 
January  or  February,  scraped,  washed  and  carefully  dried.  It  contains  starch,  gum,  an 
acid  resin  which  gives  its  hot  taste,  and  a  volatile  oil  which  gives  its  odor.  The  best  comes 
from  Jamaica.  There  are  2  kinds— white  and  black ;  white  is  considered  superior.  It  is  aro- 
matic, stimulant,  and  in  hot  weather  is  valuable  as  a  medicine.  It  is  less  heating  to  the 
system  than  might  be  expected  from  its  taste. 

Mace  is  the  membrane  which  surrounds  the  nutmeg.  It  contains  about  ilA%  of  an  aro- 
matic oil,  and  its  general  qualities  are  similar  to  the  nutmeg. 

Pepper.— Of  this  aromatic  berry  or  fruit  there  are  several  kinds.  The  black  is  derived 
from  berries  growing  on  vines  something  like  grapes.  It  grows  in  Malabar  and  other  places. 
Each  berry  contains  a  seed  of  a  brownish  color,  which  is  black  when  dried,  and  is  the  pep- 
per of  commerce.  White  pepper  is  made  from  black  by  steeping  the  seeds  in  lime  water  and 
then  rubbing  off  the  coats,  and  is  therefore  less  pungent  than  the  black.  Black  pepper  con- 
tains a  concrete  acrid  oil,  a  volatile  oil,  starch,  malic  and  uric  acids  and  lignine.  Small 
quantities  are  stimulant,  but  large  quantities  are  irritating.  Even  small  quantities  pro- 
duce detrimental  effects  on  inflammatory  constitutions.  Cayenne  pepper  is  made  from  the 
dried  pods  and  seeds  of  various  species  of  capsicum.  It  is  stimulating,  and  though  not  used 
as  much  as  black  pepper,  is  more  wholesome.  Chillies  is  a  Mexican  name  signifying  pods. 
When  ripe,  the  pods  are  dried  in  the  sun,  mixed  with  a  little  salt,  and  kept  in  closely  stop- 
pered bottles  for  use. 

Starch. — The  best  is  the  cheapest,  and  in  a  large  family  a  saving  may  be  made 
in  buying  by  the  quantity. 

Sugar. — The  granulated  sugar  is  less  apt  to  be  adulterated  than  the  moist 
brown  sugar.  It  is  usually  best  to  keep  some  of  the  granulated,  powdered  and 
brown  on  hand,  using  each  for  the  purpose  for  which  it  is  best  adapted.  The 
margin  of  profit  on  sugars  is  small,,  and  little  is  saved  by  buying  in  large  quan- 
tities. 

Vanilla  Beans. — The  best  come  from  Mexico.  They  should  be  fresh,  pliable 
enough  to  wind  around  one's  finger,  and  oily.  Do  not  buy  those  which  are  dry 
and  brittle,  or  which  have  been  split  open,  as  that  is  sometimes  done,  and  part  of 
the  seeds  extracted.  If  they  are  covered  with  fine  needle-like  crystals,  looking 
much  like  hoar  frost,  they  are  of  the  best  quality,  and  rich  with  aroma. 

The  vanilla  bean  is  the  fruit  of  a  plant  (an  orchid)  indigenous  to  Mexico,  Peru,  Brazil, 
etc.  The  fruit  is  cylindrical  in  shape  and  is  filled  with  small,  black,  oily  seeds.  Its  odor  is 
due  to  the  presence  of  benzoic  acid,  and  is  so  powerful  that  it  is  said  to  intoxicate  those  who 
climb  the  trees  to  gather  it.  Vanilla  is  not  suitable  for  flavoring  food  for  invalids  because 
of  its  medicinal  qualities.  It  is  a  gentle  stimulant  and  promotes  digestion.  The  Mexican 
beans  are  the  best. 

The  tonka  beans  are  the  seeds  of  the  Dipterus  odorata,  which  is  a  native  of  Guiana.  The 
odor  comes  from  a  volatile  oil  which  they  contain. 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS. 

"Economy  of  food  is  the  wealth  of  Nations." 

By  properly  caring  for  the  provisions  which  are  bought,  a  considerable  saving 
can  be  made  in  the  housekeeping  expenses,  and  every  housekeeper  should  un- 
derstand the  best  methods  of  keeping  the  various  articles  of  food,  and  should 
make  arrangements  for  taking  care  of  them.  A  good  cellar  and  store  room  are 
among  the  most  important  rooms  in  the  house,  and  no  better  investment  can  be 
made  than  to  fit  up  such  rooms  suitably.  Those  who  are  not  fortunate  enough 
to  have  rooms  of  this  kind  such  as  they  would  like,  can  usually  fit  up  some  sub- 
stitute by  exercising  a  little  ingenuity.  In  the  absence  of  proper  places  for 
keeping  provisions,  a  hanging  safe,  suspended  in  an  airy  situation,  is  the  best 
substitute.  Carelessness  in  the  use  and  care  of  the  provisions  marks  the  poor 
and  shiftless  housekeeper. 

The  larder  should  be  cool  and  dry,  with  the  outer  air  playing  freely  through 
it,  and  it  should  be  in  the  shade — if  possible  have  it  face  due  north,  so  that  the 
sun  never  comes  in.  It  should  be  impervious  to  vermin  and  insects,  especially 
flies.  A  few  shelves,  a  few  strong  iron  hooks  on  which  to  hang  meat,  and  some 
wire  covers  for  cold  meat,  are  among  the  principal  requisites. 

MEAT,  GAME  AND  FISH. 

Meat  requires  a  dry,  airy  place,  and  should  always  be  hung  up,  not  laid  on  a 
plate  or  dish,  and  before  being  hung  up  it  should  be  wiped.  The  most  favorable 
weather  for  keeping  meat  is  when  it  is  cool,  clear  and  dry.  When  the  atmosphere 
is  thick  and  moist,  meat  will  soon  taint.  Keeping  meats  for  a  few  days  in  a  cool 
place  improves  them,  except  veal,  lamb  and  pork.  In  most  cases  fresh  meat  is 
cooked  too  soon  after  being  killed,  which  arises,  perhaps,  from  the  general  want 
of  proper  larders,  and  the  dread  of  the  meat  being  spoiled.  The  consequence  is, 
that,  instead  of  being  palatable  and  tender,  the  meat  is  tough  and  disagreeable, 
and  not  so  nutritious  or  easily  digested  as  it  ought  to  be.  But,  although  it  is  ad- 
visable that  animal  food  should  be  hung  in  the  open  air  until  its  fibres  have  lost 
some  of  their  toughness,  yet,  if  it  is  kept  till  it  loses  its  natural  sweetness,  its 
flavor  becomes  deteriorated.  As  soon  as  the  slightest  trace  of  putrescence  is  de- 
tected, it  has  reached  its  highest  degree  of  tenderness,  and  should  be  dressed  at 
once.  Meat  is  frequently  cut  from  an  animal  and  cooked  at  once  after  death,  in 
warm  climates,  and  before  what  is  called  rigor  mortis  (the  stiffness  of  death]  sets 
in.  Meat  is  harder  to  masticate  and  digest  during  this  rigor,  which  comes  on 
soon  after  death,  and  the  object  in  "keeping"  the  meat,  as  is  done  in  temperate 
climates,  is  to  let  this  rigor  pass  off,  as  it  is  then  better  for  eating;  pounding  the 
meat,  after  cutting  it  in  thin  slices,  hastens  the  process.  Domestic  fowls,  wild 
game  frequently,  frogs,  fish  and  some  mollusks,  are  about  the  only  animals  cooked 
in  this  climate  before  this  rigor  comes  on. 

Meat  may  be  kept  fresh  for  5  or  6  days  in  the  hottest  weather  by  putting  it  in 
a  stone  crock,  and  completely  covering  it  with  skimmed  milk,  just  turning  sour 
or  getting  thick;  but  the  liquid  must  fully  cover  the  meat,  and  it  should  be  kept 
in  as  cool  a  place  as  convenient;  then  wash  it  well  before  cooking  it. 

On  buying  meat  in  hot  weather,  if  you  have  no  refrigerator,  wipe  it  dry,  dredge 
it  with  flour  and  pepper,  and  hang  it  in  the  larder;  these  can  easily  be  washed  off 
before  it  is  cooked.  Or  it  may  be  prepared  as  above,  wrapped  in  a  dry  cloth, 
and  covered  with  charcoal  or  ashes.  To  keep  it  from  tainting,  charcoal  is  about 
the  best  thing. 

Should  meat  be  tainted  at  all,  it  is  best  to  wash  it  with  vinegar  and  water,  or 
powder  it  with  charcoal,  and  then  wash  it  off  with  water;  it  will  afterwards  be  bet- 
ter roasted  than  boiled.    The  habit  which  many  people  have  of  putting  meat  di- 

(36) 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS.  3? 

tectly  on  the  ice,  in  its  wrapping  paper,  is  a  very  bad  one;  the  juices  are  drawn 
out  by  the  water.  It  is  not  the  ice  but  the  cold  air  which  checks  decay,  so  that 
meat,  etc.,  should  be  placed  near  the  ice  but  not  on  it.  After  keeping  meat  on
