ice,  it  should  be  cooked  immediately  on  taking  it  off.  When  it  is  seen  that  meat 
will  not  keep  long,  it  is  better  to  parboil  or  half  roast  it  at  once;  after  which,  when 
it  is  cooked,  it  must  be  plunged  into  boiling  water,  if  to  be  boiled;  or,  for  roast- 
ing, put  it  before  a  very  hot  fire  to  start.  During  the  summer  months  it  is  diffi- 
cult to  procure  meat  that  is  not  either  tough  or  tainted;  it  should,  therefore,  be 
examined  when  it  comes  in,  and  if  flies  have  touched  it,  the  part  must  be  cut  off 
and  the  remainder  well  washed.  To  protect  meat  from  flies,  one  method  is  to 
use  a  wire  meat  safe,  or  cover  the  joints  with  a  long,  loose  gauze,  or  some  thin 
cloth,  and  hang  them  from  the  ceiling  of  a  dry  room.  Pepper  and  ginger  should 
be  sprinkled  on  the  parts  likely  to  be  attacked  by  flies,  but  should  be  washed  off 
before  the  joint  is  put  to  the  fire. 

Keeping  meat  in  winter  is  comparatively  easy,  and  it  should  always  be  hung 
till  in  good  condition;  it  is  better  to  do  this  at  home  than  trust  to  the  butcher. 

If  a  little  creosote  is  placed  on  a  small  plate  immediately  under  a  piece  of 
meat  as  it  hangs  in  the  safe  or  larder,  and  a  piece  of  cloth  is  spread  over  both 
meat  and  plate,  the  creosote  will  form  an  atmosphere  around  the  meat  which  will 
keep  it  for  3  or  4  days  longer  than  otherwise,  and  it  will  not  impart  the  slightest 
smell  or  taste  to  the  meat.     It  will  also  free  the  larder  from  flies. 

When  meat  is  hung  in  a  damp  place  a  ad  has  become  moldy,  and  what  is 
usually  termed  faint,  it  may  be  quite  restored  by  being  pared  where  necessary, 
and  washed  all  over  with  vinegar  and  water  before  cooking. 

Frozen  meat  should  be  thawed  out  gradually  by  soaking  it  in  cold  water;  a 
piece  weighing  10  lbs.  will  take  10  to  12  hours  to  thaw,  but  do  not  cook  it  until 
it  is  entirely  free  from  frost.  A  coating  of  ice  may  gather  on  the  outside,  but  it 
will  easily  cleave  off.    Do  not  let  meat  freeze,  however,  if  you  can  help  it. 

As  for  cold  meat,  it  may  be  said,  that  when  newly  cooked  meat  is  brought  from 
the  table,  and  is  to  be  put  aside  for  after  use,  it  should  be  put  on  a  clean,  dry  dish, 
placed  in  the  larder,  and  covered  with  a  wire  cover;  if  any  liquor  or  gravy  is  left 
about  it,  the  meat  is  apt  to  become  sour.  Cold  meat  is  always  best  when  it  has 
not  been  cut  while  warm,  as  then  the  juices  have  not  run  out,  and  remain  to  en- 
rich the  meat. 

Beef  may,  in  favorable  weather,  be  kept  from  4  to  10  days  to  advantage.  The 
kernel  in  the  round  of  beef,  and  the  marrow  from  the  bone  should  always  be 
taken  out  in  hot  weather  before  hanging,  as  it  taints  before  the  joint  itself. 
Those  living  in  the  north  will  find  it  economical  to  buy  a  hind  quarter  in  cold 
weather  and  have  it  cut  up;  that  which  is  not  needed  at  once  can  be  packed  with 
ice  or  snow  and  kept. 

Mutton  may,  in  favorable  weather,  hang  2  or  3  weeks  or  more,  as  it  will 
keep  longer  than  beef.  When  wrapped  in  a  cloth  wet  with  vinegar,  and  hung  in 
a  dry  cellar,  it  keeps  well  and  improves  in  tenderness.  Epicures  like  it  hung  for 
a  month,  and  it  will  keep  for  that  length  of  time  without  taint  if  the  weather  is 
favorable. 

Veal,  lamb  and  pork  ought  not  to  be  kept  more  than  1  day,  or  2  days  at  the 
most.  It  is  best  to  cook  it  soon  after  being  killed.  If  lamb  has  to  be  kept,  it 
should  be  partly  cooked,  as  that  is  the  most  certain  way  to  preserve  it  from 
taint. 

Game  and  Poultry. — Game  may  be  kept  more  easily  than  poultry,  but  it  re- 
quires care  and  constant  watching  to  bring  it  exactly  into  condition.  No  rules 
can  be  given  as  to  how  long  it  may  hang,  for  not  only  does  it  depend  on  the  kind 
of  game  and  the  weather,  but  still  more  on  the  taste  of  the  individuals  who  have 
to  eat  it.  Some  people  like  it  almost  putrid,  while  others  cannot  touch  it  if 
at  all  high;  but  it  may  be  said  that  when  the  feathers  come  out  very  easily,  it  is 
generally  considered  fit  for  cooking.  Old  game  will  hang  longer  than  young,  and 
afterwards  will  require  longer  cooking.     Game  is  frequently  hung  2  or  3  weeks. 

When  game  or  poultry  becomes  strong,  put  some  charcoal  in  water  and  soak 
the  bird  in  it  for  a  few  hours,  which  will  sweeten  a  bird  that  seems  spoiled. 
Some  people  put  soda  in  the  water,  but  the  charcoal  is  best.  Sportsmen  who 
wish  to  ship  game  can  send  it  a  long  distance  if  they  will  shut  out  the  air  by 
tying  a  string  tightly  about  the  neck,  and  then  stuff  some  charcoal  up  the  vent. 


m  KEEPING  PROVISION^. 

Turkeys  may  be  kept  a  fortnight.  Ducks  and  geeses  if  their  feathers  are  left 
on  and  they  are  hung  by  their  legs,  may  be  kept  in  a  good  larder  for  several 
days.  Great  care  mtlst  be  taken  in  picking  feathered  animals  which  have  been 
kept,  for  the  skin  will  be  easily  torn.  Even  delicate  birds  can  be  preserved  if 
they  are  drawn  as  soon  as  they  are  killed,  and  then  carefully  washed  in  a  solution 
of  soda  and  water;  afterwards  dry,  put  some  charcoal  inside,  and  after  spreading 
a  cloth  over  it,  hang  it  up  in  some  place  that  is  cool  and  dark.  All  water  birds 
should  be  eaten  as  fresh  as  possible,  as  their  flesh  is  oily,  and  soon  becomes  rank. 
Poultry  is  eaten  fresh,  and  does  not  require  hanging. 

Fish  of  nearly  all  kinds  is  best  when  cooked  as  fresh  as  possible.  If  it  has 
been  kept  on  ice,  it  should  be  cooked  at  once  on  taking  it  off,  as  it  will  get  soft 
soon  and  its  flavor  will  be  lost.  When  fish  is  plentiful  and  cheap,  and  a  larger 
quantity  is  purchased  than  is  immediately  wanted,  any  surplus  which  will  bear 
it,  should  be  potted  or  pickled  or  salted  and  hung  up;  or  it  may  be  fried  and  then 
used  for  stewing  the  next  day.  Fresh  water  fish,  having  frequently  a  muddy 
smell  and  taste,  should  be  well  cleaned  and  soaked  in  strong  salt  and  water.  If 
of  a  sufficient  size,  it  may  be  scalded  in  salt  and  water,  and  afterwards  dried  and 
dressed.  Cod-fish,  whiting  and  haddock  are  none  the  worse  for  being  a  little 
salted  and  kept  a  day;  if  the  weather  is  not  very  hot  they  will  be  good  for  2 
days.  In  cleaning  fish  it  is  best  to  use  as  little  water  as  is  consistent  with  clean- 
liness. 

Cold  fish  should  be  saved,  as  it  may  be  utilized  in  salads  and  prepared  in 
many  attractive  ways. 

Sprinkling  fish  with  sugar  will  keep  them  a  long  time,  and  it  does  not  injure 
their  flavor.  Keep  them  in  a  horizontal  position  to  enable  the  sugar  to  penetrate 
them  well.    Salmon  is  improved  by  this  treatment. 

To  preserve  fish  alive  for  transporting  from  place  to  place,  stop  up  their  mouths  with 
crumbs  of  bread  steeped  in  brandy,  and  pour  a  very  small  quantity  of  brandy  into  them; 
pack  them  in  clean  straw.  The  fish  will  become  quite  torpid,  and  in  this  state  may  be 
kept  10  or  12  days.    When  put  into  water,  they  will  gradually  recover  after  3  or  4  hours. 

To  keep  oysters,  put  them  in  a  tub  and  cover  them  with  salt  and  water;  leave 
in  12  hours,  then  take  them  out  and  let  stand  12  hours,  without  water.  If  left 
without  water  every  alternate  12  hours  they  will  keep  much  better  than  if  left  in 
it  constantly.  Never  use  the  same  water  for  them  twice.  Or  live  oysters  in  the 
shell  may  be  put  in  some  cool  place,  like  a  cellar,  and  sprinkled  with  salt  and 
cornmeal,  then  cover  with  a  thick,  wet  cloth.  Repeat  every  day,  and  keep  the 
cloth  wet.  They  will  keep  this  way  10  or  15  days.  When  taken  from  the  shell, 
keep  them  on  ice,  and  the  sooner  they  are  eaten,  the  better. 

VEGETABLES. 

A  great  deal  is  lost  in  most  households  because  this  class  of  provisions  are  not 
suitably  cared  for,  and  we  offer  suggestions  about  keeping  the  more  important 
articles.    We  elsewhere  give  the  best  methods  of  canning  and  preserving. 

All  vegetables,  when  cut,  may  be  kept  fresh  by  putting  the  stalks  into  water; 
servants  generally  insist  on  immersing  the  whole  vegetable,  which  favors  decom- 
position. All  withered  vegetables  should  have  the  stalks  freshly  cut,  and  the 
ends  should  then  be  put  in  a  bowl  of  water,  just  as  withered  flowers  would  be 
treated.  Through  the  stalks  they  suck  up  water  enough  to  fill  their  shrunken 
cells,  and  that  makes  them  green  and  stiff  once  more;  but  it  is  a  mistake  to  plunge 
the  entire  plant  under  water.  Of  course  this  does  not  apply  to  the  washing  of 
vegetables  and  salads,  which  cannot  be  too  complete. 

Potatoes  are  sometimes  laid  in  the  sun  to  dry,  but  if  left  too  long  in  the  sun, 
they  turn  yellow  or  green  and  strong,  and  are  then  unfit  for  food.  All  green 
vegetables  should,  as  a  rule,  be  used  as  soon  after  being  gathered  as  possible. 
They  begin  to  ferment  and  lose  their  wholesomeness  and  flavor  very  shortly  after 
being  taken  from  the  ground.  When  they  have  to  be  kept  for  a  day  or  two,  place 
them  in  a  perfectly  dry,  dark  and  cool  situation,  but  not  exposed  to  currents  of 
air.  Aim  to  keep  each  vegetable  separate  from  others  to  prevent  contamination 
of  flavor.    Do  not  wash  them  until  immediately  before  being  used. 

To  keep  vegetables  like  onions,  turnips,  etc.,  during  the  winter,  a  common 
method  is  to  bed  them  in  sand,  in  a  cool  place,  but  where  they  will  not  freeze;  or 
air  slacked  lime  may  be  used.    Another  method  is  the  following:    Sink  a  barrel 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS.  39 

(Or  a  box  or  cask,  which  will  be  better)  %  of  its  depth  into  the  ground  in  the 
cellar;  heap  dirt  around  the  part  projecting  out  of  the  ground,  with  a  slope  on  all 
sides;  in  this,  place  the  vegetables  you  desire  to  keep;  cover  the  top  with  a  water- 
tight cover,  and  when  winter  sets  in,  throw  hay,  straw  or  leaves  on  top.  If  the 
bottom  is  out  of  the  cask  or  barrel  it  will  be  better.  Cabbages,  celery  and  other 
vegetables  will  keep  in  this  way  as  fresh  as  when  taken  from  the  ground.  Celery 
should  stand  nearly  perpendicular,  celery  and  earth  alternating.  Freedom  from 
frost,  ease  of  access,  and  especially  freshness  and  freedom  from  rot  are  the  ad- 
vantages claimed.  Again,  Prof.  Maynard,  of  the  Mass.  Agricultural  College, 
writes:  "Many  cellars  are  often  so  warm  or  dry  that  such  vegetables  or  roots  as 
the  turnip,  beet,  parsnip,  etc.,  do  not  keep  well.  To  overcome  this  difficulty,  soil 
is  often  used  to  pack  the  roots  in,  but  the  best  way  to  preserve  them,  and  the  one 
requiring  the  least  labor,  is  as  follows:  In  the  coolest  and  darkest  corner  of  your 
cellar  spread  a  thin  layer  of  fine,  moist  leaves.  Those  of  the  apple  tree  are  as 
good  as  any.  If  there  is  a  little  admixture  of  snow  or  frost,  all  the  better.  Upon 
the  leaves  place  a  layer  of  roots  one  deep,  then  a  layer  of  leaves,  and  so  on  until 
the  pile  is  complete.  Very  large  quantities  should  not  be  stored  in  one  pile,  as  the 
leaves  would  be  liable  to  heat.  By  this  method  I  have  kept  the  common  English 
turnips  in  perfect  condition  until  May  1,  and  beets  until  August.  They  are  thus 
easily  preserved  and  not  as  difficult  to  get  at  as  when  stored  in  sand  or  soil." 

Roots  of  all  kinds  may  be  preserved  in  an  ice  house,  where  one  has  one,  till 
the  return  of  the  natural  crop.  Pill  the  cracks  in  the  ice  with  straw,  cover  the 
top  with  the  same  material,  and  on  it  place  baskets,  boxes,  casks,  etc.,  and  fill 
them  with  carrots,  turnips,  beet-roots,  potatoes,  etc.  They  will  thus  keep  fresh 
and  uninjured. 

Most  vegetables  keep  best  when  the  temperature  is  low,  but  they  should  not 
freeze.  Some  articles,  like  apples,  keep  best  in  quite  a  low  temperature,  and  may 
be  kept  for  months  after  freezing  solid,  if  not  moved  or  disturbed  while  frozen. 
Vegetables  which  are  frozen,  or  touched  with  frost,  should  be  thawed  out  gradu- 
ally, by  putting  them  in  cold  water  until  the  frost  is  all  out. 

Artichokes. — These  keep  well  bedded  in  sand  in  a  cool  place.  Another  plan  is 
to  take  as  many  as  you  intend  to  keep,  and  boil  them  barely  enough  to  be  able  to 
pull  off  all  the  leaves  and  choke;  lay  the  bottoms  on  a  tin  plate  and  put  them  in 
the  oven.  When  thoroughly  dry,  and  quite  hard,  put  them  in  a  paper  bag,  and 
hang  them  in  a  dry  place.  Before  using,  they  must  be  soaked  in  warm  water  3  to 
4  hours,  changing  the  water  very  often.  Let  the  last  water  be  boiling  hot;  the 
bottoms  will  then  be  very  tender,  and  eat  as  well  as  fresh  ones. 

Beans. — To  keep  these  fresh  for  winter,  procure  a  wide-mouthed  stone  jar, 
lay  on  the  bottom  of  it  some  freshly-pulled  French  beans,  and  over  them  put  a 
layer  of  salt;  fill  the  jar  up  in  this  way  with  alternate  layers  of  beans  and  salt. 
The  beans  need  not  all  be  put  in  at  the  same  time,  but  they  are  better  if  the  salt 
is  put  on  while  they  are  quite  fresh.  They  will  keep  good  all  winter.  On  using 
any,  steep  them  for  some  hours  in  fresh,  cold  water.  A  pinch  of  baking  soda  in 
the  water  in  which  they  are  boiled  gives  them  the  bright  green  color  they  have 
when  fresh.  Cover  the  top  layer  with  plenty  of  salt,  and  keep  the  cover  tied  on 
tightly  between  the  times  of  using  the  beans. 

Dried  string  beans  are  excellent  in  winter.  Cut  the  beans  up  in  the  usual 
lengths,  dry  them,  and  put  them  in  a  bag.  In  winter  soak  them,  and  cook  them 
in  the  usual  way. 

Cabbages. — Gather  them  before  the  severe  fall  frosts;  let  the  coarse,  outside 
leaves  remain  on  them.  Fix  a  strong  string  around  the  stalk,  and  suspend  them 
from  the  timbers  of  the  ceiling,  head  downwards.  The  cellar  should  be  cool  and 
dry.  This  will  preserve  them  to  a  certainty.  Another  good  method  is  to  cut  the 
cabbages  from  the  stump,  pack  them  close  in  a  barrel  or  box,  and  fill  up  all  va- 
cancies with  dry  chaff  or  bran,  and  keep  in  a  dry  cellar. 

Cauliflower.— This  may  be  kept  till  February  by  putting  it  in  a  cellar,  and 
covering  the  roots  and  stalks  with  earth.  Or  it  may  be  placed  in  a  trench  in 
the  garden,  roots  down,  and  covered  with  earth  close  up  to  the  head,  and 
then  covered  with  hay  or  straw  4  or  5  inches  thick,  placing  just  enough  earth 
on  the  straw  to  keep  it  in  position.  This  method  answers  in  the  latitude  of 
New  York,  but  in  colder  climates  a  thicker  covering  would  be  required. 

Celery. — This  may  be  kept  in  good  condition  all  winter  in  a  cool,  dry  cellar,  by 


40  KEEPING  PROVISIONS. 

having  it  set  in  earth.  When  a  small  quantity  only  is  wanted,  take  a  box,  stand 
the  celery  up  in  it,  and  place  a  little  earth  about  the  roots. 

Celery  may  be  kept  fresh  for  a  week  or  more  if  it  is  wrapped  up,  first  in  brown 
paper,  and  then  in  a  towel,  and  laid  in  a  cool,  dark  place.  Before  it  is  wanted  for 
the  table,  let  it  lie  in  cold  water  for  an  hour.    This  will  make  it  crisp. 

Cucumbers. — These  are  much  improved  by  being  placed  with  the  stalk  down- 
ward in  a  jug  of  cold  water  for  some  hours  before  being  used.  A  large  cucumber 
may  be  kept  in  this  way  and  used  in  portions  for  2  or  3  days.  They  may  be  kept 
for  a  longer  period  by  cutting  them  when  at  their  best,  and  packing  them  in  dry 
sand,  so  that  they  do  not  touch  each  other,  and  keeping  them  in  a  cool,  dry  place. 

Horseradish. — To  keep  this,  grate  it  in  the  season,  while  green,  put  in  bot- 
tles, fill  them  up  with  strong  vinegar,  cork  tightly  and  set  away  in  a  cool  place. 

Mushrooms.—  Pick  and  cut  off  the  stalks,  wipe  them  clean,  from  the  large 
ones  remove  the  brown  part,  peel  off  the  skin,  and  lay  them  on  paper  in  a  cool 
oven.  When  fully  dry,  put  them  into  paper  bags  and  keep  them  in  a  dry  place. 
When  required  for  use,  simmer  them  in  gravy,  and  they  will  swell  to  their 
original  size. 

Onions  tied  up  in  strings  in  the  old-fashioned  way  invariably  keep  much  bet- 
ter than  those  laid  on  shelves  or  on  the  floor.  They  should  be  kept  in  a  very  dry 
place.  Or,  put  them  in  boxes  or  barrels,  and  if  there  is  danger  of  their  freezing, 
cover  them  with  straw  or  chaff  at  the  approach  of  cold  weather.  Store  them  in 
the  coolest  part  of  the  cellar.  They  are  often  injured  by  being  kept  in  too  warm 
a  place.  Alternate  chilling  and  thawing  destroys  them,  but  if  put  where  they 
will  remain  frozen  all  winter,  no  considerable  injury  will  result  if  they  are 
thawed  out  slowly,  and  in  a  dark  place,  but  use  them  soon  afterwards. 

Parsnips. — Before  the  ground  freezes  in  the  fall,  enough  should  be  dug  to 
use  during  the  winter.  They  can  be  stored  in  barrels  or  boxes,  packed  in  dry 
sand  or  chaff.  Keep  them  in  a  dry  and  cold  place.  The  balance  can  be  left  in  the 
ground  where  they  grew  until  the  frost  is  out  in  the  spring,  when  they  should  be 
dug  and  used  before  they  begin  growth,  for  as  soon  as  that  takes  place  they  lose 
their  sweetness. 

Green  peas  may  be  kept  for  winter  use  as  follows:  Gather  the  peas  when 
plentiful,  shell  them,  and  then  wash  and  scald  them  in  hot  water.  When  thor- 
oughly drained  put  them  into  bottles  and  fill  up  each  one  with  strong  brine;  at  the 
top  of  the  bottle  pour  a  thin  layer  of  salad  oil.  Cork  and  seal  the  bottles,  which 
must  be  quite  full  and  kept  upright.  Or,  when  full  grown,  but  not  old,  pick  and 
shell  them,  and  spread  in  thin  layers  on  dishes  or  tins,  and  dry  them  very  grad- 
ually in  a  cool  oven  or  before  a  bright  fire,  stirring  them  frequently.  When  hard, 
let  them  cool,  pack  in  stone  jars,  cover  close,  and  keep  in  a  very  dry  place. 
When  used,  soak  them  in  cold  water  for  several  hours  before  boiling,  till  they 
look  plump;  they  are  excellent  for  soup. 

Potatoes. — These  should  not  be  exposed  to  the  sun  and  light  any  more  than 
is  necessary  to  dry  them,  after  digging  them  from  the  hill.  Every  10  minutes  of 
such  exposure,  especially  in  the  sun,  injures  their  edible  qualities.  Dig  them, 
and  when  dry,  put  them  immediately  in  a  dark  cellar.  Apples  and  potatoes 
should  not  be  kept  in  the  same  cellar  unless  necessary.  If  so,  the  apples  should 
be  kept  in  the  coolest  and  the  potatoes  in  the  warmest  part.  Grocers  who  ex- 
pose potatoes  for  sale  in  the  hot  sun  would  do  well  to  remember  what  is  said 
above. 

To  keep  potatoes,  intended  for  the  table,  from  sprouting,  take  boiling  water, 
pour  it  into  a  tub,  and  turn  in  as  many  potatoes  as  the  water  will  cover,  and 
leave  them  2  or  3  minutes;  the  hot  water  will  scald  the  eyes  and  prevent  their 
growing;  then  pour  off  all  the  water,  let  them  dry,  and  lay  them  on  boards  one 
deep,  or  put  them  in  sacks,  and  keep  them  in  a  cool,  dry  place,  and  you  will  have 
good  potatoes  the  year  round.  Do  not  leave  them  in  the  light,  as  that  affects 
them  unfavorably. 

Sweet  potatoes  need  a  warm,  dry  temperature.  They  keep  well  when  packed 
in  barrels  of  dry  sand  or  dry  leaves.  A  temperature  of  45°  to  50"  is  best  for 
them. 

Squashes  require  a  very  dry  place.  They  may  be  bedded  in  sand.  Keep 
them  cold,  but  do  not  let  them  freeze.  They  keep  well  on  shelves  in  a  dry,  warm 
closet;  grocers  often  keep  them  on  the  top  shelves  of  the  store. 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS.  41 

Pumpkins. — To  dry  pumpkins,  take  those  which  are  ripe,  pare,  cut  into  small 
pieces,  stew  soft,  mash,  and  strain  through  a  colander  as  if  for  making  pies. 
Spread  this  pulp  on  plates,  in  layers  not  quite  an  inch  thick;  dry  in  the  stove 
oven,  kept  at  so  low  a  temperature  as  not  to  scorch  it.  It  will  become  dry  and 
crisp  in  about  a  day.  Store  these  sheets  in  a  dry  place.  To  use,  soak  the  pieces 
over  night  in  a  little  milk,  and  they  will  return  to  a  nice  pulp  as  delicious  as  the 
fresh  pumpkin.  The  quick  drying  after  cooking  prevents  any  portion  from 
slightly  souring,  as  is  always  the  case  when  the  uncooked  pieces  are  dried. 

Tomatoes. — Keep  in  a  cool,  dry  place,  but  they  are  very  perishable  and  should 
be  cooked  or  canned  soon,  or  they  will  decay. 

Turnips. — These  should  be  kept  dry,  and  as  near  the  freezing  point  as  pos- 
sible, as  they  sprout  easily.  They  may  be  kept  during  the  winter  buried  in  the  earth. 

Herbs,  to  Dry. — Gather  them  on  a  very  dry  day,  and  just  before  they  begin  to 
flower.  If  gathered  when  damp  they  will  not  be  so  good.  Free  them  perfectly 
from  dust  and  dirt,  pick  off  all  bad  or  discolored  leaves,  divide  them  into  small 
bunches  with  their  roots  cut  off,  and  dry  them  quickly  in  a  hot  oven  or  before  the 
fire,  as  by  this  means  most  of  their  flavor  will  be  preserved.  The  quicker  they 
are  dried,  the  better,  but  be  careful  not  to  burn  them.  Tie  them  up  in  paper 
bags  and  keep  in  a  dry  place.  Or,  what  is  a  better  plan,  after  drying  as  above, 
pick  off  the  leaves,  pound  them,  pass  through  a  fine  sieve,  put  each  kind  in  a 
separate  bottle,  cork  tightly,  and  label  it  clearly.    Have  them  cold  when  bottled. 

Herbs. — These  are  a  most  valuable  addition  to  the  resources  of  the  cook,  and  the  more 
useful  ones  should  be  in  every  house.  If  judiciously  used  for  seasoning,  they  add  greatly 
to  the  flavor  and  attractiveness  of  food.  Much  of  the  charm  of  French  cookery  comes  from 
the  skilful  use  of  seasoning.  The  useful  herbs  can  be  bought  for  a  small  sum,  but  almost 
any  housekeeper  can  easily  raise  all  she  needs  with  very  little  trouble.  Either  ibasil,  mint, 
sweetmarjoram.parsley,  sage,  thyme  or  tarragon  can  be  raised  in  little  boxes  on  a  window 
sill,  or  In  the  yard  in  a  sunny  spot.  The  time  for  gathering  them  is  as  follows:  Mint,  pars- 
ley and  tarragon  in  June  and  July  ;marjoramand  thyme  in  July  and  August;  sage  and  basil 
in  August  and  September. 

Bay  Leaves  are  taken  from  a  species  of  laurel,  L.  Nobilis.  It  is  a  native  of  Asia  Minor, 
but  is  cultivated  as  a  handsome  evergreen.  The  leaves  are  large,  shining  and  lance-shaped, 
and  are  bitter,  aromatic  and  narcotic.  They  are  used  by  cooks  and  confectioners  because 
of  their  agreeable  flavor.  Most  of  the  dried  figs  imported  into  this  country  are  packed  in 
bay  leaves.  The  laurel  among  the  Greeks  was  sacred  to  Apollo,  and  from  it  the  garlands 
were  made  which  were  placed  on  the  heads  of  heroes  and  poets. 

Borage  is  a  useful  plant  that  does  best  in  a  light  soil,  as  a  very  rich  soil  makes  it  too 
luxuriant  and  affects  its  flavor.  The  young  leaves  can  be  made  into  a  salad,  or  they  can  be 
dried  for  winter,  and  used  to  flavor  soups  and  sauces. 

Cherry  Laurel  is  another  species  of  laurel  (the  Cerasus  Lauroserasus).  Its  leaves  are 
used  for  flavoring  blanc  mange,  puddings,  sauces,  etc.,  but  cooks  ought  to  understand  its 
true  character.  The  leaves  should  be  used  with  great  caution,  and  but  few  should  be  used 
at  a  time,  because  when  wet  with  water  they  yield  prussic  acid,  which  is  a  deadly  poison. 
This  same  acid  is  found  in  bitter  almonds  and  gives  them  their  flavor,  and  a  little  of  it  is 
also  found  in  peach  pits  and  apple  seeds. 

Sweet  Basil  is  a  tender  annual,  a  native  of  Asia.  The  leaves  and  leafy  tops  are  highly 
aromatic,  and  their  flavor  much  resembles  that  of  cloves.  It  is  much  used  in  French  cook- 
ery, but  not  much  by  English  or  American  cooks. 

The  Marjoram  is  a  plant  of  which  there  are  several  varieties.  The  sweet  or  knotted 
marjoram  is  the  one  usually  preferred  in  cookery.  It  is  a  native  of  Greece.  It  is  rather 
tender,  and  requires  a  light,  dry  soil,  but  if  taken  in  doors  and  'kept  in  a  warm,  sheltered 
place,  free  from  wet,  it  may  be  kept  through  the  winter.  The  leaves  have  an  agreeable  aro- 
matic flavor,  and  are  used  in  soups,  stuffings,  etc.  Medicinally  it  is  stimulant,  tonic,  and 
useful  for  nervousness. 

Mint. — There  are  many  varieties  of  mint,  and  the  species  are  distributed  nearly  all  over 
the  world.  The  green  mint  or  spearmint  is  the  variety  commonly  used  in  cookery,  the 
leaves  being  used  in  sauces  and  salads,  and  to  counteract  the  flatulent  properties  of  some 
young  vegetables.  Spearmint  is  highly  esteemed  as  an  anti-spasmodic  and  stomachic. 
Peppermint  is  another  variety,  possessing  the  same  properties,  but  is  much  stronger.  The 
oil  which  is  extracted  from  it  is  a  carminative  and  stimulant,  and  is  used  for  flavoring 
lozenges,  etc. 

The  Parsley  is  a  hardy  biennial  plant,  a  native  of  Eastern  Europe.  There  are  several 
varieties,  the  Hamburg  parsley  being  grown  for  the  sake  of  its  roots  which  are  eaten  like 
parsnips.  The  leaves  "of  parsley  are  nutritious  and  stimulating,  and  are  used  as  a  flavoring 
for  soups,  etc.,  as  well  as  for  garnishing  foods.  The  root  is  diuretic.  The  fruit  (seed)  is  a 
deadly  poison  to  many  birds,  especially  parrots. 

To  Keep  Parsley  for  the  winter,  use  that  which  is  freshly  gathered,  wash  it  free  from 
grit  and  dirt,  and  put  it  into  water  which  has  been  slightly  salted  and  well  skimmed,  and 
let  it  boil  2  or  3  minutes;  then  take  it  out,  drain,  and  lay  it  on  a  sieve  before  the  tire  and  dry 
as  quickly  as  possible;  store  away  in  bottles  in  a  dry  place.  When  wanted  for  use,  pour  a 
little  warm  water  over  it  and  let  it  stand  about  5  minutes. 

The  Sage  is  a  hardy  shrub,  a  native  of  the  south  of  Europe.  There  are  several  kinds  of 
it,  known  as  the  red,  the  green,  etc.  Its  principal  use  in  cookery  is  for  stuffing  and  sauces, 
the  red  being  most  agreeable  for  this  use,  and  the  green  next.  The  other  varieties  are  used 
for  medicinal  purposes. 


42  KEEPING  PROVISIONS. 

Savoby.— Of  this  there  are  2  kinds,  summer  and  winter.  Summer  savory  is  grown  for 
seed  and  used  both  green  and  dried;  winter  savory  can  he  grown  from  slips  and  cuttings. 
Both  kinds  are  highly  esteemed  in  cookery  for  their  powerful  aromatic  flavor. 

The  Tarragon  is  a  hardy  plant  which  grows  in  light,  dryish  soil.  Itis  a  native  of 
Siberia,  and  belongs  to  the  order  Composite?.  It  has  smooth  leaves,  of  a  bright  green  color, 
which  are  agreeable  to  the  taste,  and  are  used  principally  for  making  the  tarragon 
vinegar. 

The  Thyme  is  a  native  of  Spain  and  Italy,  and  was  well  known  to  the  Romans.  Its 
leaves  possess  an  agreeable  and  highly  aromatic  flavor,  and  are  used,  in  both  a  green  and 
dried  state,  to  season  soups,  sauces,  stuffings,  etc.,  but  a  little  of  it  goes  a  long  ways.  The 
Lemon  Thyme  is  a  variety  of  the  wild  thyme,  remarkable  for  its  smell,  which  closely  re- 
sembles the  rind  of  a  lemon. 

FRUIT. 

Fruit,  as  well  as  vegetables,  will  keep  very  fresh  if  you  can  manage  to  put 
the  stalks  in  water,  only  it  must  not  be  in  a  close  or  dark  place.  When  apples, 
oranges,  lemons,  pears,  etc.,  are  to  be  stored,  they  should  not  touch  each  other, 
and  they  should  be  protected  from  heat,  cold  and  damp,  as  much  as  possible — 
sunshine  is  not  desirable.  A  fruit  room  does  not  require  much  ventilation,  un- 
less it  can  be  given  without  altering  the  temperature.  Alternating  from  heat  to 
cold  produces  condensation  and  evaporation  of  moisture,  which  soon  causes 
decay,  and  it  should  therefore  be  avoided.  Fruit  should  always  feel  dry  to  the 
touch.  Fruit  rooms  which  are  above  ground  should  be  double-walled  and  ceiled, 
but  when  wholly  or  partially  sunk  in  the  ground  this  is  not  so  important,  if 
darup,is  otherwise  excluded. 

Many  fruits  are  best  kept  by  being  dried,  preserved,  or  canned;  those  subjects 
are  fully  discussed  elsewhere  in  this  work. 

Apples. — The  best  method  of  keeping  apples  is  to  carefully  wipe  each  one,  put 
them  on  shelves,  and  cover  them  with  white  paper;  keep  them  from  touching 
each  other;  to  do  this,  a  number  of  shallow  trays  may  be  provided,  and  supported 
on  racks  one  above  the  other.  The  larger  and  finer  kinds  can  be  each  wrapped 
in  paper.  Another  plan  is  to  pack  the  apples,  so  that  they  will  not  touch  each 
other,  in  dry  oats,  or  sawdust,  much  as  eggs  are  packed;  or  put  them  in  layers  of 
dry  sand.  Bruised  apples  soon  rot  and  infect  those  touching  them.  A  dry,  cold 
place  in  which  there  is  little  change  of  temperature  is  best.  A  piece  of  linen 
cloth  spread  over  apples  will  make  them  less  likely  to  freeze.  When  kept  stored 
in  barrels  do  not  unhead  them  until  they  are  wanted.  It  is  rarely  a  good  plan  to 
sort  over  the  apples  to  pick  out  the  rotten  ones;  leave  them  undisturbed  until 
used. 

Apricots. — These  are  difficult  to  keep  long.  If  gathered  before  they  are  quite 
ripe  on  the  shady  side,  and  laid  on  a  sieve  in  the  fruit  room  or  a  cool  cellar,  they 
will  keep  perfectly  for  a  few  days,  but  not  long. 

Cherries. — Fresh  cherries  will  not  keep  long;  they  can  be  dried — see  "Dried 
Fruit." 

Cranberries. — Keep  these  in  the  cellar,  under  water — say  in  a  barrel  or  keg — 
and  they  will  keep  through  the  winter.    Change  the  water  every  3  or  4  weeks. 

The  cranberry  is  nearly  related  to  the  bilberry.  The  species  are  all  natives  of  cold 
countries,  and  grow  well  on  swampy  land.  The  fruit  is  too  acid  to  be  eaten  raw,  but  has  a 
very  pleasant  taste  when  cooked.    The  juice  contains  malic  and  citric  acid. 

Filberts  come  from  a  cultivated  variety  of  Corylus  cupuliferce.  They  con- 
stitute a  rich  but  rather  indigestible  article  of  food.  The  best  way  to  keep  them 
is  to  pack  them  when  quite  ripe  and  dry,  in  earthen  jars,  and  place  a  thick  layer 
of  salt  over  them.  Tie  a  paper  over  them,  keep  in  a  cool  place  and  they  will  keep 
several  months.  If  they  get  to  look  dark  or  moldy,  place  them  in  a  colander, 
and  shake  gently  over  a  pan  of  red  hot  coals  on  which  a  little  powdered  sulphur 
has  been  sprinkled;  the  fumes  will  soon  remove  the  discoloration  and  make  them 
fit  for  the  table. 

Figs. — These,  if  not  gathered  from  the  tree  until  they  are  ripe  and  tender,  will 
keep  in  the  fruit  room  for  a  few  days  without  growing  moldy,  but  no  longer;  on 
ice  they  will  keep  2  or  3  weeks. 

Grapes. — To  keep  well,  they  must  not  be  too  ripe;  pick  off  any  imperfect 
grapes  from  the  bunches.  On  the  bottom  of  a  keg  put  a  layer  of  bran  that  has 
been  well  dried  in  an  oven  or  in  the  sun;  on  the  bran  put  a  layer  of  grapes,  with 
bran  between  the  bunches  so  that  they  may  not  be  in  contact;  proceed  in  the 
same  way  with  alternate  layers  of  grapes  and  bran  until  the  keg  is  full;  then 


Keeping  provisions.  43 

close  it  so  that  no  air  can  enter.  Another  plan  is  to  take  a  box  and  first  lay  a  pa- 
per, then  a  layer  of  grapes  (selecting  the  best  bunches,  removing  all  imperfect 
ones,  and  dipping  the  end  of  the  stems  in  sealing-wax);  then  put  in  another  paper, 
then  more  grapes,  and  so  on  until  the  box  is  full;  then  cover  all  with  several  folds 
of  paper  or  cloth;  nail  on  the  lid  and  set  in  a  cool  room  where  it  will  not  freeze. 
Use  small  boxes,  so  as  not  to  disturb  more  than  will  be  needed  in  a  few  days. 
Give  each  bunch  plenty  of  room  so  that  they  will  not  crowd,  and  do  not  use  news- 
papers. 

Lemons. — (1).  Wrap  each  one  in  common  tissue  paper,  and  lay  them  on  a 
shelf  so  that  they  do  not  touch  each  other.  The  shelf  should  be  in  a  dry,  dark 
cupboard,  free  from  draughts.  (2).  Lemons  will  keep  good  for  months  by  putting 
them  in  a  jug  of  butter-milk,  changing  the  butter-milk  about  every  3  weeks. 
When  the  lemons  are  required  for  use,  they  should  be  wiped  dry  with  a  cloth. 
(3).  Put  them  in  an  open  jar  or  basin  (not  a  tight  jug)  of  water,  which  should  be 
kept  cool  and  changed  2  or  3  times  a  week,  taking  care  not  to  bruise  the  lemons; 
but  in  this  way  they  gradually  lose  flavor.  (4).  Bed  them  in  layers,  in  jars,  in 
fine,  dry  sand,  having  the  sand  about  3  inches  deep  between  the  layers;  pack  them 
stalks  down,  and  do  not  let  them  touch  each  other.  Store  them  in  a  cool,  dry 
place,  and  they  will  keep  for  months.  (5).  They  may  also  be  kept  strung  to- 
gether, or  in  nets,  and  hung  in  a  dry,  airy  place,  but  they  must  not  touch  each 
other. 

Lemon  Juice. — It  is  convenient  sometimes  to  preserve  lemon  juice.  Proceed 
as  follows:  Squeeze  a  number  of  fine  lemons,  taking  care  that  they  are  all  quite 
fresh.  Strain  the  juice  through  muslin,  and  pour  it  into  small  bottles,  and  pour 
just  enough  olive  oil  on  top  to  cover  it  and  keep  out  the  air.  On  opening  for  use, 
dip  in  cotton  wool  first,  to  absorb  the  oil;  it  will  not  stay  good  long  after  being 
opened,  so  use  small  bottles.  Another  plan  is  to  put  %.  lb.  sugar  in  ^  pint 
lemon  juice;  stir  them  together  with  a  silver  spoon  till  the  sugar  is  quite  dis- 
solved. Pour  into  small  bottles,  cork  well,  and  tie  bladder  tightly  over  the 
corks. 

Lemon  belongs  to  the  citrus  family,  and  is  native  to  the  north  of  India.  It  was  unknown 
to  the  ancient  Greeks  and  Romans,  and  was  introduced  into  Europe  during  the  crusades. 
It  is  now  widely  naturalized.  Its  acidity  is  due  to  the  presence  of  citric  and  malic  acid  in 
the  juice.  It  also  contains  3  or  4  %  of  gum  and  sugar,  and  some  albumenous  matter,  and  a 
little  potash  and  phosphoric  acid.  Lemons  with  thick  rough  skins  are  almost  certain  to  be 
dry  and  spongy,  so  choose  those  with  thin,  smooth  skins.  Persons  of  bilious  and  sanguine 
temperaments  may  freely  drink  the  lemonade  made  from  its  juice,  but  those  with  irritable 
stomachs  should  avoid  it  on  account  of  its  acid  and  therefore  irritating  qualities. 

Melons  of  all  varieties  should  be  kept  in  a  cool,  dry  room.  They  should  be 
cut  when  just  ripe. 

Oranges  should  be  wrapped  in  soft  paper  and  laid  on  shelves  or  in  a  drawer. 
The  atmosphere  should  be  cool,  dry  and  even  in  temperature. 

Peaches  and  Nectarines. — Different  varieties  of  these  vary  in  their  keeping 
qualities.  On  a  cool,  airy  shelf  in  the  fruit  room  they  should  keep  2  weeks  or 
longer;  on  ice,  they  will  keep  4  to  6  weeks,  but  must  be  used  as  soon  as  they  are 
taken  out — almost  before  they  cool.  Nectarines  keep  rather  better  than  peaches. 
Wrap  each  one  in  paper,  and  keep  like  pears. 

Pears. — Wrap  each  one  in  paper  and  keep  it  separate  from  others.  Keep  in  a 
dry,  even,  cool  temperature.    They  do  not  keep  well. 

Persimmons. — These  may  be  kept  by  putting  a  layer  of  brown  sugar  in  an 
earthen  jar;  then  put  in  a  layer  of  persimmons,  then  sugar,  and  so  on  with  alter- 
nate layers;  have  sugar  on  top.     Keep  paper  closely  tied  over  the  top. 

Pineapples. — Take  ripe  pineapples,  pare  and  remove  the  eyes,  and  with  a  sil- 
ver fork  separate  the  pulp  from  the  core;  add  1  ft),  granulated  sugar  to  each  2 
cups  of  this  pulp  and  let  stand  until  the  sugar  dissolves,  stirring  occasionally; 
then  put  it  in  glass  fruit  jars,  screwing  on  the  covers  tightly. 

Plums  can  be  kept  several  weeks  if  wrapped  in  thin  paper  and  laid  singly  on 
wood.     Bruising  them  hastens  decay. 

Ripe  nuts  are  best  kept  in  a  cellar  in  a  covered  earthenware  crock.  Put 
them  in  when  freshly  gathered,  and  tie  stiff,  glazed,  brown  paper  over  the  cover. 
Walnuts  keep  well  packed  in  salt  in  crocks.  Walnuts  which  have  become  shriv- 
elled should  be  steeped  in  milk  and  water  for  a  few  hours — say  6  or  8;  this  will 
make  them  plump  and  fine,  and  cause  them  to  peel  easily. 


44  KEEPING  PROVISIONS. 

The  Walxtjt  is  a  native  of  Persia.  In  the  south  of  Europe  it  is  quite  largely  consumed, 
being  there  considered  wholesome  and  nutritious.  The  unripe  fruit,  when  the  shell  is  soft, 
makes  an  excellent  pickle.  By  boiling  them  in  syrup  a  delicate  sweetmeat  is  prepared. 
The  oil  pressed  from  the  nut  is  sometimes  used  as  a  substitute  for  olive  oil  on  the  continent, 
and  it  is  the  nut  oil  used  in  painting.  The  matter  left  after  the  oil  is  extracted  is  nutritious 
for  poultry  or  cattle.    Grated  walnuts  are  used  in  Spain  for  tarts  and  other  dishes. 

Berries  should  be  kept  uncovered,  and  in  a  cool,  shady  place.  They  will  not 
keep  long. 

GROCERIES;  ETC. 

The  following  suggestions  about  keeping  some  of  the  more  important  articles 
in  this  line  will  be  helpful  to  many. 

Butter  should  be  kept  in  a  cool  and  airy  place— but  entirely  excluded  from  the 
air  and  in  as  large  a  mass  as  possible.  When  ice  is  not  obtainable  in  hot  weather, 
get  some  saltpetre,  dissolve  it  in  cold  water,  and  put  a  pan  of  butter  in  it  almost 
as  far  as  the  top;  lay  a  clean  cloth  over  the  butter,  and  let  the  corners  rest  in  the 
saltpetre  water;  keep  the  jar  in  a  cool,  dark  place;  this  will  keep  it  fresh  and 
moist.  Another  plan,  which  will  keep  it  cool  and  hard  without  ice,  is  to  set  a 
plate  of  butter  under  a  crock,  and  cover  it  with  wet  cloths  as  directed  for  keep- 
ing milk  without  ice.  Butter  absorbs  odors  readily,  and  should  never  be  put  in 
any  place  which  is  tainted  with  odors,  from  vegetables  or  anything  else,  nor 
should  it  be  left  exposed  to  the  air  in  occupied  rooms.  It  becomes  rancid  when 
freely  exposed  to  the  air.    Leaving  butter  in  water  spoils  it. 

The  best  way  to  keep  butter  from  the  air  is  to  put  it  in  a  crock  or  tub,  and 
spread  common,  coarse-grained  salt  to  ~%  or  %  inch  in  depth  on  top;  then  coyer 
with  a  cloth  and  lay  anything  flat  on  top  of  the  crock.  The  salt  by  long  keeping 
will  turn  to  brine,  which  will  effectually  keep  out  the  air,  and  may  easily  be 
poured  off  at  any  time.  If  kept  in  a  cool,  sweet  room,  however,  good  butter  will 
keep  without  the  brine. 

To  keep  butter  sweet  when  it  seems  likely  to  taint,  take  some  lime  water  and 
put  the  vessel  containing  the  butter  into  it,  leaving  it  there  until  the  sweetness  is 
restored. 

Rancid  butter  may  be  sweetened  and  recovered  (1)  by  washing  and  kneading 
it  well,  first  in  new  milk,  and  afterwards  in  cold  spring  water;  butyric  acid,  on 
which  the  rancidity  depends,  being  freely  soluble  in  new  milk.  (2).  Beat  the 
butter  in  a  sufficient  quantity  of  water,  in  which  you  put  25  to  30  drops  of  lime 
chloride  to  each  pound  of  butter.  After  mixing  it  till  all  its  parts  are  in  contact 
with  the  water,  it  may  be  left  in  from  1  to  2  hours;  afterwards  withdraw,  and  re- 
peatedly wash  in  fresh  water.  (3).  Melt  the  butter  and  skim  as  for  clarifying; 
then  put  into  it  a  piece  of  bread  thoroughly  toasted  all  over.  In  1  or  2  minutes 
the  butter  will  loose  its  offensive  smell  and  taste.  (4).  Thoroughly  mix  1  tea- 
spoon of  soda  and  1  tablespoon  of  salt  for  each  pint  of  butter;  then  add  equal 
parts  of  butter  and  cold  water,  set  on  the  stove,  and  bring  all  to  a  boil;  then  re- 
move, let  cool,  and  take  off  the  butter;  it  will  be  sweet,  and  good  for  cooking. 
(5).  A  little  saltpetre  worked  into  butter  that  has  become  sour  or  rancid  will 
render  it  sweet. — Remember  that  great  care  must  be  taken  not  to  burn  butter 
when  trying  it  out. 

A  word  about  rancid  butter.— Nobody  eats  it  on  bread,  but  it  is  sometimes  used  in  cooking, 
although  it  really  ruins  every  dish  into  which  it  enters.    It  is  injurious,  and  many  cases  of  - 
dyspepsia  can  be  directly  traced  to  rancid  butter.    It  should  never  enter  into  the  composi- 
tion of  any  dish  which  appears  on  the  table.    Sweeten  it  or  throw  it  away. 

To  keep  butter  hard  in  hot  weather,  without  ice,  invert  a  large  crock  of  un- 
glazed  earthenware  over  the  dish  containing  the  butter,  (a  large,  clean  flower- 
pot will  answer);  the  porousness  of  the  earthenware  will  keep  the  butter  hard  if 
the  pot  is  wrapped  in  a  wet  cloth.  Another  way  is,  after  one  meal  is  served,  take 
a  silver-plated  butter-dish,  clean  and  wash  it,  then,  when  cool,  place  on  it  sufficient 
butter  for  the  next  meal;  set  the  dish  in  a  shady,  airy  place  (in  a  draft  if  possi- 
ble); wet  a  clean  towel  with  cold  water,  cover  the  dish  tightly  with  the  towel  and 
let  it  stand  until  the  next  meal;  the  evaporation  of  the  water  will  cool  the  butter. 

Milk  should  never  be  mixed;  that  is,  do  not  put  the  evening's  milk  in  with 
what  was  left  over  from  the  morning;  the  one  will  spoil  the  other.  Never  use  a 
milk  jug  or  can  that  is  not  perfectly  clean;  the  least  impurity  adhering  to  the 
bottom  or  sides  will  be  sufficient  to  taint  it.  In  hot  weather  if  much  milk  is  ta- 
ken in  the  morning,  scald  it  at  once  and  it  will  keep  good  through  the  day. 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS.  45 

Never  let  milk  stand  a  moment  in  the  sun,  and  do  not  let  it  stand  in  zinc,  copper 
or  brass  vessels,  nor  in  earthen  vessels  lined  with  lead  glazings,  as,  if  it  sours  at 
all,  the  lactic  acid  will  attack  these  metals  and  taint  the  milk.  Well  tinned,  the 
best  glazed,  or  wooden  vessels  are  unobjectionable  if  properly  cleaned.  There 
can  be  no  question  but  what  the  germs  of  contagious  diseases,  like  scarlet  fever, 
diptheria,  etc.,  have  been  transmitted  by  milk.  It  is  a  great  absorbent  of  gases, 
etc.,  and  should  never  be  left  uncovered  in  any  occupied  room,  like  a  sitting- 
room  or  bedroom. 

Adding  a  little  baking  soda  to  milk  will  keep  it  from  souring  rapidly.  In 
souring,  the  milk  sugar  turns  into  lactic  acid,  which  is  neutralized  by  the  alkaline 
soda.  The  flavor  of  the  soda  will  not  be  apparent  in  milk  soups,  etc.  A  little 
borax  added  to  milk  suppresses  the  incipient  lactic  fermentation  which  produces 
its  souring.  The  little  borax  thus  taken  with  the  milk  is  harmless,  and  it  will 
keep  the  milk  unaltered  for  some  time.  'Milk  is  so  ready  a  transmitter  of  dis- 
ease germs,  that  inhabitants  of  cities  and  towns  would  do  well  to  scald  all 
they  use.  Milk  can  also  be  canned  like  fruit;  take  glass  jars,  fill  them  with  milk, 
screw  the  lids  on,  and  put  them  in  a  steamer,  which  should  be  set  over  cold 
water;  then  slowly  heat  it,  and  for  about  an  hour  steam  the  jars;  finally  the  tops 
should  be  tightened  again,  and  the  milk  will  keep  for  many  days.  A  little  cal- 
cined magnesia  or  grated  horseradish  is  sometimes  added  to  milk  to  retard  its 
souring. 

Milk  slightly  turned  may  be  sweetened  and  rendered  fit  for  use  by  stirring  in 
a  little  soda;  the  alkali  neutralizes  the  lactic  acid.  Sour  milk  can  be  scalded, 
which  will  render  it  sweet  again;  the  curd  and  whey  will  separate,  and  in  bread, 
the  latter  is  better  than  shortening. 

As  salt  curdles  new  milk,  it  should  not  be  added  to  milk  preparations  until  the 
dish  is  prepared. 

To  keep  milk  without  ice  put  it  in  a  tin  pail,  cover  it  with  a  wire  gauze;  then 
spread  over  it  several  layers  of  coarse  cloth  or  carpet,  which  is  thoroughly  wet 
with  water,  and  set  it  in  a  cool,  shady  place,  where  there  is  a  draft  if  possible;  the 
evaporation  from  the  cloth  will  cool  and  keep  the  milk  as  well  as  most  refrigera- 
tors. 

Cream  may  be  kept  for  24  hours  if  scalded  without  sugar,  and  by  the  addition 
of  sugar  it  will  remain  good  twice  as  long,  if  kept  in  a  cool  place. 

Cheese. — When  uncut,  this  only  needs  to  be  kept  dry.  After  being  cut,  it 
should  be  wrapped  in  a  buttered  paper  and  hung  in  a  cloth  bag  in  the  air;  or 
wrap  it  in  a  linen  cloth,  and  keep  it  in  a  box,  in  a  cool,  dry  place.  Cheese,  as  well 
as  milk  and  butter,  will  absorb  strong  odors,  and  should  be  kept  away  from  them. 
Cheese  excluded  from  the  air  quickly  molds,  but  it  should  be  thoroughly  guarded 
from  flies  in  a  cheese  cloth  bag  of  2  thicknesses. 

The  mites  which  attack  cheese  are  so  small  that  they  appear  to  the  eye  like  animated 
dust.  The  best  way  to  treat  them  is  to  rub  them  off,  ana  then  brush  the  cheese  over  with 
hot  lard,  and  repeat  the  process  at  intervals.  Mites  are  not  to  be  confounded  with  skippers 
which  are  the  larvae  of  a  little  fly,  and  which  find  their  way  into  the  interior  of  cheese,  or 
into  parts  of  cheese,  after  being  cut. 

Eggs. — These,  unless  perfectly  fresh,  cannot  be  kept  under  any  circumstances. 
Store  them  in  a  cool  place,  and  avoid  changes  of  temperature.  In  packing  them, 
always  place  the  small  end  downwards.  Eight  parts  of  bran  to  1  of  quicklime, 
makes  an  elegant  packing  for  eggs  in  transportation.  There  are  various  methods 
of  keeping  eggs,  the  following  being  the  best:  (1).  Select  perfectly  fresh  eggs, 
and  put  a  dozen  or  more  at  a  time  into  a  willow  basket;  immerse  this  for  5  sec- 
onds in  boiling  water  containing  5  lbs.  common  brown  sugar  to  each  gallon  of 
water.  Immediately  after,  place  the  eggs  on  trays  to  dry.  The  scalding  water 
causes  the  formation  of  a  film  of  albumen  next  the  inner  surface  of  the  shell,  and 
the  sugar  closes  all  the  pores.  Pack,  when  cool,  small  end  down,  in  an  intimate 
mixture  of  1  part  finely  powdered  charcoal  to  2  parts  of  dry  bran.  Eggs  thus 
stored,  have  kept  perfectly  for  6  months.  (2).  Dip  each  egg  separately  in  melted 
parafine,  perfectly  covering  its  surface;  then  pack  as  before.  (3).  Use  2  pints 
of  fresh  slacked  lime  and  1  pint  of  common  salt  to  each  pail  of  water,  and  mix 
well.  Fill  a  barrel  half  full  of  this  liquid,  and  eggs  put  in  it  after  June,  will  keep 
for  weeks.  (4).  Pack  the  eggs,  not  too  close  together,  in  clean,  dry  sawdust  or 
bran;  they  will  keep  for  several  weeks.    (5).  Dip  the  eggs  into  a  solution  of 


46  KEEPING  PEOVISIONS. 

silicate  of  soda,  kept  liquid  by  adding  a  little  tepid  water;  then  dry  them,  pack 
down,  and  they  Will  keep  a  year  or  more.  (6).  Rub  the  eggs  with  linseed  oil, 
and  place  them,  small  end  down,  in  sand.    They  will  thus  keep  for  months. 

The  philosophy  of  keeping  eggs  consists  in  the  fact,  that  the  shells  are  porous,  and 
through  these  little  openings  or  pores,  the  water  inside  will  evaporate  and  air  enters  to 
take  its  place.  The  air  which  enters  thus,  carries  in  bacteria  which  cause  decomposition, 
and  hence,  coating  the  shell  with  anything  which  keeps  the  water  in  and  the  air  out,  pre- 
vents decay.  The  evaporation  of  the  water  and  entering  of  the  air  is  what  makes  a 
stale  egg  lighter  than  a  fresh  one. 

Dried  Eggs. — The  eggs  are  beaten  to  a  uniform  consistency,  and  spread  out  in 
thin  cakes  on  batter  plates;  this  dries  them  in  a  paste,  which  is  to  be  packed  in 
close  cans  and  sealed.  When  required  for  use,  the  paste  can  be  dissolved  in 
water  and  beaten  to  a  foam,  like  fresh  eggs.  It  is  said  that  eggs  can  be  pre- 
served for  years  in  this  way,  and  retain  their  flavor. 

Coffee. — This  should  be  kept  from  the  air  in  a  tightly  closing  canister.  Coffee, 
like  butter  and  milk,  will  absorb  exhalations  from  other  substances,  and  thus 
sometimes  acquires  a  bad  flavor.  This  should  be  borne  in  mind  in  storing  it.  It 
is  best  not  to  grind  it  until  just  before  it  is  used.  As  the  green  berry  improves 
with  age,  and  will  keep  2  or  3  years,  it  can  be  profitably  bought  by  the  sack. 

Tea. — Exposing  tea  to  the  air  destroys  its  flavor,  so  that  it  should  be  kept  in 
tightly  closing  glass  or  tin  canisters.  Tea  will  yield  nearly  twice  as  much 
strength  if  it  is  ground  or  crushed  just  before  pouring  on  the  hot  water,  but  the 
pulverized  tea  sold  in  the  market  is  apt  to  be  tea  sweepings  and  not  good. 

Flour  and  meal  of  all  kinds  should  be  kept  in  a  cool,  dry  place.  Flour  will 
absorb  odors,  so  that  it  should  not  be  stored  near  anything  odorous,  like  fish, 
onions,  etc.  Keep  the  temperature  even,  and  neither  extremely  cold  nor  warm. 
Properly  kept,  it  improves  with  age.  It  should  be  sifted  and  warmed  before  be- 
ing used  in  cold  weather.  Flour  and  meal  have  a  tendency  to  absorb  moisture, 
and  when  moist,  they  decompose  more  readily,  and  the  lower  forms  of  life  are 
apt  to  develop  in  them,  so  keep  them  dry  and  well  covered. 

Meal,  sago,  macaroni,  semolina,  and  similar  substances,  are  sometimes  at- 
tacked by  mites.  They  are  so  small  as  to  be  invisible  singly,  but  a  peculiar  fine 
powder  is  to  be  seen  at  the  top  of  the  farina;  there  is  also  a  smell  something  like 
honey  or  fermentation.  They  never  appear  in  a  dry  storeroom,  but  are  some- 
times brought  from  the  grocer's.  The  only  thing  to  be  done  when  they  appear  is 
to  burn  the  infected  stock,  and  heat  the  jar  almost  red  hot  before  using  it  again. 

Rice  and  all  seeds  should  be  kept  dry,  and  closely  covered  to  keep  them  from 
insects;  they  should  be  examined  occasionally  even  then. 

Jams  and  Preserves  should  be  kept  as  dry  as  possible.  If  the  outside  of  the 
jar  feels  damp  to  the  touch  at  any  time,  be  sure  the  place  is  a  bad  one.  At  once 
wipe  them  dry  with  a  cloth,  and  remove  to  drier  quarters. 

Honey. — To  keep  honey  after  it  is  strained  from  the  comb  or  wax,  simmer 
gently,  skim  off  the  whitish  foam  which  rises  to  the  surface,  and  it  will  become 
perfectly  clear.  Put  it  in  jars,  when  cool,  and  cover  tightly.  The  vessel  used  for  boil- 
ing should  be  earthen,  or  tin.  To  keep  honey  in  the  comb,  selectcombs  free  from 
pollen,  pack  them  edgeways  in  jars  or  cans,  and  pour  in  a  sufficient  quantity  of 
the  boiled  and  strained  honey  (as  above)  to  cover  the  combs;  then  cover  the  jars 
with  cloth  or  leather,  and  tie  tightly.  Twenty  years'  trial  proves  these  recipes 
to  be  good. 

Honey  heated  in  a  can  to  200°  F.,  and  then  closed  air  tight,  will  not  granulate 
again  while  thus  sealed  up. 

Maple  Sugar. — To  keep  this,  run  the  soft  sugar  into  jars,  and  cover  it  with 
that  which  is  just  right  to  cake;  it  will  retain  its  flavor  indefinitely. 

Lard,  to  keep  from  Molding. — Lard  made  from  intestinal  fat  will  not  keep  as 
well  as  leaf  lard.  It  should  be  soaked  2  or  3  days  in  salted  water,  changed  each 
day.  If  properly  tried,  kept  in  a  cool,  dry  place,  and  in  perfectly  clean  crocks,  it 
will  not  be  likely  to  mold.  See  the  recipe  for  keeping  it  sweet  in  the  article  on 
curing  meats.    Fresh  lard  may  be  kept  in  tin  vessels. 

Suet. — To  keep  suet,  choose  that  which  is  firmest  and  freest  from  skin  and  veins; 
remove  all  traces  of  these;  then  put  the  suet  in  the  saucepan,  at  some  distance 
from  the  fire,  and  let  it  melt  gradually;  when  melted,  strain  into  molds,  fold  in  a 
white  paper,  put  it  in  a  linen  bag,  and  keep  it  in  a  cool,  dry  place.    When  used, 


KEEPING  PROVISIONS.  47 

it  must  be  scraped,  and  it  will  make  an  excellent  pie  crust  with  or  without  butter. 
Thus  prepared,  it  will  keep  a  year. 

Beef  Suet  can  be  kept  through  the  year  for  cooking  purposes,  if  sliced  and 
packed  in  strong  brine,  like  any  salted  meat.  When  needed  for  cooking,  take  the 
needed  quantity,  rinse  off  the  brine,  and  try  it  out  in  the  frying-pan.  It  also 
supplies  the  place  of  lard  or  butter,  if  rendered  and  put  away  as  lard. 

Salt.—  The  principal  thing  in  keeping  salt,  is  to  keep  it  dry — store  it  in  the 
dryest*place  you  have.  If  it  becomes  damp  in  the  salt  cellars  or  elsewhere,  dry 
it  in  the  oven  and  crush  it  again.  Mixing  a  little  corn-starch  in  the  salt  used  in 
salt  cellars  or  for  the  table,  will  keep  it  from  becoming  lumpy;  use  about  1  part 
corn-starch  to  5  parts  salt. 

Spices. — These  are  best  kept  in  small,  tightly  closing  tin  boxes  or  glass  bottles, 
with  a  clear  label  on  each  one,  so  that  it  can  be  selected  without  loss  of  time. 

Soap. — If  hard  soap  is  bought  in  quantities,  and  kept  till  it  hardens  well,  it 
will  waste  less  in  using. 

Cider,  to  Keep  Sweet.— Vee  only  sound  apples.  Make  the  cider  when  the 
weather  is  almost  cold  enough  to  freeze  the  apples.  Expose  the  cider  during  freez- 
ing weather,  and  stir  it  till  the  whole  is  reduced  as  near  the  freezing  point  as  pos- 
sible without  freezing.  Then  barrel  it,  bung  up  tight,  and  place  in  a  cellar  kept 
nearly  down  to  the  freezing  point.  As  long  as  kept  cold  enough,  it  will  not  fer- 
ment, and  as  long  as  it  will  not  ferment,  it  will  not  sour. 

Vinegar,  if  bought  by  the  keg,  should  be  kept  in  the  cellar,  in  a  cool  place.  A 
little  can  be  kept  in  the  kitchen,  in  a  tightly  corked  jug,  from  which  to  fill 
cruets.  Keep  vinegar  in  wood,  glass  or  earthen  vessels,  as  its  acid  will  act  on 
brass,  copper  or  glazed  ware,  producing  a  poisonous  compound. 

Yeast. — Keep  this  stored  in  glass  or  wooden  vessels.  Ordinary  beer  yeast 
may  be  kept  fresh  and  fit  for  use  for  several  months,  by  placing  it  in  a  close  can- 
vass bag,  and  gently  squeezing  out  the  moisture  in  a  screw  press,  the  remaining 
matter  becomes  as  stiff  as  clay,  and  must  be  kept  in  close  vessels. 

Food  of  all  kinds  will  keep  longer  if  cooled  quickly;  the  quicker  it  cools,  the 
longer  it  keeps.  Remember  this  especially  of  bread  and  soups.  Before  putting 
bread  or  cake  into  a  jar  or  box,  see  that  they  are  fully  cooled,  otherwise  the  steam 
will  cause  them  to  mold.  Any  cooked  food  placed  in  a  cellar  or  refrigerator 
without  being  quite  cool,  will  absorb  a  disagreeable  odor  from  the  confined  air. 

Bread  or  cake  should  be  kept  in  a  closely  covered  stone  jar  or  tin  box.  If  you 
have  any  crusts  or  pieces  of  stale  bread,  do  not  throw  them  away,  but  dry  them 
in  a  moderate  oven;  they  can  then  be  rolled  on  a  board  into  crumbs,  and  they 
will  be  useful  for  anything  that  is  breaded,  like  cutlets,  croquettes,  etc.  Waste 
nothing.  Stale  cake  may  be  worked  into  puddings,  while  pieces  of  stale  bread 
can  also  be  utilized  for  puddings,  or  for  toast,  dressing  for  meat,  etc.  Save  all 
soups  and  gravy  also,  as  a  little  of  either  will  add  to  many  dishes. 

Exposing  to  Sunlight. — Never  let  milk,  cream,  butter,  salad  oil,  or  any  food 
containing  it,  stand  for  a  single  moment  where  the  rays  of  the  sun  will  strike  it. 
Oils  and  fats  of -every  kind  should  be  kept  from  the  air  and  sunlight  as  much  as 
possible.  The  chemical  action  of  the  sun's  rays,  and  even  long  exposure  to  light, 
gives  to  fats  a  sun-burned  taste,  which  is  expressed  by  an  old  provincialism  as 
"bingy."  You  can  easily  know  this  taste  by  leaving  a  bit  of  bread  and  butter 
lying  in  the  sun  for  a  while  and  then  eating  it,  or  rather  trying  to  eat  it,  for  you 
will  not  eat  much. 

When  food  comes  from  the  market,  it  should  receive  proper  care,  which  is  not 
always  given  it.  Meat,  milk,  cream,  butter,  and  in  fact  all  foods  attract  in  a 
greater  or  lesser  degree,  any  impure  odors  near  which  they  may  be  placed.  The 
whole  contents  of  a  large  refrigerator  may  be  ruined  by  a  bit  of  putrid  meat, 
which  has  been  overlooked  by  a  careless  servant.  The  refrigerator,  larder,  cellar, 
cupboards  and  closets  where  food  is  stored,  whether  cooked  or  uncooked,  should 
be  inspected  at  least  once  a  day  by  the  housekeeper,  to  see  that  nothing  is  spoil- 
ing. Unless  this  is  done,  especially  in  hot  weather,  there  is  danger  that  illness 
may  result  from  eating  fermenting  food,  or  that  more  food  material  may  be 
wasted  than  is  placed  on  the  table. 


SOUPS. 

Soups  are  of  2  kinds;  the  heavy,  rich  soups,  made  from  stock  prepared  the  day 
before,  with  the  addition  of  other  ingredients  on  the  day  of  serving,  and  plain 
soups,  made  entirely  in  the  morning  before  dinner.  These  latter  will,  no  doubt, 
be  more  useful  for  ordinary  occasions,  being  lighter,  and  quite  as  palatable,  and 
they  certainly  are  more  economical  than  those  made  from  stock. 

Soups  are  made  by  cooking  meat,  fish  or  vegetables  in  water  or  milk,  and  they 
are  seasoned  with  a  great  variety  of  flavorings.  If  you  see  a  recipe  calling  for  a 
great  variety  of  ingredients,  many  of  which  you  do  not  have,  you  will  find  that 
you  can  often  use  what  ingredients  you  do  have  and  still  produce  a  very  good 
soup,  as  many  of  the  ingredients  can  be  omitted  without  much  loss — it  will  sim- 
ply change  the  flavor  a  little.  Remember  that  the  2  important  points  with  soups 
are,  (1)  their  nutritive  properties,  which  are  extracted  from  the  meat,  fish,  etc., 
and  (2)  the  flavoring  derived  from  herbs,  spices,  etc.,  added  for  that  purpose. 
Some  soups  will  contain  a  good  deal  of  nourishment  and  others  very  little;  some 
will  be  very  highly  and  others  only  slightly  flavored.  The  names  are  often  given 
to  soups  because  of  their  principal  ingredients,  as  with  "Beef,"  "Chicken,"  "Po- 
tato," or  "Cabbage  Soup,"  and  sometimes  because  of  the  color,  as  "White,"  or 
"Brown  Soup,"  while  often  they  are  purely  fanciful.  By  bearing  these  things  in. 
mind,  and  by  studying  the  principles  involved  in  extracting  the  nutritive  proper- 
ties from  the  fish  or  meat,  the  inexperienced  cook  can  soon  master  the  mysteries 
of  soup  making,  and  she  can  then  easily  make  new  and  appetizing  combinations 
for  herself. 

The  Utensils  Used. — The  soup-kettle  or  stock-pot  for  an  ordinary  family 
should  hold  from  6  quarts  to  2  gallons.  The  allowance  of  soup  for  each  person  is 
a  half  pint,  and  unless  the  family  is  very  large,  a  kettle  of  this  size  will  give 
ample  room  for  a  full  supply,  after  straining  from  the  soup  material.  The  best 
kettle  is  of  iron,  and  if  kept  scrupulously  clean  (as  all  cooking  utensils  should  be) 
it  will  not  discolor  the  soup.  If  anything  should  happen  to  turn  color  in  it,  there 
is  nothing  harmful  in  the  oxide  of  iron,  whereas,  some  articles  of  food  are  made 
poisonous  if  prepared  in  brass,  copper,  or  lead,  especially  acid  foods,  if  allowed  to 
stand  in  those  vessels.  The  soup-kettle  should  be  high  in  proportion  to  the  size, 
as  only  a  small  surface  in  contact  with  the  hot  range  is  needed  for  the  long,  slow 
simmering  required  in  the  preparation  of  soups.  A  kettle  of  that  form  takes 
less  room  on  the  range,  and  when  dinner  is  being  prepared,  this  is  desirable. 
Other  utensils  used  in  the  preparation  of  soups  are,  1  coarse  and  1  fine  colan- 
der, and  2  or  3  squares  of  cheese-cloth  for  strainers.  The  cloth  strainer  should 
be  boiled  in  water  with  a  little  cooking-soda  added.  If  boiled  in  soap,  the  odor 
is  apt  to      remain  in  the  cloth,  giving  a  bad  flavor  to  the  soup. 

The  Principles  Involved  in  Soup  Making. — Many  cooks  have  little  idea  of 
the  principles  involved  in  extracting  the  nutritive  properties  from  meat,  but  they 
are  quite  simple,  and  are  as  follows:*  Fibre,  fat,  gelatine  and  albumen,  with  ex- 
tractives and  salts  (to  which  some  writers  give  the  name  of  osmazome)  are  the 
component  parts  of  all  meats.  Fibres  swell  up  and  soften  when  subjected  to  the 
action  of  hot  water.  They  are  a  valuable  and  nourishing  food,  but  are  insoluble 
in  water,  and  constitute  nearly  all  that  remains  of  meat  after  long  boiling.  Fat 
is  dissolved  by  boiling,  but  as  it  is  contained  in  cells  covered  by  a  very  fine  mem- 
brane which  never  dissolves,  a  portion  of  it  always  adheres  to  the  fibres.  The 
other  portion  rises  to  the  surface  of  the  stock,  and  is  that  which  has  escaped 

*Count  Rumford,  writing  of  his  experiments  in  feeding  the  Bavarian  army,  says:  "I  con- 
stantly found  that  the  richness  or  quality  of  a  soup  depended  more  upon  the  proper  choice 
of  the  ingredients,  and  the  proper  management  of  the  fire  in  the  combination  of  these  in- 
gredients, than  upon  the  quantity  of  solid  nutritious  matter  employed;  much  more  upon 
the  art  and  skill  of  the  cook  than  upon  the  sums  laid  out  in  the  market."  To  which  we  may 
add,  that  the  poor  suffer  more  from  the  common  lack  of  skill  than  the  rich,  because  they 
are  least  able  to  bear  the  losses  it  entails,  and  therefore  we  say,  that  to  no  class  is  a  clear 
understanding  of  the  principles  of  cookery  so  important  as  to  the  poor. 

(48) 


SOUPS.  49 

from  the  cells  which  were  not  whole,  or  which  have  burst  by  boiling.  Fat  is  also 
added  to  thickened  soups,  where  it  combines  with  starch  and  does  not  rise  to  the 
surface.  The  gelatine  is  soluble  in  water,  and  is  the  basis  and  nutritious  portion 
of  soup  stock.  It  is  the  gelatine  which,  when  enough  of  it  is  present,  causes  the 
stock  to  become  a  jelly  when  cold.  Albumen  surrounds  the  fibres  of  meat  in  its 
soluble  state,  just  as  it  exists  in  an  unboiled  egg.  It  is  soluble  in  cold  or  tepid 
water,  but  it  begins  to  coagulate  at  145°  and  sets  into  a  jelly  at  160°,  for  which 
reason  it  is  evident  that  if  the  water  is  boiling  when  the  meat  is  put  in,  or  if  it  is 
afterwards  made  to  boil  up  quickly,  the  albumen  will  harden,  and  will  form  a 
thin  case  or  shell  which  will  prevent  the  gelatine  and  osmazome  from  dissolving, 
and  the  result  will  be  a  thin  and  tasteless  stock.  Albumen  partially  dissolved, 
makes  the  cloudy  appearance  of  soup  and  jelly;  coagulated  albumen  is  removed 
as  scum.  Scum  is  wasted  food.  It  is  well  also  to  know  that  the  size  of  the  piece 
of  meat  affects  the  solubility  of  the  albumen,  as  the  part  farthest  from  the  sur- 
face will  receive  enough  heat  to  be  coagulated  before  it  is  dissolved,  if  the  piece 
is  large.  The  extractives  and  salts  (osmazome)  are  soluble  in  water,  cold  or  hot, 
and  they  give  flavor  and  perfume  to  the  stock.  The  flesh  of  old  animals  contains 
more  than  that  of  young  ones,  and  brown  meats  contain  more  than  white,  and 
make  the  stock  more  fragrant.  By  roasting  meat,  a  portion  is  converted  to  cara- 
mel; therefore,  by  putting  the  remains  of  roast  meats  in  the  stock-pot  a  better 
flavor  is  obtained.  Choose  lean  rather  than  fat  meat  for  soup.  See  also  what 
we  say  elsewhere  about  the  principles  involved  in  cooking  meats. 

Bones  are  composed  of  gelatine,  a  fatty  fluid  something  like  marrow,  and  an 
earthy  substance,  to  which  they  owe  their  solidity.  As  much  gelatine  is  con- 
tained in  2  oz.  of  bone  as  in  1  lb.  of  meat,  but  it  is  so  encased  in  the  earthy  sub- 
stance, that  usually  only  the  surface  is  dissolved  in  boiling  water;  when  the 
bones  are  broken,  there  are  more  surfaces,  and  more  gelatine  is  dissolved;  and  by 
reducing  the  bones  to  powder,  all  the  gelatine  can  be  obtained.  Long  continued 
boiling  is,  therefore,  needed  for  bones,  and  this  boiling  should  continue  until  the 
earthy  matter  is  full  of  little  empty  holes  where  the  gelatine  once  was,  and  until 
the  bone  has  lost  %  of  its  weight.  They  should  boil  for  days  rather  than  hours. 
The  best  way  to  break  bones  is  to  pound  them  roughly  in  an  iron  mortar,  adding 
a  little  water  from  time  to  time  to  prevent  their  heating.  It  is  a  great  saving  to 
thus  utilize  bones  which  are  often  entirely  wasted.  If  the  bones  are  finely 
broken,  tie  them  up  in  a  bag  and  put  them  in  the  stock-pot.  Now  gelatine  forms 
the  basis  of  stocks,  but  although  nourishing,  it  is  without  taste,  and  so  stock 
made  entirely  of  it,  is  not  liked.  Flavoring  and  salts  are  needed  to  make  it  palat- 
able, and  when  meat  and  vegetables  are  added  to  the  cooking  bones,  they  make 
the  stock  savory. 

Stock. — When  meat  is  used  solely  to  make  stock,  cut  it  into  very  small  pieces, 
put  it  into  cold  water,  and  heat  very  slowly;  but  if  you  wish  to  have  stock  and  a 
piece  of  savory  meat  also,  choose  the  freshest  meat,  and  have  it  cut  as  thick  as 
possible,  for  if  it  is  a  thin,  flat  piece,  it  will  not  look  well,  and  will  be  soon  spoiled 
by  the  boiling.  It  is  impossible,  however,  to  have  first  rate  stock  and  good 
boiled  meat  at  the  same  time,  as  the  two  objects  are  diametrically  opposed.  As 
it  extracts  all  the  juices  from  the  surface  .of  meat  to  wash  it,  that  should  never 
be  done  unless  it  is  absolutely  necessary.  Add  cold  water,  and  put  the  stock-pot 
on  a  gentle  fire  and  heat  it  gradually.  The  albumen  will  dissolve  if  it  is  never 
heated  above  160°,  and  will  not  coagulate,  but  as,  in  this  state,  it  is  much  lighter 
than  water,  it  will  rise  to  the  surface,  and  this  is  what  makes  the  scum.  The 
rising  of  the  hardened  albumen  {scum)  has  the  same  effect  ia  clarifying  stock  as 
the  white  of  eggs  (which  is  albumen)  and,  as  a  rule,  it  may  be  said,  that  the  more 
scum  there  is,  the  clearer  the  stock  will  be.  If  you  want  clear  stock,  remove  the 
scum  when  it  rises  thickly.  Do  not  let  the  stock  boil  fast,  because  then  part  of 
the  scum  will  be  dissolved  and  another  part  will  go  to  the  bottom,  thus  making 
it  difficult  to  obtain  a  clear  broth.  Always  try  to  have  the  fire  very  regular,  as 
then  it  will  not  be  necessary  to  add  cold  water  in  order  to  make  the  scum  rise; 
but  if  the  fire  is  too  warm  at  first,  that  will  be  necessary.  If  fried  onions  are 
added  to  stock,  tie  them  in  a  little  bag,  as  they  may  cloud  it  without  this  pre- 
caution.   Generally  speaking,  the  longer  the  stock  simmers,  the  better  it  will  be. 

Removing  Fat. — The  English  and  Americans  prefer  soup  with  the  fat  re- 
moved; it  has  become  the  fashion  and  the  fat  is  thought  to  look  badly,  but  the 


50  SOUPS. 

fat  is  food,  and  quite  nutritious.  The  common  people  on  the  continent  prefer 
their  soup  covered  with  "  eyes "  (fat).  The  scum  is  coagulated  albumen,  and  if 
clean  meat  was  used,  the  scum  will  not  be  objectionable;  it  is  certainly  nutritious, 
and  to  throw  it  away  is  clear  waste.  Its  presence  is  not  noticeable  in  thickened 
soups  as  it  is  mingled  in  and  hidden  by  the  other  ingredients.  Those  who  want 
clear  soups  can  easily  make  them,  but  it  is  well  to  understand  that  they  are  less 
nutritious  than  others.  In  invalid  cookery  the  fat  is  removed  because  the  stom- 
ach is  then  weak,  and  often  not  able  to  digest  it,  but  with  healthy  people  the  case 
is  different. 

When  it  is  desired  to  remove  the  fat,  soup  can  be  allowed  to  stand  until  next 
day,  and  it  can  then  all  be  taken  off;  then  the  soup  can  be  re-warmed.  Or  any 
surplus  fat  on  the  surface  of  soup  may  be  removed  (after  skimming  off  as  much 
as  possible  with  a  spoon)  by  drawing  a  piece  of  blotting  paper  across  it,  which 
will  remove  all  the  remaining  fat.  Or  to  take  fat  from  soups,  sauces,  etc.,  thor- 
oughly wet  a  cloth  with  cold  water  and  pour  the  stock  through  it;  the  coldness  of 
the  cloth  will  coagulate  the  fat,  which  will  collect  on  the  cloth;  repeat  if 
necessary;  if  any  particles  of  fat  remain,  take  them  off  with  blotting  paper  as 
above.  The  fat  on  the  cloth  can  be  removed  and  clarified,  and  is  good  for  many 
purposes. 

Flavorings. — Add  vegetables  used  for  flavoring  about  an  hour  before  serving 
soup;  that  is,  do  not  add  them  until  the  juices  are  nearly  extracted  from  the  meat. 
Wash  and  pare  the  vegetables,  cut  them  into  small  pieces,  and  keep  them  in  cold 
water  until  ready  to  add  them  to  the  soup.  They  can  be  first  blanched  or  par- 
boiled if  very  strong  or  old.  If  desired,  a  part  or  all  of  the  vegetables  can  be 
fried  before  being  added  to  the  soup;  potatoes,  cabbages  and  onions  are  better 
for  being  boiled  in  separate  water  before  being  added.  Parsley,  summer  savory 
and  celery  are  also  often  used;  onions  and  garlic  we  speak  of  in  the  next  paragraph. 
Some  cooks  occasionally  use  cloves,  bay  leaf,  nutmeg,  etc.  For  a  variety,  try  a 
little  caraway  seed  in  potato  soup.  Just  before  serving,  add  the  salt  and  pep- 
per. The  flavoring  of  all  food  is  an  important  matter  and  should  never  be 
slighted.  For  soups  and  stews  the  best  rule  is  to  combine  several  flavors  without 
having  any  one  of  them  too  prominent.  As  boiling  spoils  the  flavor  of  wines  and 
catsups,  if  they  are  added,  it  should  not  be  until  just  before  serving.  Unless  the 
soup  is  to  be  thick,  no  flavor  should  be  added,  but  if  some  of  the  meat  and  veg- 
etables used  are  fried,  it  will  improve  the  color  and  flavor,  but  be  sure  nothing, 
either  meat  or  vegetable,  is  added,  which  will  have  the  taste  of  being  smoked  or 
burnt.  Great  richness  may  often  be  given  to  soups  by  the  addition  of  fried  cu- 
cumbers. Cut  off  celery  leaves,  dry  them,  and  put  them  in  a  tin  can,  and  you 
will  have  them  the  year  round  to  flavor  soups. 

Using  Onions. — Onions,  garlic  and  shallot  are  disliked  by  so  many  people, 
that,  in  making  stock,  or  any  of  the  quickly  made  soups,  it  is  better  to  leave  them 
out;  but  as  many  people  consider  soup  flavorless  without  those  vegetables,  a  dish 
of  stewed  onions,  garlic  or  shallot  may  be  cooked  separately,  and  on  serving  it,  a 
little  can  be  added  to  the  soup  given  to  those  who  like  such  flavors.  In  this 
way,  soup  either  with  or  without  onions  can  be  served  from  the  same  tureen. 

Onion  juice  for  flavoring  can  easily  be  obtained  by  peeling  and  mincing  an 
onion  and  squeezing  it  in  an  ordinary  lemon  squeezer,  or  cheese-cloth.  The  odor 
can  be  removed  from  the  squeezer  and  knife  by  washing  them  in  cold  water. 

Garlic  is  an  eastern  plant  which  has  been  cultivated  from  ancient  times.  It  belongs  to 
the  same  family  as  the  onion,  and  possesses  its  properties  in  an  intensified  degree.  It  is 
much  used  in  Italian,  Spanish  and  French  cookery,  but  very  little  in  English  and  Ameri- 
can. It  is  very  wholesome,  assists  digestion,  and  acts  as  a  slight  stimulant  and  tonic.  It 
possesses  much  medicinal  value.  As  a  rule,  in  using  garlic,  it  is  not  chopped,  but  the  flavor 
is  imparted  simply  by  rubbing;  cut  a  little  off  of  the  end  of  the  garlic,  and  rub  it  on  the  dish 
in  which  the  food  to  be  flavored  is  placed. 

The  Chive  is  a  plant  belonging  to  the  same  family  as  the  leek  and  onion,  being  the 
smallest  of  the  onion  tribe.  Its  properties  are  similar  to  the  onion,  and  it  is  used  like  that 
in  soups,  sauces,  salads,  etc.,  but  it  is  much  stronger,  and  only  about  %  as  much  is 
needed. 

The  Leek  belongs  to  the  same  order  as  the  shallot,  garlic  and  onion.  It  possesses  simi- 
lar properties  to  the  onion.  It  is  very  succulent  and  wholesome,  but  to  prevent  tainting  the 
breath  it  should  be  well  boiled. 

The  Shallot,  Shalot  or  Eschalot,  is  a  native  of  Palestine.  It  was  introduced  into 
Europe  by  the  Crusaders.  It  belongs  to  the  same  family  as  the  common  onion,  but  its  fla- 
vor is  stronger,  and  it  does  not  leave  an  unpleasant  odor  behind.  The  leaves  of  the  plant 
may  be  cut  while  growing,  and  used  like  young  onions.    It  is  used  for  high  flavored  soups 


SOUPS.  51 

and  gravies,  and  is  excellent  when  pickled.  Shallot  vinegar  is  used  for  flavoring  soups, 
sauces,  hashes,  etc. 

Rocambole  is  often  called  Spanish  Shallot,  and  belongs  to  the  order  IMiacoe.  It  is  very 
similar  to  garlic,  but  is  milder. 

The  Sorrel  (Rumex  Acetosa)  is  a  hardy  perennial,  which  grows  in  Europe,  Asiatic  Russia 
and  North  America.  It  grows  in  any  good  garden  soil,  and  when  fully  grown,  the  leaves 
are  gathered  singly,  and  they  are  used  to  flavor  soups,  salads  and  sauces.  Sorrel  was 
known  to  the  Romans,  who  sometimes  stewed  it  with  mustard,  and  seasoned  it  with  a  lit- 
tle oil  and  vinegar.  It  is  not  much  used  in  English  and  American  cookery,  but  is  used  con- 
siderably in  France.  Its  acid,  which  is  very  pronounced,  is  a  combination  of  oxalic  acid 
with  potash,  called  by  chemists,  binoxalate  of  potash.
